Kind: captions Language: en Good day I'm Norman Wildberger we're on our way to examining and ultimately deflating the modern theory of sets before I do that I would like to give you a alternative point of view towards set theory and in fact more generally to other kinds of data structures in mathematics that's we're going to start doing today so I want to set up much more careful much more precise much more concrete approach to how we organize things in mathematics okay so set theory is an attempt to organize mathematical objects at least that's certainly one of its aims and this is parallel to attempts to organize physical or real-life objects to classify things to put things into containers in such a way that the structure is more accessible so there are also obvious connections to computer science all right so I remind you that the standard approach is that well the fundamental kind of object is that of a set and here's the standard way of thinking and writing down a set it's an object that you get by taking these curly brackets sometimes called braces and then you put a few elements in and you separate them by commas and we agree that this notation means that this set is unordered that is if we rearrange the order of these elements the set has not changed and we also agree that we're going to not allow repetitions so that each one of these elements can only appear once in the set all right in this video we're going to look at sets but in the context of a wider view of data structures more generally we're going to see that sets are just one of four different types of important types of data structures and we're also going to introduce some novelty into the laying out of this theory and in particular we're going to have to say goodbye to this notation we're going to let go of this notation for a set now those of you have been watching my channel for a while know that I'm not afraid of going off in different directions and introducing new notation and ideas if they're necessary and this is a situation where it is I think necessary to have another look at exactly how we write things down notation is very important in aligning with our understanding allowing us to move forward smoothly so here's a chart that lays out the four different possible kinds of data structures that we're going to be emphasizing today okay so there are actually four kinds of objects call sets ordered sets multi sets and lists and they're characterized by these two different properties the property of either being unordered or ordered and the other property of being either without repetition or with repetition so let's have a look at these four possibilities the one that we're mostly familiar with is that of a set which as we've said is an unordered object it doesn't matter if we permute the elements and it's also without repetition we do not allow things to be repeated now have a look at this new notation for a set okay still has the curly brackets or braces but now there are no longer commas separating the elements there are just spaces this suggests that the elements are in fact not in a particular order they are kind of freely # 158 - An introduction to the Tropical calculus ｜ Data Structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 158 [1_ZfvQ3o1Ac].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day I'm Norman Wildberger today we will have a little introduction to something called the tropical calculus which is an alternative way of thinking about the algebra of sets or multi sets it's a rather interesting relatively recent innovation which goes under several different names tropical calculus sometimes called the Max Plus algebra sometimes minimax algebra and it has its origins in control theory and the tropical refers to the fact that to some connection with Brazil now I'm not actually that confident about the the history I think probably a number of different people have discovered some of these ideas sort of independently but maybe some of you can inform us as to more details of what actually happened in terms of the development of this subject so the framework is we're thinking about a number system which for us might be the natural numbers the integers or the rational numbers and we're looking at this number system from the point of view of sort of the set theoretic or data theoretic point of view where Union or max and addition are the two main concepts we're going to be focusing on so we have two numbers x and y we can talk about their union which is just the maximum of those two numbers and we can talk about their sum in the usual fashion now these two operations so if we ignore the multiplication and the other operations for a second these two operations together generates some interesting kind of algebra and associated to them there's sort of some secondary operations so if we have Union we could also think perhaps in terms of the intersection or the minimum of two numbers and the sort of inverse operation to addition subtraction and we've talked about how we can sort of think about various properties these operations forming something which we rather informally call it an a realm now we could have a realm in the context of natural numbers or also of integers or also of rational numbers now what motivates the tropical calculus is one particular law that holds in this system which is this particular distributive law that M plus n Union P equals m plus n Union M plus P and I remind you that that means that if you take m and you add the maximum of N and P but that's the same as the maximum of M plus N or M plus P it's kind of obvious right that you take m and you take the bigger of these two and add it to M that's the same thing as looking at M plus N and M plus P those two sums individually and taking the maximum of those now this distributive law looks very much like a much more familiar distributive law M times n plus P equals M times n plus M times P that primary school students learn when they're learning about basic arithmetic so the parallel here is that the addition here is sort of playing the role of the multiplication in the usual setting and this Union operation is sort of playing the role of the addition in the usual setting as the parallel between these two types of distributive laws that really motivates the idea of the tropical calculus so we might say that the basic idea of the tropical calculus is that we're going to replace these operations of Union and addition with addition and multiplication respectively and then exploit our familiarity with the usual algebra involving these two operations so we're very familiar with algebra involving addition and multiplication and we've just seen that these two operations behave a lot like these two at least with respect to the distributive law so the idea is and we're working with these two operations in the realm context what we do is we just replace these two operations with these ones and sort of pretend that we're doing ordinary algebra or more precisely our familiarity with ordinary algebra allows us to relatively easily manipulate things here which then have also some meaning in this context so to set this up I'm going to introduce my own particular way of doing this which is a sort of an attempt to try to introduce this tropical calculus with a minimal amount of new notation so I'm going to use just ordinary addition and multiplication but to distinguish one of these new interpretations from the traditional one I'm going to put a capital T in brackets to denote that this is a tropical equation here so whenever we have an equation with a T either before or after it then we're going to interpret that equation in a tropical sense and that means that we have to define very precisely what the meaning of all the various tropical equations are so an equation with a T behind it has a new meaning that we are now going to establish step by step for a number of different kinds of different cases so our basic setup is we're going to start with rational numbers we could also in fact do this over the natural numbers or the integers but the rational numbers is probably the biggest domain that way we sort of include the other ones all at the same time so x y&amp;z are rational numbers so let us define X plus y equals Z tropicale okay that means that X Union Y equals Z so here's an ordinary equation with an ordinary meaning this is the usual Union that we already are familiar with so this is saying that the union of x and y is that or in other words at them maximum of X and why is that okay so when we have that connection between the three rational numbers x y&amp;z we're going to rewrite that this way using the symbol plus X plus y equals Z with a teeth all right so this plus sign in the context of a T really means Union similarly x y equals Z in the tropical context means that X plus y equals Z all right so we're adopting the usual convention that X Y means the same as x times y so here's the operation of multiplication but it's now a novel kind of multiplication it means that X plus y equals Z in the usual sense so as a kind of a consequence of that well as x times y equals n means X plus y equals n then y equals x to the N tropically is going to mean that Y actually equals X plus X plus X plus X and so on n times it's just multiplication and fold and since multiplication is addition the meaning of this is that y equals n times X in the usual sense that's ordinary multiplication so ordinary multiplication because there's no T there this is a T that means this has to be interpreted in this tropical sense so for example five plus seven equals seven as a tropical equation and that's because the union of five and seven is seven Union of course the same as max the max of five and seven is seven so this is the way we write that in the tropical sense five times seven equals twelve in the tropical sense because in the tropical sense this multiplication really means ordinary addition ordinary addition to five and seven is twelve all right so now we have some properties of these operations in this tropical context we have for example associative laws both for tropical addition and for tropical multiplication and there's just # 159 - Inclusion⧸Exclusion via multisets ｜ Data structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 159 [oWJIQdo1vpQ].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day I'm Norman Wildberger in today's lecture we're going to be looking at a very core formula in set theory usually called the principle of inclusion exclusion and it's telling us something about sizes of unions and relating that to sizes of sets and intersections the simplest example is when we have two sets a and B and the usual picture or Venn diagram that goes with it is something like this so here this circle represents the set a this circle represents the set B this area in between here in red is representing the intersection of the two sets and the collection of all of the green area represents the union okay and the principle of inclusion exclusion for these two sets a and B it's usually stated this way that the number of elements or the size of a union B is the sum of the size of a plus the size of B minus the size of the intersection a intersect B now this result generalizes in a natural way to more than two sets to in fact n sets and it's really a core result in a modern set theory so we're going to be looking at this result but definitely with new eyes in this video so we're going to learn first of all an extension of this theorem where the emphasis is not with sets but rather with multi sets and we're actually going to understand this theorem in a fundamentally new kind of way here is what the principle or theorem looks like for three sets a B and C whether Venn diagram looks something like this so the statement is that the size of a union B Union C in other words the size of the whole conglomeration where we put everything together is equal to the sum of the size of a plus the size of B plus the size of C and then we have to subtract the sizes of the three intersections the size of a intersect B which would be that area there the size of a intersect C which would be that area there and be just like C which would be that area there but then in addition we have to add the size of the common intersection a just like bead ii c which is represented by this little area here that's common to all three sets so it was kind of an alternating aspect we take the sum of these three and then we Kind: captions Language: en Good day I'm Norman Wildberger we've been talking about multisets and slight innovation called interval multi-sets last time and I want to show you how these kinds of ideas fit naturally into elementary number theory just talking about natural numbers and prime factorization so that's we're going to talk about today so we all know the fundamental theorem of arithmetic going back to Euclid absolutely core aspect of the way we think about numbers which is that every natural number greater than one can be written as a product of prime numbers in essentially a unique way that means that we can change the order of the primes but otherwise the factorization is unique so this is certainly a fundamental and foundational theorem the statement is perhaps not quite as pleasant as one might hope it to be because we have to talk about this factorization and the non uniqueness in terms of rearranging the ordering it takes a little bit of care to say that in the precise way we're going to be talking about that in today's video giving an alternate kind of way of thinking about how to State or frame the result but I should also tell you that unfortunately this theorem is not really true in general this is a much more serious issue and we are gonna have to talk a lot about this at some future time okay so let me just flag this that this is a theorem only kind of let me give you an alternate kind of way of stating what this theorem might be about let's say it this way that for any natural number n less than or equal to 10 to the 10 cubed there is an M set P belong to am set of n of prime numbers that the actual number n is the product over all the peas in this M set of P so this symbol pi this capital PI means product sort of analogous to the Sigma notation meaning some so it means we're taking a product over these kind of indices but this is an index over a multiset so we have to include each one of the elements in the multiset as many times as it actually appears in the multi set and this product of primes P in this M set P which we could say is P of n so let me illustrate this for example if n is 12 then multi set P is 2 2 3 so it's an M Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day I'm Norman Wildberger and welcome to 2016 in today's video we're going to be looking at some fundamental counting problems that are very essential in modern combinatorics and they're really associated with these four types of data structures that we've been talking about in the last few videos lists ordered sets multi sets and sets and each one of these can be thought of in terms of two numbers n and K so we talk about K lists from n or k ordered sets from n k multi sets from n and just K sets from n and in each of those cases we can ask how many of each of them are there it's question of counting so these are almost surely the four most basic counting problems in mathematics or at least certainly in combinatorics So today we're going to address those and there's some rather familiar answers but perhaps some unfamiliar ones too so our first counting problem is to understand how many lists there are which are K lists from n so while laws of arithmetic that parallel the usual laws of arithmetic for normal numbers so for example the addition is commutative a plus B equals B plus a it's also associative a plus B plus C equals a plus typically it's not nevertheless the associative law for multiplication does hold and this is a quite interesting and almost remarkable fact it's not entirely obvious at all that that rather complicated multiplication that we got by multiplying rows and columns and repeating that it has this property that if you take a B and then multiply that by see it's the same as multiplying a by B of an inverse B would be called the inverse of a it has to be a two-sided inverse so that it has the property that you can multiply it by a on either side and get this identity matrix this identity important Concept in linear algebra okay so to get at this notion of rank so I'm going to explain this my next video but to get at it we are going to be looking at Associated vexels so what we're going to do is we're going to look at rows of this maxle for example this third row right here see that third row 74 25 23 we're going to think about taking those entries and S of peeling them off to create a onedimensional version of a maxle namely a vexel right and the vexel is 74 2523 and we're going to say that this is the third row of the maxle m so the third row of maxle M is this vexel and correspondingly if we say take the second column that'll be C2 C2 of M it's the second column of them will be this vexel so here the vexel occurs as a row vexel and here the vexel occurs as a column vexel okay but uh basically this is the idea that we want to think about rows of these uh maxel but we want to think of those rows as really living in this one-dimensional kind of world of vexels rather than as special types of maxel in this two-dimensional situation because ultimately we want to compare this row with this row we want to talk about linear combinations of rows or of columns and to do that it's most convenient if we work in this really truly sort of one-dimensional story uh contained in the world of vexels okay so that's roughly where we're going to go in our next video where we're going to carry on uh talk about the rank of a matrix and the role vexels in understanding that very important notion I hope you'll join me for that I'm Norman Wildberger thanks for listening # 173 - A disruptive view of big number arithmetic ｜ Data structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 173 [B57eecnwMGY].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day I'm Norman Wildberger in this video we're gonna have a bit of a disruptive view of big number arithmetic sometimes there is nothing better to bring us down to earth and let us let go of these various dreamings having to do with infinite sets and overconfidence about things that are beyond the hill by just concentrating on what we actually can write down and the consequences of looking at the boundaries the farthest extents that we can find in our actual field of vision so it's commonly asserted that the 20th century mathematics was really the first to have understood the infinite that that previous eons of uncertainty about infinite this and if that were dispelled by the 20th century rigor and that the infinite took some kind of central stage in modern mathematics this is a very commonly expressed view I think it's a lot of bunk ok so this is not true ok it's a it's a fantasy it's wishful thinking it's a consequence of unclear definitions and dubious logic the reality is that 20th century mathematics really weakened itself dramatically by refusing to clarify clearly what was going on with so called infinite things it was just willing to accept these kinds of reasonings even if they could not be put on a solid foundation it was really a case of wishful thinking so no one understands the infinite and no one has even defined the infinite properly in the context that people usually like to think about it's a question of definition ultimately so the antidote as far as I'm concerned to these kind of wofully thinking that characterizes a lot of 20th century mathematics is a little bit of a healthy dose of big numbers looking at what we can actually do that's what we're gonna do today so we have already talked about extremely big numbers earlier in this video series back in math foundation 17 and at that time I introduced rather briefly these sort of higher multiplication operators star 1 which is multiplication star 2 which is exponentiation and star 3 which one might call some kind of triangular power operation that that video I think was maybe a little bit too advanced those concepts were maybe a little bit too advanced for that video today I want to have a more direct but more in-depth view at sort of bigger numbers without the level of abstraction that these higher or hyper operations necessarily in bulk so in our next video we are going to look at the idea of complexity and then we'll touch base again with this little bit more sophisticated approach to big numbers so let's start out rather naively and let's say let's try to create some big numbers so if we go back to our very first video when we talked about what natural numbers actually are at least some possibilities for them we saw that one sort of naive way of thinking about a natural number was just as a bunch of marks bunch of strokes on a page perhaps or little crosses on a piece of bark or something like that so that a primitive person maybe a hundred thousand years ago might register something like this as recording some quantity somewhere maybe a number of antelope at a waterhole and they might count something like this so saying well it's 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 thirdly so that's already a pretty big dumper if you're a primitive person about a hundred thousand years ago that would be a big number and it'd be sort of hard to fathom what was going on to that number you know the difference between taking that number and removing one or two it's hard to determine it ok now moving up several hundreds of thousands of years perhaps to the math foundation 6 where we get to the hindu-arabic system that we use for talking about numbers we can write down much bigger numbers with this hindu-arabic formation and we could read this as they started here see the sort of ones and thousands millions billions trillions quadrillions perhaps so 978 quadrillion 346 trillion 210 billion 16 million four hundred ninety nine thousand one hundred seventy five so that's quite a big number so this method of writing down numbers as hindu-arabic form is a lot more powerful than our primitive kind of form but it's a lot more sophisticated as well then going up one step we can employ exponents so we can write things like this is going a little bit beyond the traditional hindu-arabic form so this is ten to the hundred a number which is actually called a Google that name was introduced in the 1930s I believe by young boy he was a son of a mad dishin who math just asked him what should we call this number and the boy said aw let's call it on Google that name somehow stuck notice the different spelling from the usual google ok and here is what this number P looks like if we sort of rind out to give you a sense of how big it is there's one followed by a hundred zeros and you can see this is a lot bigger number than this one and in fact this way of writing things allows us to very quickly write a lot bigger numbers if we added another zero just there then I'd have to replace as a string of 100 zeros with a thousand zeros so a lot more work so just a little bit more effort there gives you a vastly bigger number so this is a step up in terms of moving up the ladder of getting bigger and bigger numbers to a Google so if we want to ascend further in this direction we can cook up bigger numbers by jacking up towers of exponents so for example there's our Google ten to the hundred that of course can also be written as ten to the ten squared it's the way we an exponent but 10 squared is read first and know starts to break down and continues to break down further and further the more we go this is very disconcerting and also sheds a lot of Doubt on these grandio claims that we are in possession of all natural numbers that's complete nonsense okay so what we've been talking about is complexity of a natural number it's a very powerful but relatively simple notion that captures how difficult it is to write down a given natural number where we have various arithmetical operations at our disposal so we had to look at uh the complexity which I called triangular complexity uh which is reasonably natural one and we saw that very roughly we had a picture something like this that if here is sort of the scale of natural numbers from one up to say 10 triangle 10 and my favorite Z = 10 triangle 10 + 23 is just adjacent there that the complexity of these numbers can be sort of visualized by a graph that looks very roughly something like this okay so this is a very distorted inaccurate not to # 184 - Reconsidering natural numbers and arithmetical expressions ｜ Data structures Math Foundations 184 [s25B-op0ifA].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In today's video, we're going to move towards reconsidering natural numbers, which are, of course, a very fundamental part of mathematics. It's actually how we started this series some years ago. But now, with our investigation of really big numbers and the really big arithmetic that goes with those big numbers, we've started to see that maybe there's room for reconsideration of what a natural number actually is. So, here are some questions that will motivate us: What exactly is a natural number? Can we say precisely, concretely, and logically what a natural number is? Should we distinguish between different types of natural numbers? Maybe there's some reason for separating the different kinds of things that we've been currently grouping together into one big box into separate boxes and looking at things a little bit more finely. Should we distinguish between natural numbers and arithmetic expressions? Arithmetic expressions can evaluate to numbers, but there's some reason to believe that we should treat them as something separate from the numbers that they evaluate to. In thinking about these questions, I think it's instructive to keep in mind the words of a very well-known American mathematician, Errett Bishop (1928-1983), who was a pioneer in what is sometimes called constructive mathematics. We're going to be talking about his work, especially when we get to calculus and analysis more. He was a very important thinker in the 20th century, one who realized that the status quo really needed to be moved in a different direction. But he gave a very lovely prescription here, which I think is well worth studying. He says, "Meaningful distinctions need to be preserved." Meaningful distinctions need to be preserved. He's talking about a situation where you're looking at something and there's different kinds of objects, and there's something fundamentally different about them. What you don't want to do is just lump them together, ignoring those fundamental differences that are actually there. You want to acknowledge those differences, those distinctions, and build them into your description of what's going on. Very sensible and a very useful kind of dictum. So far in this course, we have treated natural numbers as belonging to one type, a type called nat. This has included actually quite a few different kinds of objects. So we started out by thinking of a number as a rather primitive listing of marks or strokes on a page, perhaps going back to some kind of Neanderthal arithmetic. Then we moved to Hindu-Arabic numbers like 54,107, which, of course, is very different from this—it’s a different kind of object, really. So one key problem is that most of our theorems about numbers and their arithmetic start to break down and then fail as we move from small, primitive numbers through to Hindu-Arabic numbers and through to these mega-huge mathematical arithmetic expressions for numbers. Kind: captions Language: en Good day. I'm Norman Wildberger. Today, we are going to be looking at almost philosophical aspects of what mathematics is really about. It's a very interesting question, of course. It touches base with philosophical or scientific issues, and there are lots of different opinions that are possible. But I'm going to be sort of positioning things in a somewhat novel way, as you'll see. There's a really essential dichotomy or duality that manifests itself. So if you asked the ancient Greeks what mathematics was, they would have probably said something like arithmetic and geometry—at least when we're talking about pure mathematics. Applied mathematics, of course, also involves astronomy and other subjects. But pure mathematics really rests on arithmetic and geometry. Later on, algebra would also have been included in that general story. Now, which of these is actually fundamental has changed over the years. With the Cartesian Revolution, the emphasis shifted from geometry as being sort of fundamental to more arithmetic, and that's really what most mathematicians these days, I think, would lean towards—that arithmetic is really sort of the bottom of mathematics, really resting ultimately on the framework of the natural numbers, which we denote by "N" or "Nat." But in the 20th century, a new direction appeared when people were trying to create a framework for modern analysis. They realized that things were a little bit difficult, and they ended up bringing in philosophers and philosophical approaches, which ultimately resulted in creating set theory—this modern set theory—which ostensibly is some kind of framework for analysis. We are going to critique this very strongly in future videos, but that was sort of the motivation for this introduction of set theory as a foundational framework for mathematics. In that direction, people started asking, "Well, if we're going to use set theory to prop up analysis"—they didn't say it that way, of course, but that was really what was going on—"then how about other parts of mathematics? Can we build arithmetic from set theory?" And then they did come up with a scheme for doing this. So, by the end of the 20th century, we had this idea that first we start with set theory, and then we create arithmetic, geometry, algebra, analysis, and so on from that. Here is this way of constructing natural numbers from sets that goes back to John von Neumann, which we've talked about. So in this framework, everything is a set, and the starting point is the empty set, which turns out to be what we equate with the number zero. Then we create the number one by taking this empty set and taking a set containing the empty set, so it's really the set containing zero. The number two—well, one way of describing it is that it's the set consisting of both 0 and 1. So here is 0, and here is 1, and that combination is the number 2. Then the number 3 is the set consisting of 0, 1, and 2. So there's 0, there's 1, and there's the 2 that we've just created. So we get this inductive way of creating natural numbers using these ever-bigger zoos of forests of sets within sets within sets. If we ask something like, "What is 78,143 explicitly in the system?" Well, then we have a very big headache. If we had to actually stare at that thing and write it down, or even just stare at it in terms of these brackets, it would be a very big headache. But aside from being rather unwieldy and, I think to most people, unnatural, this set-theoretical approach to natural numbers also has some theoretical challenges. In particular, how are we going to define the operations of addition and multiplication on natural numbers when we view them in this very complicated set-theoretical fashion? And how are we going to prove the laws of arithmetic from those definitions? Now, it is supposed by most practicing mathematicians that this has been done somewhere, somewhere over the hill. Some logicians have done this, and we don't have to worry about it—that the laws of arithmetic are just God-given to us by the logicians. It's an unhappy state of affairs. So it means that modern students aren't actually exposed to seeing the arguments in front of them. We're not talking about something that's very tangential or oblique, right? This is a core issue in mathematics: how are we going to set up arithmetic with natural numbers? If we're going to do it in such a convoluted way that we can only sort of point to these indirect references to things that are somewhere else, it's a very unhappy state of affairs. The 20th-century thinking has been very disappointing, really. There's this whole sequence of wrong thinkings that have been introduced in this general direction. We have this idea that math is based on sets, that everything is a set. The definition of a set itself is finessed by the use of axioms. We really ought to define what a set is properly if we're going to base everything on sets, but that's too hard, and so we just assume that we know what a set is and invoke axioms to conjure up the things that really ought to be theorems. The foundations of arithmetic then become subservient to unwieldy and overly complicated set-theoretic arguments. The naturalness of arithmetic is lost. We're giving up our arithmetic to the set theorists, and in fact, no one really uses the proofs critically. When I say "no one," I mean practicing mathematicians, students at universities, people who actually use the mathematics. To them, these proofs are essentially non-existent; they're invisible. And I think it's a very unhappy state of affairs. We're talking about arithmetic, after all, right? We ought to be able to lay out arithmetic in a more sensible and more publicly verifiable way than this. What do we see? Well, we see on this side nothing; nothing. On this side, something. There's a difference—the difference between nothing and something. I claim that this simple-minded notice is really the central core dichotomy or duality underlying mathematics—the distinction between nothing and something. So this mark or stroke that we're representing here, this basic symbol that we're using, is the ingredient for our natural numbers. If we did that, we would go in philosophical circles; we could do that for years and years, talking about what does nothing mean, what does something mean. Okay, I just want to base things on our intuitive understanding of those terms. We all have an intuitive understanding of those terms and represent the something by this symbol. There are other possible symbols that we could have used and that we could use. So we've used this one here, but maybe somebody else might be more comfortable with a small circle, or a star, or maybe a slanted line, or maybe even a dot. Those would be alternative, completely valid ways of representing something. We could, of course, have other symbols as well, but the point is we're just choosing one particular symbol, and that symbol represents this primordial idea of something. And nothing—well, there's no choice about how to represent that. There may be a lot of choices about how to represent something, but there's no choice about how to represent nothing. It's always represented in the same way—by emptiness. You leave the page empty; you don't put anything on it. That is nothing, in our view. That's how we're representing nothing on the page; it's just empty. So although we've given a general definition of natural numbers as M-sets of marks, we still want to construct them, okay? We want to create them, put them on a page, and demonstrate what it is that we're talking about explicitly. And we're going to do this inductively, which means one step at a time. I'm going to start with our empty M-set, which is the simplest possible situation. It's the empty M-set, which has nothing inside it; it contains nothing. And we're going to give it a name. We're going to call it zero because that's the traditional word that we use for that particular number, and we'll often write it as a separate symbol with this sort of "o"-type number symbol. So zero is, by definition for us, this M-set here. So, in fact, this symbol here is extraneous; we don't actually really need it. It's logically unnecessary; we could use just this thing here instead of zero everywhere. But we prefer to connect with ordinary experience and just then think about the usual zero as being actually, officially, this empty M-set. So for us, this is the first natural number; it's the first one that we actually explicitly write down. Our first natural number is zero. And now we can construct more natural numbers, and the idea is always the same: we're going to take what we have, and we're going to add something to it; we're going to insert something. So the next one is this one here—the set or M-set consisting of something. It's an M-set with a single mark in it. So make a mark, you put it in the M-set, and that we can say is the number one. And then we can take this one and insert another mark beside it, so we have mark, mark. That M-set is two. Mark, mark, mark—that's the M-set three. Mark, mark, mark, mark—that's the M-set four. Mark, mark, mark, mark, mark—that's the M-set five. Notice, first of all, how much simpler this construction of natural numbers is than the set-theoretical one. Okay, notice that it's actually a natural progression that actually mirrors ordinary usage. Primitive peoples would have written number five in very much this way—a bunch of five marks on a page, a piece of papyrus, or something. This is very natural. The only thing that's a little bit sophisticated, that they wouldn't have done, is putting everything in a pair of brackets to enclose it, to make the boundary very clear that the natural number doesn't extend out here or out here. So these natural numbers are so constructed in order. We see that it's not just that we have a whole bunch of natural numbers; rather, we start with zero, and then we start to sequentially construct more and more. There's a natural order: after zero, then we have this one, and then we have this one, and then we have this one, and then we have this one, and then we have this one, and so on. And we can keep going, at least until our patience or our resources run out. So this is very explicit, very concrete, and now there's no more question about whether we have a natural number or not. So from this point of view, you have a natural number precisely when you can actually write down a multiset of marks. If you can do that, then that's a natural number. And if you can't do it, well, then you don't have such a natural number. It's a very explicit way of pinning down arithmetic to something concrete and, in fact, very close to our experience. So this is going to, I think, be a good foundation for arithmetic. There's a very good foundation for everything to take. But notice that it's exploiting our understanding of multisets. If we weren't comfortable or familiar with multisets, we might not think to do this. If we were only focused on sets, then we're not able to do this. This is something that we can do because we do understand multisets—a big power that we've gotten from understanding our data structures and not just sticking to 20th-century set theory. Okay, so one key ingredient in this discussion is the role of nothing, okay? Identifying nothing as a kind of ingredient in this story. And I think it's worthwhile for us to explore this a little bit more and to discuss other ways in which this "nothing" concept is going to figure in the mathematics that we're slowly going to create here. We've already seen some examples of it, but we need to readjust our thinking a little bit because the way we think about nothing currently in mathematics is subtly, though quite distinctively, different from what I'm advocating here. So we have to get on top of nothing. We'll do that in our next video. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. 

---

# 186 - The curious role of ＂nothing＂ in mathematics ｜ Data Structures Math Foundations 186 [EbSJwDphAb8].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day. I'm Norman Wildberger. Today we want to have a look at the role of the term "nothing," the concept of nothing, in mathematics, which historically has been very interesting. It's a kind of nebulous concept, of course, that a lot of people have had different points of view towards, and what I'd like to do is introduce a really dramatically new way—an additional new way—of thinking about nothing and its role in mathematics. So, in preparation for a discussion of that, let's have a look at some of the history. A good place to go back to is the ancient Babylonians. Babylonians were sort of the geniuses of the ancient world long before the Greeks came on the scene. They had a very highly developed, original, and powerful mathematics. So around 3,000 BC, their culture developed in the Mesopotamian area, between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, and they had a sexagesimal system, which means base 60—very interesting—and they also used a floating-point positional system very much like the one we do. So they used basically only two symbols: there was a symbol for one and a symbol for ten, which they wrote with this sort of wedge-like implement that they used to write on clay tablets. To express 47, they would make four of these tens and seven of these ones, and they would arrange them in something like this, and that would be 47. So they used these to represent the numbers from 1 to 59—they are the corresponding digits for the Babylonians, just as the numbers 1 to 9 are for us. Well, actually, we use zero as well. So then, otherwise, what they did is they had a base 60 representation so that they would clump their numbers in various places linearly. So this representation here—there'd be a 47 visible there, and here you can see a 21, and here there's some kind of space, and over here there's a 2. So this would represent, for them, well, 47, 21 times 60, so this would be the 60s column and 0 60 squares and 2 60 cubes. So altogether, 2 times 60 cubed plus 21 times 60 (we'll ignore this one) plus 47 for a total of what we would call 27,037. Now, an interesting aspect of their arithmetic was that they didn't really have a very clear or concrete representation of the decimal point, so there was some kind of ambiguity in their system. On another occasion, they could have represented this as being 21 ones, 0 60s, 2 60 squares, and 47 over 60. This gave them a flexibility that was actually quite useful. So what's interesting for us is their use of the space to represent zeros. They had this very clear idea—they were going to work with powers of 60, and if they were missing a power of 60, they'd just leave a space. They weren't going to put anything there. The reader should be able to see that there's a space in here and sort of register that they're missing the 60 squares, but they have 60s and 60 cubes. So this is going back quite a long time, and so this really is a use of the decimal kind of system, very much like the Hindu-Arabic one that we use, with the notable exception that they're working in base 60 and that they don't have an explicit form for a placeholder, but they're using the space pretty much exactly in the corresponding way. So they can certainly deal with numbers pretty much the same way that we can. Now, the Babylonian culture lasted a long time—we're talking about a several thousand-year span—and in the later part of this time frame, around 300 BC, there was a great flowering of astronomy and work in that direction. And part of that story was a very interesting development: the Babylonians started to use an explicit symbol to represent this placeholder that previously they just had a space for. So this is actually the first really recorded use of a symbol for zero, which predates the Hindu development, actually. So here's what they did: they employed basically a similar kind of symbol to their number two—just these two sort of wedges coming down—but they're now at an angle and they're kind of a little bit above the regular reading of things. So they could see that as we're reading this here, this symbol here represents not an explicit number two but rather a placeholder for the zero. So they could read this as 2 times 60 squared plus 0 times 60 plus 24, which would be our 7,224. Now, this symbol here really is only being used as a placeholder, okay? It's to make more explicit, more unambiguous, the fact that there is nothing in this place, particularly right here. It's not the case that this symbol was used as a separate number—that was the development that the Hindus get credit for, okay? So they did not have this idea that there's a new number, which is these two slanted symbols. Nevertheless, it's really the first use of the term zero, and the astronomers actually did employ this notation. However, it was never really equated with nothingness. We didn't have this idea that there's nothing and that nothing is actually represented by this particular symbol explicitly. Now, many histories of mathematics concentrate on the Mediterranean area and the Asian developments as well. Of course, there were also very important cultures—in fact, cultures—in the Americas: the Mayans, Aztecs, and Incas. And the Mayan culture is particularly interesting because they had a very interesting arithmetic and quite a sophisticated calendar reckoning and corresponding astronomy. So in Central America, the Mayan culture—well, it had been around from at least 2000 BC, but there was a classic period from 250 AD to 900 AD when things really developed in a rather sophisticated way. And we know that they had a so-called vigesimal system, which is base 20, and it was actually relatively efficiently written down with only three symbols—more or less a symbol for zero, which was this sort of odd kind of eye-shaped thing (and in fact, there were quite a lot of variants on the way of writing that zero), and then there was a dot which represented one, and a horizontal bar which represented five. So they would count zero, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, like this, six like this, up to 19. And then after 19, well, then they had a base 20 system. So these were sort of the basic digits that they would use, and then they would have these things stacked up in a vertical system, not a horizontal one. And they were very interested in astronomy and calendars, and that explains a little bit that their system for stacking things up was not quite what you would expect in a base-20 system. There was an important twist involved. So here, for example, is a Maya number. So we see these symbols stacked one on top of each other. We recognize this one here as the symbol for 11—it has two fives and the one. And then there's a zero, and then there's a two, and then there's a three. So what do these various things represent? Well, the bottom one was 11, or 11 ones, and here we have 02s, but the next level does not correspond, as we might imagine, to 20 squared. This next level actually represents how many 360s we have, so this would mean that there are 2 times 360, and the next level represented 20 times 360. So after this, we keep multiplying by 20, but we have this basic 360. So this means that we have 3 times 20 times 360s, so I think that's a total of 22,361. So there's certainly an unevenness to these levels—we have ones, and then 20s, and then 360s, and then 20 times 360s, 20 squared times 360s, and so on. And the reason for that is because they were very interested in the calendar—they had a very good understanding of astronomy and the year especially. So they knew there were 365 days in a year, and so 360 was kind of the closest to that. So they had a splitting up of the 360 into 18 periods of 20, so it was 18 times 20, and then there were five left over. And it was because of that 360 that appeared in their calendar that they had a number system that kind of reflected that aspect of things. So their numbering, or their numerology, I think, was very closely connected somehow with the year, days, and time. So as an idea of their accuracy, they knew that the solar year actually was a little bit more than 365 days, and effectively they knew that, in our language, it was 365.2420, while our current understanding is that it's 365.242198. So this is pretty impressive, kind of accuracy, in their understanding. So the Mayans did have a symbol for zero, and they used that in their positional representation of numbers, not so much different from the Babylonians. Now, what about the ancient Greeks, who were, of course, sort of the powerhouse of ancient mathematics in terms of developing the geometry and mathematics that we know today? Well, their arithmetic was not as impressive as their geometry. They certainly knew a lot of interesting things about arithmetic, but their actual notational arithmetical tools that they had were not always very powerful. In fact, often they would use the Babylonian system to make calculations. They had a kind of an alphabet kind of system where they represented numbers by letters of the alphabet, but this was cumbersome, and they did find that the Babylonian system was useful to borrow. But we can also see that they philosophically were not inclined to think about zero as a very important concept. Philosophically, they did not like or were mistrustful of this idea of the void or nothing, and maybe we can attribute that a little bit to their emphasis on geometry. So, for example, if you have a very geometrical approach to numbers, which they did—they liked to think about numbers as being represented by physical or geometrical patterns or designs. So, for example, triangular numbers figured prominently in their thinking: 1, 3, 6, 10, and so on. And if you're thinking about something like triangular numbers represented in this very physical way with these little triangular piles of circles, then it's not very natural for you to want to consider zero. Zero doesn't really figure in this because it's clear that this pattern of triangular numbers really ought to start with one—that's the starting point, and that's the next one, and that's the next one. It's somehow unnatural to talk about zero in this context. So aside from their geometrical inclination, they also had very strong ideas about philosophy. They thought very deeply about what the world was really about, and they came to this conclusion that it was not really proper to try to make something out of nothing, right? So, you know, the idea of nothing or a void was not sufficiently rich to be able to convert that into something. And in some sense, that's what we're doing, right? When we're thinking about zero, we're thinking about nothing, and then we're writing a symbol to represent that nothing, and then that symbol becomes something that we manipulate along with other numbers. So in some way, we're creating something out of nothing by the use of a modern symbol like this, but the Greeks did not like that way of thinking. They thought of both infinity and the void as things to be avoided conceptually—they were problematic. So this philosophical orientation was well expressed by a prominent Greek philosopher, Parmenides, who was a big influence on Plato, and he wrote on this topic in a number of different places. So here are some quotes from Parmenides. He says that "it is necessary to speak and to think about what is, for being is, but nothing is not." Okay, this is in the direction of "we should talk about things which actually are; we can't talk about things which are not, because they're not." Another quote: "Nothing comes from nothing"—this idea that you can't take nothing, that concept nothing, and create something out of it. That's a vacuous kind of activity; you shouldn't do that. So we also read from him that "for this view that that which is not exists can never predominate." He's saying that the idea that we can consider that which is not—so that which is not—and actually objectify that and create something out of it, that's not a valid way of thinking. So Parmenides had a big influence on Plato, who actually named one of his dialogues after him, and this really, you know, was representative of the Greek way of thinking and explains to some extent why theirs was not a fertile arithmetic for this idea of a symbol for zero to develop and blossom. I'd like to mention also that the Hebrew tradition is also in this direction. So in the Hebrew tradition, the void is also treated with suspicion, and one tries to avoid it. So the void is considered to be a state of separation from God. So first there was the void, and then there was God creating other stuff, and so the void is some kind of thing that we want to stay away from—it's not pleasant. So there's a philosophical orientation that is very close to people's thinking about this subject. You know, what does it mean to contemplate nothing, for example? What would the world be like if there was nothing? It's a very profound question, and people have been thinking about these kinds of things for a long time, and one's orientation towards this kind of philosophical position would naturally influence one's mathematics and the kind of mathematics that one might want to do. And so now we come to the heroes of this story—the culture that really introduced zero the way we know it now. And now we recognize that this was a fundamentally, crucially important development in the history of science, and I have talked about that in a previous video on the Hindu-Arabic numbers. So we're talking about ancient Indian philosophy, religion, and mathematics. The Indus Valley culture goes back to around 3,000 BC, but we have evidence to suggest that they were thinking along somewhat modern lines around 350 BC, where the Brahmi notation for numerals was starting to look pretty similar to the modern system. And they had a positional base 10 system that was in place around 500 AD. So here are some original kind of numerals—you can sort of see some similarities with some of our current numbers. Now, what's interesting in the Indian point of view here, amongst other things, is that their philosophical orientation seemed to predispose them to being happy with the idea of thinking about nothing as a legitimate object for study and incorporating it into mathematics. The Indians were not afraid of nothing, philosophically. In fact, they had a kind of reverence for it—they were very comfortable with the idea of nothingness and emptiness. Maybe simplistically we could say that nothing was viewed as a state from which we arise, and it's a state that we eventually return to. So maybe from an Indian point of view, there's nothing to be afraid of—it's a sort of natural part of the whole story. In any case, in Sanskrit, they had lots of different words representing different aspects of emptiness, void, nothing, and so on. So they conceptually had a framework in which nothing naturally played a reasonable role, and this was then, of course, very crucial in this development after they had these numbers, to eventually introduce actually a symbol for zero that turned out to be another number, essentially on par with the ones that they already had. So we have evidence that a dot for zero was in place around 200 BC, and that gradually was then replaced with the more familiar round circle zero. And by around 500 AD, it was in place as a placeholder in a decimal system pretty much the same as we have today. And then we come to Brahmagupta, a prominent Indian mathematician and astronomer, who around 628 AD took an important additional step to establish zero not just as a placeholder in a notational system for more general numbers, but actually to recognize that zero was an entity that had an independence—it had a role to play. And that role, in fact, turned out to be a rather important one in arithmetic. So that zero could be the result of an arithmetical computation: so 5 - 5 equals 0. That is then an equation that you could actually write down, whereas formerly it was difficult to say that 3 + 0 = 3. You can multiply by 0: 0 times 7 = 0. You can subtract 0 from something: 4 - 0 is 4. In fact, he even discusses division by zero and had an understanding that if you take a number and you divide by zero, you're getting something like infinite. All right, so this is a very huge step. And I've talked about, you know, the importance of the Hindu-Arabic system in many places, and it's really not possible to overestimate the importance of this whole development in the modern world. The world that we live in, the highly technological, mathematical world, rests very solidly on this—well, to us, it seems relatively simple and straightforward because we've been taught from a very early age, over and over and over again, how this numerical system works. But it's actually full of subtlety and richness, this Hindu-Arabic system, and the zero, of course, plays a very important role. Now, if we move ahead to European times, to more modern times, the role of nothing or zero was still actually quite contentious in the 16th, 17th, and 18th centuries, where people were examining the physical universe much more carefully. And, for example, questions about whether it was possible to consider empty space or not were being discussed. So the ancient Greeks had reservations about being able to treat empty space as something sort of independent of other stuff. But 17th and 18th-century scientists started investigating the possibility of thinking about space as being free from any stuff inside it—trying to create a vacuum, for example. And in the framework of Newton's laws, I mean, these are sort of operating in sort of empty space quite happily. But if we fast forward then again to 20th-century physics, things become then again more complicated, and the confidence and the certainty that maybe 19th-century mathematicians had about the void or vacuum started going in the other direction again, and people started becoming more aware that this was still a rather problematic concept and actually full of subtlety. So in modern 20th-century physics, the vacuum is treated as a really central but very complicated and rich kind of concept. So to the modern physicist, the vacuum is full of possibilities—it's not at all just empty space and nothing in it. So this is largely due to the role of quantum mechanics and a certain principle called the uncertainty principle, which roughly tells us that we can't really know everything about a situation, let's say, right? And that goes for any kind of situation, even a situation where it looks like there might be nothing in it. So if you have a situation where you have some empty piece of space and there's nothing in it, you still can't know everything about it. And that can be interpreted as saying that there's a lowest possible energy sort of for empty space, but you can't really make that energy completely zero—there's always some energy there. That's a kind of a bottom possible energy, but that supports various strange things that might happen. So in the modern vacuum, physicists think that there are all these simultaneous creations and annihilations of particle and antiparticle pairs that are sort of instantaneously happening where we, you know, have a little bit of empty space and suddenly there's an electron and a positron. So an electron has an antiparticle called a positron, and these things can be created sort of out of nothing, more or less, and then shortly thereafter, they can then annihilate each other and go back to nothing. So this quantum vacuum is sort of seething with all these possibilities of these particles emerging for a short period of time, and so on. So it's a very rich and mysterious kind of concept. So the modern physicist now is returning to not being so sure what nothing means, actually, in the kind of physical world. And so this is a very interesting story about nothing, and I think the last word has not been said. And I would like to really emphasize the possibility that maybe even in mathematics, which is at least potentially independent of these quantum mysteries, even in mathematics there's the potential room for thinking about nothing or the void in a different or an additional way to what we already have. So I don't necessarily want to change the role of zero, which is a great, you know, very important concept in our number system and so on, but I do want us to think more liberally about the possible role of nothing—a more fundamental kind of nothing than actually zero. So zero is still something that's been created out of nothing, but I want to propose that there's room in mathematics for nothing, which is still just nothing, okay? It hasn't been converted into something. We don't have to create something from nothing; we can think about nothing itself, and that can be a concept that can help us. And so, okay, that's what I want to do—I want to introduce nothing into mathematics again, but in a more primitive, fundamental, really void kind of fashion. I think there's a role to play, and that is especially clear when we have this multiset point of view that we're getting used to here. So in our next video, I want to talk about nothing in a multiset context and why mathematics really does need new thinking about nothing. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. [Music] 

---

# 187 - Multisets and a new framework for arithmetic ｜ Data Structures Math Foundations 187 [vZ5ItJkfLy4].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day. I'm Norman Wildberger. In this video, we're going to look at the arithmetic of natural numbers from a somewhat new point of view, with this data structure orientation relying particularly on the notion of an M-set or multiset. An interesting feature of this is that we are really looking at using the idea of nothing in a novel fashion here, so there's an opportunity for really rethinking this fundamental building block or pillar of mathematics. Arithmetic with natural numbers, after all, is absolutely fundamental in setting up so much of modern mathematics. So, the interesting kind of idea or dichotomy that I introduced a few videos back is that we can start off, when we're building up mathematics right at level zero, by thinking about nothing and something—these two fundamental orientations: nothing being a blank page, an empty space, and something being represented by some kind of mark, maybe a unit, something relatively simple that represents to us something. Okay, and this gives us the beginnings of the new framework for arithmetic, where we start by defining a natural number to be an M-set of marks, the simplest marks, such as, for example, this thing here. So, we have an M-set—it means we have square brackets, and inside we have some marks, and that's what a natural number is. So, it's very simplistic; it's really capturing the same kind of orientation that we had in our very first video, but it's a little bit more sophisticated because now we're putting things in the multiset framework. And we can now be more precise and say that "nat" represents the type of natural numbers represented this way. So, in this formulation of things, you have a natural number precisely when you've gone through the trouble of writing out an expression like this. An important distinction between what we're doing now and the way we started the series is that now the empty multiset is going to be included as a possible natural number because if we're thinking about natural numbers as being M-sets of marks, then it's possible to have an empty M-set, and that empty M-set we're going to call zero. So, that's our distinguished zero object in our world of natural numbers now. So, that's a distinction between the way we started the series, where zero was not included—historically, zero is not really a natural number—but with this sort of more modern point of view, we are including zero in the natural numbers. So, to summarize, the fundamental ingredients that we really have here, okay, in this setting up of natural numbers are the two concepts of nothing and something, represented by emptiness—just nothing on the page—and represented by this mark or this unit, something which doesn't have to be a straight line. It could be something else; it could be a little circle or dot, something else. But it should be uniform, so they should all look the same, and it should be something simple, something kind of fundamental and very easy to write down. And the other thing that we need, of course, are brackets—the brackets that we're using to denote multisets that contain our bunches of marks so that we know where they end, so they're bounded, and we have control over where they actually are. And so, we have these things called natural numbers, and here are some examples. So, there's a natural number, there's a natural number, there's one, there's one, there's another one, there's another one, there's another one, then there's the empty set natural number—they're all natural numbers. Now, some of them might be the same—for example, this one here looks a lot like this one, and these are the same—but that's fine. Notice that there's not intrinsically necessarily an order to them at this point—maybe a bit of a subtle issue, but we're just saying what a natural number is. We have not yet gone to the trouble of organizing them all in some kind of list or pattern. Okay, we've just identified what it means for an expression to be a natural number of type "nat." And let us compare this view to the 20th-century view of natural numbers, which was reliant on a prior set theory and posited that natural numbers were things that were cooked up from sets of sets, subsets and such, basically starting with the empty set. So, for example, the number four was the set {0, 1, 2, 3}, where 0 was the empty set, one was the set involving the empty set, two was the set consisting of 0 as well as one, and three is the set, well, consisting of 0, 1, and 2. So, in the 20th century, the view was that the number 4 officially is this thing. Now, our view, which is a 21st-century point of view—we're moving ahead here—is that the number 4 is represented by this: it's a multiset which has these marks in it. I want you to compare these two things, all right, and objectively, scientifically, rationally tell me which one is better, which one corresponds naturally to the way people think about natural numbers. Which of these views do you think supports a more sensible notion of operations, addition, multiplication, and proofs, etc.? Okay, so it's pretty clear what the answer is, of course. And let's have a look at equality—the idea of testifying when two expressions that we write down are actually equal. It's a very important notion, so we're going to have a look here. Here's the definition: two natural numbers are equal, underline that, precisely when we can match up their entries. What do I mean by that? Well, here are two natural numbers—there's a natural number, and there's a natural number—and the claim here is that these are equal. And what does it mean to be equal? Well, it means that we can match up the entries from this one with the entries from this one. So we'll match these and these, these ones are going to get matched up, and these are going to get matched up, and these are going to get matched up. You can also do that sort of physically—these are matched up, and then these, and then these, and then these and the next. Okay, so it's not really necessary to count them; we don't have to have a prior notion of counting to do this, okay? We're just matching them up in the way that some primitive person might match up, you know, five fingers with 5 X's on a piece of bark—just match them up. Here we have a natural number which is not equal to this natural number, and the reason is when we try to match them up—say, match that one with that one, and match this one with this one, and match this one with this one—well, then this one here does not match up with anyone there, and so we can't match them up, and so these two are not equal. So, this is meant to be simple, right? This is meant to be a theory that a primary school student can understand and appreciate and see, "Yeah, this is corresponding with something reasonable, something intuitive, something useful," right? So now we come to a very important and interesting development: what happens when we look at the addition of natural numbers—the simplest and most fundamental operation in some sense? Okay, so an interesting thing happens here, which again is probably going to go outside your natural intuition and experience—we're going to look at addition in a different way. So it's usually considered that addition is a binary operation; that almost goes without saying. When we're starting to do arithmetic in the usual framework, for example, we say A equals B plus C—we have number B, we have a number C, we're adding them together to get another number A. We start with two things, and we combine them, and we get another number. But in the M-set framework, which is something that most modern students of mathematics are not that familiar with, in the M-set or multiset framework, we have more flexibility, right? We can think a little bit bigger than we can, perhaps, with other data structures. In particular, the multiset framework is not ordered naturally, and it's not constrained by just having one of everything, right? So we can deal with multiplicities with M-sets, and we can deal with things not being ordered—that's the essential orientation. So that affects us when it comes to defining addition. So it turns out that in the M-set framework, having a binary operation is neither necessary nor desirable. What are we talking about here? Well, to add natural numbers, what we're going to do is we're going to form a new M-set containing all of the entries of the M-sets that we have. I want to show you this physically, okay? So here is a box, a blue box, and in this blue box, here we see a couple of little boxes or containers. So here's a container—it's got some coins in it, okay? There's another container, and then here's another container—got some more coins in it. And here's a third container containing some more coins, okay? So these are M-sets; it's a multiset of coins. There's some repetition, and they're not ordered. This is another M-set of coins, and here's another M-set of coins. So we have a couple of different M-sets of coins, and now I'm going to add them. I'm going to show you how to do this physically—I'm probably going to drop this doing this, but okay. So I'm going to take this one, and this one, and another one, okay? And I'm going to add them, but I'm going to do it in a non-binary fashion, okay? I want to add them all together. Let's see if we can do this, okay—three things in no particular order. Whoops, I just dropped one; better pick that one up. Okay, there we are, back on track here. Okay, we have three multisets. I'm now going to add—ready? Watch carefully, okay? There's no more left in our containers—we have dumped everything out, and you can see, perhaps, that we have all the coins there in this new multiset. We have added the three multisets together, but we have not done so in a binary way, right? We've just dumped them all out—independent of order, independent of having to do two at once, right? In principle, we can have, you know, lots of different containers, and we're just dumping them all here. We can have, say, three people doing this—a lot easier. We all sort of dumped our individual containers into the M-set, we'd be adding the contents. It's a very interesting, non-standard view of what addition is. So, for example, when we're writing stuff down, okay, here's an M-set of marks, here's an M-set of marks, here's an M-set of marks. So we have three natural numbers in this case, and we're going to add these natural numbers together. What do we do? We create a new M-set, which is obtained by copying each one of these entries into the M-set here. And how we actually do that, well, it's not prescribed. We can do that in a number of different ways—this thing here is essentially unordered. We're writing it in a kind of an ordered way, but that's not really essential. So we could take these ones and sort of go 1, 1, 1, or maybe I should take mark, mark, mark, and then mark, and then mark, mark, mark, mark. We're just dumping out the contents of these three into a new box, conceptually. I want you to think about having boxes, having a number of people just dumping into the containers, and everything dropping into a box. So this is the arithmetical analog of dumping the contents of several boxes into a new box. Now, with this naive, almost physical understanding of how we add multisets—in this case, add numbers, natural numbers—we note that the commutativity and associativity properties of addition are essentially obvious here, because we're only dealing with something, a situation where order is unimportant, and the only objects that we have—in fact, not a variety of coins—we just had these unit marks. That's all that ever appears in our natural numbers—they're always the same. And the commutativity and associativity are really obvious notes. If you've got a bunch of primary school students and you've got them practicing dumping multisets of coins—like, just coins of one type, let's say, you know, ten-cent pieces—so you have a multiset of ten-cent pieces, each of the kids has a multiset of ten-cent pieces, and you're, you know, adding all the M-sets together, they'd have a lot of fun. They would quickly determine that, you know, if Jane and Bill combined theirs into a box and Jane does hers a little bit before Bill and they look at what they get, and then they do it again and Bill drops his in a little bit before Jane, it's not going to make any difference—the order is not important. If we have three people and they kind of dump, and one person gets to dump theirs in first, it's not going to make any difference to the collection. I'm sure students are going to be able to understand that—it's going to be obvious to them after a while of playing around. So, you know, in particular, and with our linear way of writing things, if we have this M-set—mark, mark—and this M-set—mark, mark, mark—then we combine them, we get mark, mark, mark, mark, mark. And that's, of course, the same thing as if we combine mark, mark, mark with mark, mark, mark in the usual kind of fashion. Or if we have three of them, and we combine these guys first, and then we add that one, well, that's going to be the same as if we add the first one to the sum of these ones. So even children can be taught this system, and they can be taught this before they have a language—before they actually have been given names for the numbers. They don't need to know that this is the number five, this is the number three, this is the number two—we can just refer to this as the M-set "mark, mark, mark," and this is the M-set "mark, mark." Maybe we could use the word "units"; maybe it's a little bit better word—"unit, unit, unit." This one is "unit, unit." Okay, so they could deal with things at that level, and they could get a sense of how this number system works, independent of having actually a naming system for numbers. So this is a claim that I'm making—it's sort of my intuition. I haven't actually gone out and taught kindergarten kids this system, but maybe somebody could have a go at playing around in this fashion. And maybe somebody has already done this—maybe it's a standard kind of educational procedure for very young people. In any case, there's something very simple-minded and, I think, down-to-earth about this very concrete, simple conceptual framework or approach to arithmetic. So, especially advantageous is that these basic properties become essentially obvious facts to even children who are working with such a system—that's my direct recommendation. All right, so now let's look at multiplication. How are we going to define multiplication of natural numbers? If we look at the earlier videos in the series, you'll see that we've defined multiplication there in a somewhat geometrical fashion. Here we are going to stick with a more arithmetical approach. So, to multiply natural numbers, we're going to extend this following multiplication of our unit marks. So mark times mark equals mark, or if we prefer, unit times unit equals unit. We're going to extend that to multiplication of M-sets of units. So here is an M-set of units, or a natural number, here's another M-set of units. To form the product, we're just going to take all possible products between the units here and the units there—note that one times that, that one times that, and so on—all possible products, and write each one of those possible products down in its own M-set, so multiplicities are fine. And then we'll replace each one of these with what they're equal to, so we just get a unit repeated, in this case, six times. So unit, unit times unit, unit, unit equals unit, unit, unit, unit, unit, unit. So multiplying M-sets involves multiplying all possible products of their elements. It's the same procedure, the same orientation that we had when we were multiplying axles. So we might think about this as having a dramatically simplified multiplication table—that our new multiplication table for natural numbers basically just involves this: there's really only one thing to remember, that a unit times unit equals unit, or mark times mark equals mark, or if we prefer, something times something equals something. Now, the distributive law is, I think, almost obvious now, right? Suppose we have what we call 2 times 4 equals 8, and we have what we could call 3 times 4 equals 12. Now, written out with lots of units or marks instead of the words that we have—well, if we've gone through all possible products between these M-sets to get this, and we've gone through all possible products of these ones to get this, then if we combine these two—if we add these two M-sets to get this one—and multiply it by the same common M-set over here, right? Then all the possible products that we're going to get from now multiplying these marks with these ones—all those possible products are going to be either of this kind or of this kind. If we think of this thing here as being composed—say, those first two are from there and the next ones are from there—so I think it's pretty clear. If you got a child to do this enough times, they would catch on that if you are multiplying this times this, and you know what that is, you know what this is, that you could compute the product here just by taking these two and adding them. Of course, the way things would look would look a lot more extensive than, you know, much more primitive than what we usually do with our Hindu-Arabic system, but still, there's something kind of fundamental and straightforward in principle about this story, even though in practice it gets to be a little bit onerous for us to write, especially if the numbers are big. But the observant viewer will notice that we haven't quite covered all the bases with this multiplication rule that we've given, because what happens when we multiply, say, the empty M-set with a natural number? This is also a natural number—it's what we're calling zero. So what happens if we take 0 times, say, this M-set? Okay, so the rule is, well, we're supposed to consider all possible products of the form unit times unit, where one of them is obtained or taken from the first M-set and the other one's obtained from the second M-set. Okay, but in this case, there are no units in this first thing, so there are no such products.
