We cannot form any products—unit times unit—where they're respectively from both of the ingredient M-sets. We cannot, and so the product of these two natural numbers will also be necessarily empty. Another thing to observe is that associativity of multiplication, which is a more subtle issue, right? Why is 3 times 4 times 5 the same as 3 times 4 times 5? That essentially really ultimately boils down to this associative law—the fact that unit times unit times unit is the same as unit times unit times unit. That's true because this thing here is a unit, so this is just unit times unit, so this left-hand side, it's just about becoming a unit. And it's also true of the right-hand side—that's a unit, and one times or unit times unit is unit, so we're getting unit on both sides, so they're equal, right? So this fundamental associativity at the level of the units themselves then goes up to associativity of multiplication of natural numbers. So if we wanted to multiply, say, what we would call 2 times 4 times 3 in this order, we get the same as 2 times 4 times 3, because all of the various products, say, that are involved on the left-hand side look like what? So the entries of this thing here are things of the form a unit here times a unit here, okay? And then when we multiply by this M-set, we're taking a unit here times a unit here, multiplying that combination with one of the units over here. But on this side, what we're doing correspondingly is those same units that we flagged—maybe we color them red or something temporarily—over here, we would see them again, but then they would correspond to a product over here. So here, the red one times the red one would appear on the right-hand side, and then we multiply by the red one here. So basically, we're using the same law here, just applying it to all these possible triples of units obtained—unit here times unit here times unit here. So we've seen this kind of argument before in the maxwell kind of situation. We've observed that if we have an associative product at the pixel level, that that then lifts to an associative product at the maxwell level once we just define maximum multiplication as the M-set extension of pixel multiplication. It's exactly the same kind of story, same kind of pattern. So really, it's the single associativity at the level of these units which is really responsible for the associativity of natural number multiplication. So there's an argument to be made here, and you know, you could convince some primary school student, I think, by going through this carefully with some examples. But it would be just orders of magnitude more complicated to try to convince someone of the associativity of multiplication using the 20th-century set-theoretical formulation. Okay, that's just orders of magnitude much more complicated and convoluted, right? So we are really cutting to the chase here—I mean, I think we're really boiling things down to pretty well the simplest thing that they possibly could be with this M-set point of view. I think it's valuable to say a little bit more about the role of nothing in this story so far. So we've used nothing to create this empty M-set, which is then going to play the role of zero—that's sort of in the traditional way, very much like in the standard formulation of things. But in addition, I think it's important to recognize that there's now also a new role for nothing, which is not just contained in the empty set. So our basic multiplication, for example, right? We stated it in terms of just being between unit and unit—unit times unit equals unit. But we saw that actually it's a little bit more expansive, a little bit more precise, to also say what happens to the nothing times unit. So there's really these two aspects of things: there's nothing and there's something. And what we've already observed or defined is that something times something equals something. The story is really completed by this more elaborate multiplication table, which includes also the fact that nothing times nothing equals nothing. So you can see that there's nothing and there's nothing, and nothing times nothing equals nothing, all right? And also that nothing times something, represented by that entry there, is the same as something times nothing, represented by that entry there, which is nothing, okay? So the true multiplication table, sorted at the level below the natural numbers themselves, arguably has these two objects or two concepts involved. There's both the nothing and the something—it's just that the nothing we're agreeing, we're going to write it as an empty page or as an empty piece of our page, and so it ends up looking blank. Okay, but it's a new kind of way of thinking about nothing and the role of nothing, that we're going to actually have something blank. It's not going to be a symbol that represents that blankness—the actual blankness itself is going to be addressed; we're going to acknowledge it to let it be what it wants to be. It's just empty, and so we look and we see, "Oh, there's emptiness there." It's not as if somebody has forgotten to write something, right? It's meant to be empty—it is empty. It's a new kind of thinking that's going to be very valuable for us. It's very oriented towards an M-set point of view. When we're doing arithmetic with M-sets, and we're going to jack this up to doing algebra involving M-sets as well, linear algebra, and so on, we're going to see more and more the usefulness of being able to think about nothing as just being an empty part of the page, not being represented by a symbol—just letting it be nothing. Okay, so we have the beginnings now of a new kind of arithmetic, right? An arithmetic which is multiset-oriented, which incorporates these fundamental aspects of nothing and something, and which really ends up being aligned very much with a naive layperson's approach to what natural numbers might be, at the most simplistic kind of level. So I think there's going to be a lot of theoretical implications of this, but there's also educational aspects that I think are worth touching upon. In particular, there is the connection between these numbers that we're invoking here and the names for the numbers. So we need to have a discussion about the importance of naming numbers, which also touches base with the issue of ordering numbers. So those two issues are closely related—ordering and naming—and they're both things that impact very directly on young people's original understanding of arithmetic, how they think about what arithmetic with natural numbers is. So I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger—thanks for listening. 

---

# 188 - Naming and ordering numbers for students ｜ Data structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 188 [Puk-ipOTiD4].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day. I'm Norman Wildberger. Today we're going to look at the educational aspects of this new approach to arithmetic that I'm proposing, which in some sense goes back to our first few videos in this series many years ago now, where we're thinking about a natural number as being represented by a very simple kind of representation of marks on a piece of bark or strokes on a page. We're making that a little bit more explicit by talking about M-sets of units—unit somethings or marks—and we're basically employing an M-set or multiset orientation to define arithmetic, for example, addition like this or multiplication like this. We've identified this type "nat," which represents multisets of marks, and I claim that this kind of arithmetic is in some sense more fundamental and more basic than our usual Hindu-Arabic arithmetic that we teach young people involving the symbols 1, 2, 3, 6, and so on. There is something more primitive and more elementary about this, which I think makes it possibly attractive for educators to try to instill numeracy into very young people. So today, we're going to be looking at the issues of naming and ordering these primitive natural numbers, and we want to do it in a way that's largely independent of some higher verbal facility requiring understanding of these Hindu-Arabic numerals. All right, so since we're human beings, we like to talk and to express ourselves verbally as well as by writing things down. So it's natural for us to look to having names for these simple kinds of primitive natural numbers we're talking about. So let's do that now, but perhaps in a somewhat surprising and maybe naive way. All right, so our first number is 0. This is this empty multiset—just brackets with a bit of empty space inside, okay? And we're going to give the traditional name "zero" to that. And then the next one is what we're going to call "one." So we just have a single unit something inside our M-set; we'll call that "one." And now, what about this one? Well, the temptation, of course, is to call it "two." That's already somewhat sophisticated—the idea of introducing a new name for every next number. What we're going to do, rather, is to refer to this as "11." Now, we could also call it "mark, mark" or "unit, unit," but I think it's preferable to actually use "11." This is in keeping with what a young person might want to do. So technically speaking, this is actually "one plus one"; it's actually what you get when you take this one and you add one. But let's give it a name, and let's call it "11." And similarly, let's call this one "111," and so on. You can see what the pattern is. So this is, in fact, a base-one system, if you like. We're really only going to be using these two words: "zero" and "one," and everything else is going to be built up from just repeating this particular word. And this is very much in keeping with our expressions, what we're writing down—we're really only writing down two kinds of things: either an empty space or a mark, and we're possibly repeating the marks inside our brackets. So the verbal simplicity is reflecting the written simplicity—somehow it makes sense. And of course, for a young person, learning this kind of system is, relatively speaking, a piece of cake. This is vastly simpler than learning the standard Hindu-Arabic words: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, and then eleven, and then twelve, thirteen, and fourteen. That's all reasonably complicated and takes a fair amount of repetition and learning. In fact, it's not really logically that clear, is it? There's something very logically clear about this system, even though it may be somewhat primitive. So, for example, let us look at the name of this natural number, okay? What is its name? It is "111111." That is the name of this natural number. Now, we've talked about how the original motivation for natural numbers probably arose in the context of wanting to measure how many of a certain thing there were out in the real world—maybe antelope at a waterhole. So this is a fundamental usage of numbers: to measure the size of, well, what we're calling M-sets or multisets—collections of stuff out there in the world. So, for example, here is an M-set which consists of these letters, okay? So even a student who perhaps hasn't learned how to read can still understand that these are things called letters, and we can count them in the following way: we can just go through the letters, and wherever there's a letter, we can make a mark. So for this letter, we make a mark, then another one, another one, another one, one more, one more, one more, one more. So what we've done is we've replaced each of the objects in this collection that we're interested in with one of our simple unit marks. And we can say that what we've done then is to measure the size of this collection or M-set. So the size of this M-set is this natural number. Now, M-sets in real life don't necessarily have to come in these kinds of linear bracketed forms like this. So here might be another kind of example that you could give a young person. So here is a box of some kind, and it has certain objects in it, and the objects are of the pleasant kind, and we might be interested in how big this box is in the sense of how many things are there in it. So the young person, understanding the strategy, would do the following: they would say, "Well, what we're going to do is we're going to just create a number out of the box by replacing the objects in here with our unit marks." So I'm going to make a mark there for this thing, a mark there for that heart, a mark there for that diamond, another mark there for that diamond, let's see, that rectangle gets a mark, that heart gets a mark, that rectangle gets a mark, and there's a square over there—that gets a mark, right? So I've replaced this multiset of objects with a multiset of marks. It's a simplification. So measuring the size of something is some kind of simplification where you just replace objects with our fundamental unit marks. Now, this isn't really in the standard form for a natural number, so we would want our student to express this more in keeping with our terminology. So then they would be obliged to organize these marks, right? To write them down in the sequential fashion. And so they'd have to do this in some kind of a reasonable way so they don't get confused—maybe that one there, then that one, that one, that one, that one, that one, that one, that one, and then we close the set. So let's observe that counting is actually not really involved here. It's not the case that we are counting how many of these things there are—that we have a prior verbal system for ordering numbers. Rather, what we're doing is we're just replacing objects with marks and then organizing them in a linear fashion, okay? So it's really prior to what we usually think of as counting—some kind of more simple, basic, primeval kind of operation. So to measure an M-set, we just replace each of its elements with a unit something or mark—in other words, one of our little strokes. That's what measuring M-sets means. Now, doing arithmetic with numbers is so familiar to us and ingrained in us that sometimes we're not even aware of relatively basic distinctions. And a very important distinction is that there are at least two different kinds of roles that numbers play when we're using them to describe the world around us. So numbers, as we've seen, are used to measure the sizes of collections or M-sets, but they can also be used for counting in the sense of ordering. And mathematicians have names for these different kinds of usages. So we talk about cardinal numbers and ordinal numbers. So this is somewhat fancy terminology that refers to numbers being used in the context of measuring sizes of M-sets typically, and here the ordinal numbers refer to counting the position in a list. We have something ordered, and you want to refer to a particular element in that order. So let's give two examples of these different usages of numbers. So a cardinal number usage is one that we've already been looking at. So here is an M-set, the M-set {A, C, D, A}, and we're interested in measuring how big that M-set is. So that's a cardinal number usage. So we're measuring that set, and we're getting that the answer is the number "1111," okay? So now here's an ordinal number usage, where we're referring to not an M-set, which is a collection of stuff, but we're actually interested in a list. So here's a list. You can tell it's a list because it has square brackets and it has commas, and so the commas are telling us that in this case, these elements are ordered. We're starting with A, and then B, and then C, and then D. Over here, there were no commas—this is an unordered multiset. If we interchange the order, things would not change. This is a list—the order does matter. So the question here is, what is the "1111" element of this list? The "1111"—we're talking about trying to find the element, not which is the one element, and not the "11" element, but the "1111" element, and we identify that that is actually C. So this usage of the number "1111" is subtly but importantly different from the usage of this number "1111." Here, we're measuring the size, the largeness of a collection or an M-set, and here we're specifying the position inside some ordered structure, inside some kind of sequence, which for us is best captured by the idea of a list. Now, this ordinal usage of numbers connects naturally with a natural order on natural numbers. So we originally defined natural numbers just in terms of what they are—a natural number is an M-set of marks, and we had lots of different kinds of M-sets. Now we're observing that our natural numbers can actually be arranged in a very efficient and natural way. We can list them; we can put them in a sequence starting from the smallest ones and working our way step by step, concretely, one at a time, to bigger and bigger numbers. And here, of course, is this sequence—the ordering that we're talking about, where we're starting with, in this case, the empty M-set, or 0, and then we have the M-set "1," and then "11," and then "111," "1111," "11111," and "111111." And we can keep going. So here, the three dots refer to the fact that we can go from one to the next by adding one—that is the systematic way of going from one of these to the next one, we're adding one. So that leads naturally to this terminology, which is useful: the idea of a successor of a natural number. So if "K" is a natural number—let's introduce some abstract letter to represent it—then its successor is, we might introduce this notation, "K prime." That means "K plus 1." So this is the number one, which consists of a single mark, okay? What's inside here is not the number one—there's a mark inside, that unit something. This whole thing is the number one. So, for example, here's a number—the number is "111," and its successor is, well, what you get when you take the number "111" and you add one, you add a mark, you get "1111." So that's a successor—that's the successor of that. And that's really what generates this sequence or this ordering of natural numbers. Okay, so there's now a theorem here that emerges, which is a pretty obvious theorem in some ways, but it's probably useful to lay it out concretely, and let's call it the "Theorem of Induction." So the theorem of induction is that any natural number can be obtained by starting with the empty M-set, 0, then possibly taking successors. So we've seen that we can start with 0, and then we can take a successor, we can get one, and then we can get "11," and then we get "111," and then we get "1111," and then "11111." What we're stating now is that if we had any natural number—someone writes down the natural number—that it actually is in this sequence somewhere. That if we start with zero, add one, add one, add one, eventually, at some point, we will get to that natural number that we've written down. This sequence does not miss some natural number somewhere. Okay, what is the proof case? It's close to being intuitively obvious, but if we had to write something down to convince some, maybe young person, we might say the following: if the natural number that we're starting with is 0 itself, well, then it is of this form—it's contained in this prescription because it's the first thing that we're starting with. If it's not 0, that means it contains a mark, and so it can be written as the successor of a number with fewer marks. In other words, it can be written as the successor of the number that we get by removing that mark. And we can then continue to do that—we can continue to find a number of which that is the successor by again removing a mark. So we can go from a natural number that's written down, we can find a finite sequence of numbers, each of which the one that you have is a successor of, and then eventually we get back to the empty set. That's a consequence of the fact that we've written down a finite number of marks—we've written down a specific number of marks, and so we're going to rub them off one at a time. Well, that's only going to take a certain number of steps—namely, the number of marks—and then eventually we will get down to the empty set. So it's a kind of argument that says, "All right, given the natural number, we can get back to the empty set in this very concrete, prescribed way." All right, so for example, here is a number—it's the number "111111." And we can ask, can this actually be obtained from the empty set, or 0, by taking successors? Well, we would answer that question one way, I think. The natural way is to take the number that we're given and to write it as a successor of something—we write it as a successor of what we get by removing one of the marks. So this is the successor of "11111." And similarly, this thing is the successor of "1111," what we get by removing one of the marks. This is the successor of "111." This is the successor of "11." This is the successor of "1." And this is the successor of "0." So the answer is, of course, yes, and we've sort of clearly laid out then what is the sequence that we have to go through to go from original 0 to the number that we're interested in. We have to go back up this staircase that we've just gone down. So we start with 0, and then we go to 1, and then "11," then "111," and then "1111," and then "11111," and then the next step, "111111," we get to the number that we've started with. So that, yes, this number actually is in the sequence or the listing or the natural ordering of natural numbers. So when I was younger, there was a very popular TV show for young people called "Sesame Street." Maybe it is still around, with lots of funny monsters and characters and so on. But they also had a lot of training or familiarization for students with respect to numbers. So there would be a lot of signs flashing around like "1, 2, 3," the Hindu-Arabic symbols, and, you know, one plus two is three, and three plus four is seven, and so on. And I think that's great because it introduces young people to the idea of numeracy and being familiar with natural numbers. But I'm not so convinced that these are the first steps that young people should go down. I think it's possible that this kind of more primitive number sense, where we just deal with natural numbers in this primitive form—"1111," "111111," and so on—that it's somehow simpler and more in keeping with trying to imbue a numerical sense into young people, independent of a verbal facility that may only come later. So, in principle, I think working with these kinds of natural numbers could actually happen at a much earlier age—maybe two years old, three years old. We can already get young people doing arithmetic in this context. So here are some exercises that we might give students in this direction. Okay, so for example, they might want to find the sizes of these M-sets. So here are some collections, and we want to write down what their sizes are. And the important thing is that the students do not yet need to know how to count, okay? They can do this before they have learned to count. They can write down marks for each one of these things, matching up the objects with marks, very much the same way that a primitive person at a waterhole might match up antelope with X's on a piece of bark. They can make computations like this: they can calculate what is "111" plus "111111," and they can write down the answer, and then they can tell you what the answer is in the sense of, "The answer is '1111111.'" They can do multiplication. They can do addition with more than just two things. So we can get a three-year-old to understand what's being asked here and to actually make computations. We can ask them to find the successor of some number like this. We can get them to explore the ordinal aspects of numbers. We can ask them for the "11111" element of this sequence. We can ask them to verify some basic law of arithmetic—what is "111" plus "111111"? Is that the same as "111111" plus "111"? In other words, if you interchange these two things, do you still get the same thing? They can verify that if you do this one first, and then you do this one on separate pieces of paper, and then you compare, lo and behold, you get the same thing. All right, so these are just indicative kinds of examples that I think suggest that it might be interesting to try to get young people engaged in numerical intuition and actual computation at a very early age, at an age that's before they normally do the kind of Hindu-Arabic arithmetic that they currently do. Logically and maybe pedagogically, this primitive natural number arithmetic really ought to come before the Hindu-Arabic arithmetic. In our next video, we do want to turn our attention to the Hindu-Arabic arithmetic, which of course is very important and is practically much more sensible than this is. But still, it's important for us to distinguish between these two kinds of things. So we want to make some space between this primitive natural number arithmetic and the Hindu-Arabic arithmetic. That is for several reasons, one of which is that when we're talking about very, very big numbers, then the Hindu-Arabic system is actually much more powerful, but also correspondingly more problematic to deal with than the primitive one. So we're going to have to explore not just the pros but also the cons of moving from primitive natural numbers to Hindu-Arabic ones. It's a bit of a trade-off, actually. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. 

---

# 189 - The Hindu Arabic number system revisited ｜ Data Structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 189 [711l-twD3gk].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day. I'm Norman Wildberger. We're having another look at arithmetic—the foundations of arithmetic, the fundamental basis on which modern mathematics is built. And it all comes down, of course, to natural numbers and the arithmetic with natural numbers. And this turns out to be considerably more subtle than it's usually thought, because we've appreciated now the huge complexity involved in larger and larger numbers. This is a key insight that we have been developing. So we know now that just to hope that the kinds of experience and understanding that we have developed for smaller numbers—the hope that that just extends in a natural way to all natural numbers—is misguided. We have to think carefully about the scale at which we can legitimately and logically comprehend, define, and prove our arithmetic is valid. So the reality is probably that the currently regarded theory of natural numbers is too wide, it's too ambiguous, it's too all-embracing. We need to actually be a little bit more careful in prescribing different aspects of the things which are now currently just clumped together. In particular, there are at least three notably distinct levels at which we can try to access what natural numbers are: The primitive natural number level, where we just regard a natural number as an M-set of marks, and we oblige ourselves to actually write down such an M-set explicitly and completely in order to have the natural number. And that's a reasonable type, which we have some familiarity with now. That's "nat." Then there are the Hindu-Arabic natural numbers, the most familiar ones, of course, to us, where we write down something like this: 3,793. And we're going to move to try to understand what that's all about in today's video, and we're going to try to move towards establishing some kind of definition for this other type "num," which is distinguished from "nat." But there's also this third level, which we're aware of: the arithmetic expressions for natural numbers, where we're allowed to concoct expressions involving arithmetical operations—simple ones like this, or maybe even more complicated ones like we've been looking at. And these are also candidates for numbers, but they are definitely different kinds of objects than these ones, and these ones are different kinds of objects than these ones. And they all have different scales at which they're more appropriate. Okay, that's an important thing to realize—that relatively small numbers are well captured with this; medium kinds of numbers are captured with this; and very large numbers, you have to go to some system like this. It's like Papa Bear, Mama Bear, and Baby Bear kinds of numbers. So we've had a look at primitive natural numbers, and we've seen that they have a simple arithmetic which is very easy to understand and easy to compute with, and that even relatively small children can understand sort of why this kind of arithmetic obeys the simple, important arithmetical laws that it does. When we go to Hindu-Arabic numbers, it's really a quite different story. Okay, so it turns out this is already a very subtle issue about how to set up a theory of Hindu-Arabic numbers. And we'll see here that there are at least three different ways of introducing them—at least three different ways of orienting our approach to Hindu-Arabic numbers. Now, we have to say, of course, and have to recognize, that we are so familiar with the Hindu-Arabic system because we've been taught this system from a very early age, and we have spent hours and hours—hundreds of hours—practicing, learning, and seeing applications of this Hindu-Arabic system. So it's really almost hardwired into our mental frameworks, and we shouldn't take that for granted. Okay, it's actually not a replacement for a solid logical foundation for what actually these things are. So that's what we're looking for here. We all know that we can, you know, do Hindu-Arabic arithmetic, at least for relatively small numbers where the calculator is not needed. But what exactly is a Hindu-Arabic number, and what exactly are the definitions of addition, multiplication, and so on? And how does one actually prove the laws of arithmetic for these objects? That is what we're interested in here today. So, our first approach to possibly setting up Hindu-Arabic numbers is to regard them as short forms for primitive natural numbers—that they provide us with a convenient way of concisely expressing these primitive natural numbers, which can be quite onerous to write down and express. So what we do is we look at the primitive natural numbers, and we give them the usual names. So this one here is called zero, and we give it the alternate symbol that we're familiar with. This thing here with a single mark in it is the number one with its usual number. This is two, this is three, and so on. This thing here would be twenty-seven written like this. Okay, we're all very familiar with the system here, but we know that it does take some explaining. I mean, to actually explain it to someone who didn't know what it was about, we'd have to talk about how we take one of these big M-sets of marks and subdivide them into ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, and so on, and then count the numbers of those various things and then string them together in our sort of Hindu-Arabic list. So if we proceed this way—and this is a valid, possible way of proceeding—okay, that a Hindu-Arabic number is just a short form for a primitive natural number, then the definition of operations on Hindu-Arabic numbers simply reduces to those of the primitive natural numbers. It's just a reflection of this prior arithmetic that we already have in place for the primitive natural numbers. So instead of saying "1111 + 1111 = 1111," something like that, we might rather say "9 + 13 = 22." Okay, it's just a shorthand for it. So logically, then, nothing really too much in addition is required. So if we do take this approach, then Hindu-Arabic arithmetic is completely equivalent logically to the arithmetic with natural numbers that we already have—the arithmetic with primitive natural numbers. And we can flag this usage in the following way. So let's write the following: that if we write seven is of type "nat," okay, what that means is that seven is defined as this thing here. So this is the actual object in "nat." And when we write this, we agree that this thing here is defined to be this. In other words, this is another name for this. Okay, why don't we do this then? End of story? Because there's a serious disadvantage with this, but it's a logical disadvantage that really only becomes apparent once we step outside the bounds of familiar, relatively small arithmetic. The disadvantage is that we are logically restricted to numbers less than 10^200. Why? Because this is the maximum possible size of a primitive natural number in our universe, roughly. We've seen that if you want to write down a bunch of ones or marks, okay, the very, very most that you could do, that is determined by how much room there is in the universe and how small you can write. And if you're writing at the very smallest scale, which is, let's say, the Planck scale, and you're writing to fill the entire universe, then we've decided that there's roughly 10^200 such little Planck cubes filling our universe, very roughly. Okay, and that's then an upper bound for the number of marks that you can make. Of course, you could never make anywhere near that many marks, but at least in principle, we can contemplate something like up to that amount. Okay, but we are constrained roughly somewhere at this level, right? We are not able to conceptually even imagine in our universe M-sets of stuff with a lot more than this number. For example, these various numbers that we've been talking about—Z or Z-prime or 3-star n^2 4—right? These numbers are so big that they completely overwhelm the possibility of actually exhibiting an M-set with this number of objects in it. Okay, so that means that if we're going to attach our Hindu-Arabic numbers to primitive natural numbers, then we are constrained by whatever bounds we have on the primitive natural numbers, and we are constrained because the universe is probably only so big, and we can write probably only so small. So let me illustrate this point by giving you an example of something that's arithmetically impossible for us, even though we would actually want it to be possible. So consider the question of adding this natural number to one. Well, this is the natural number 96511. I'm not going to tell you all of it, okay? You can read it there. It's a natural number with roughly about 200 digits. Therefore, this natural number is so big that it represents the size of a set which, or a multiset which, is impossible to conceive of in our universe. We cannot write down and exhibit, even theoretically, even in principle, an M-set or a set which has this number of objects in it. So if we're thinking about this as representing a primitive natural number, we are just stepping beyond the bounds of what we actually can do. So we would have to look at something like this and say, "I'm sorry, this does not make any sense because we cannot interpret this thing as a primitive natural number." But this is too limiting for us. This is too limiting. We want to be able to make calculations like this because we all could make this calculation, right? The end of doing this is you just change that three to a four. Our computers can deal with numbers which are this big—they can do all kinds of great computations with them. And if we're doing geometry in a sort of integer framework or rational number framework, once you go a few steps in complexity in some actual specific situation where you're actually making computations, then you shouldn't be surprised to have bigger and bigger numerators and denominators occurring—that'll typically be what happens, okay? When you make the addition of fractions or multiplication of fractions, then the denominators and the numerators generally get bigger. So every time you make a computation, then you're increasing sizes, and so it doesn't take that long before, you know, we can realistically end up with numerators and denominators that are of this order. We want to be able to deal with them—we don't want to just throw up our hands and say, "I'm sorry, that's no longer supported by our theoretical constructs." So we want to do bigger arithmetic, and so we need to free "num" from "nat." We do not want to identify these two things. We do not want "num" to just be a replacement or a short form for "nat." That's too limiting. All right, so notice how this understanding really does depend on our investigations of bigger numbers and our investigation about complexity, right? We've been thinking about complexity—we have this sense that, you know, there are limits out there in terms of how big you can write down expressions and correspondingly how big are the numbers that you have some realistic hope of writing down in some fashion or another. All right, so we're trying to address that—we're not pretending that we're living in some unearthly kind of universe where there's an infinite amount of room and we can just do infinitely many things just by waving our hands around. We're not doing that—we want to really pin things down solidly. All right, so what we want to do is we want to free "num" from "nat." We want to have our Hindu-Arabic number system allow us bigger arithmetic than we can do just with "nat." Not as great, but we want to do something more. All right, so let's look at a separate line of inquiry. Let's think about Hindu-Arabic numbers as a separate system from "nat," but which correlates with it. So that's our aim—to try to see some direction in which we could create a theory of natural numbers based on Hindu-Arabic representation. Okay, so this is a lot more work. This is a lot fancier than dealing with primitive natural numbers. It's several orders of magnitude more complicated, in fact. In fact, I claim it has not yet been done in 2016. There's no place where you can find a proper theory of Hindu-Arabic numbers. In particular, Peano-type axioms—some of you will have heard of an axiomatic approach to numbers—so Peano axioms don't really help us either because Peano is resting on the idea of building up natural numbers by successors. And if you want to start with one and then you add one, and then you add one, and then you add one, and so on, and you want to kind of build up an arithmetic, you're really essentially in the realm of primitive natural numbers. Okay, so Peano is just an axiomatic approach to what we're calling natural numbers. In fact, it doesn't really aid us considerably, but we'll talk about Peano at some other point. But in any case, Peano is not going to help us go beyond the primitive natural numbers. We need to do something else—we need to go beyond this need to have our numbers connected with an actual M-set or a sequence of things that we can write down starting from one. Okay, so here's a rough outline of how to proceed. So here's a program that somebody could think very carefully about and work towards creating a theory of Hindu-Arabic numbers. Isn't it remarkable that in 2016, such a program is possible, that there's this road that could be gone down by somebody who's careful and serious enough and really wants to do some work to establish arithmetic? That's what we're talking about. It's not some tangential thing built on top of the extremities of a theory built on the extremities of some other theory. This is core mathematics we're discussing here, isn't it? Okay, so how could we start? We could start by defining Hindu-Arabic numbers as expressions like this. Okay, that's not so hard to do. So a Hindu-Arabic number is an expression like this. And what does that mean? Well, it's a list, or if you like, a string of digits. The digits are all taken from this set, the usual ones: 0 to 9. And the strings have the property that, well, they can be arbitrary, except for the rule that they can't begin with zero—they can't begin with zero except for the single number zero itself. That's the only number that can start with the digit zero. By "start," I mean the leftmost digit is zero, and all lists must be non-empty. We're not allowing any empty lists. All right, so that is a reasonable definition of what a Hindu-Arabic number is, and we can agree that you have a Hindu-Arabic number precisely when you can write down such a string. You can write down a string like this one—you have a Hindu-Arabic number. So we don't need to suppose that there's some set somewhere that this is counting. Okay, the Hindu-Arabic number is just this string that you write down. That is a very sensible and concrete and reasonable way of defining these things into existence. That'd be like step one. Okay, so now that we have the objects, the numbers themselves, we are obliged to define the operations—so say addition and multiplication, the two ones that we usually start with. How are we going to define these? So note that now it's no longer possible for us to go back to the natural numbers. We can't say, "Take your Hindu-Arabic number and find an M-set or a collection or a set that has that many objects in it, and take your other Hindu-Arabic number and find another M-set that has that many, and then dump them together, and then count how many we have." Okay, that works only if we're in the realm of possibilities for exhibiting these M-sets, and we've already sort of flagged that we are going beyond that. Okay, so we cannot rely on just converting our Hindu-Arabic numbers to natural numbers and working there. But nevertheless, we can still connect with what we're doing with natural numbers. So we could create addition and multiplication tables. Okay, and so here's an addition table for just the digits themselves, and here is a multiplication table for the digits themselves. Well, the tables are not complete, but I'm sure you can all complete them. Okay, so one could sort of write down these tables and say, "This is how we're going to define the addition of just our Hindu-Arabic numbers, which are given by single digits. If we want to know what is 2 + 6, we look under the table and we say, 'Ah, it's 8.' If we want to know what is 2 * 7, we look under the table and we say, 'Ah, it's 14.'" Okay, so of course this table doesn't tell us how to add more generally, but it's a start. Okay, so we give these tables, and we define the operations on the single digits this way. And of course, we all know that these things do come from our understanding of natural numbers, the primitive natural numbers, and how addition and multiplication work for them. But in fact, we don't really need to say so. Okay, we can just say, "Here are two tables, and we're going to define addition and multiplication by these tables." That's how we tell our computer how to add digits—we don't have to explain to the computer where we got these things from. All right, so now for the second part of that second step—we need to extend our understanding or definition of addition and multiplication to general Hindu-Arabic numbers. Okay, because we've had lots of experience at this in primary school, we know at least in principle how this should proceed—or roughly, anyway. Because we know how to use those addition and multiplication tables to add and multiply more general numbers. If we want to add 237 and 49, we know that we start on the right and we add seven and nine using the table—we get 16, we write the six, we put the one up there, and then we add three and four, we get seven, we add the one, we get eight, and then we bring this two down. If we want to multiply 237 by 49, well, we multiply 9 * 7—63, we write the three, we put the six up there; then 9 * 3 is 27, we add the 6—that's 33, we put a three down, there's a three coming up there, etc. All right, so it's a question then of taking the familiar knowledge about how to actually add Hindu-Arabic numbers and telling our computer how to do that—that's essentially what the definition is. So extending our definition from single-digit operations to general string operations. All right, so this is a fair amount of work, but it's not impossible. You could certainly program a computer to do this, and in fact, if you look at computers that do have code that works on Hindu-Arabic numbers, well, they have something like this, right? That's what they do. And then we have a third challenge, which is to prove the laws of arithmetic. This is more challenging than you might think because you are not allowed to use the prior knowledge of arithmetic with natural numbers, with primitive natural numbers, okay, except for small numbers. So we can use the fact that for small numbers we know that 3 + 7 + 8 is the same as 3 + 7 + 8, you know, so we can use those things. But we don't know that associativity or, say, the distributive law or even commutativity is necessarily going to be operating on these huge Hindu-Arabic numbers. Of course, we have this intuition that they will, okay, but it's not a valid argument to say, "Well, we just convert these big Hindu-Arabic numbers to M-sets, and then we do the primitive natural number operations there; we know that things are commutative or associative or distributive there, and then we go back up." That argument only holds up to the limits of our natural or primitive natural number system, and we're proposing to go much beyond that. So we need separate, independent proofs of the laws of arithmetic. Can you appreciate that there's something to be done here? And it's actually a considerable amount of work, and it's not at all, you know, immediately obvious what the most efficient and elegant way of doing this is. This would be a great Master's project, I think, right? A student could easily spend a year or two thinking about how to do this really in a nice, elegant way—it would be very instructive. Okay, and so if we went down this road, which I'm broadly outlining here, we could put that together and then get a new system, which we could call "num"—Hindu-Arabic numbers—which extends the arithmetic of natural numbers and coincides with it when there's overlap. So you've probably guessed that that's not how we're going to proceed. We're going to proceed down a different path. So this is the third approach that I want to suggest to you in terms of establishing an arithmetic for Hindu-Arabic numbers. So I propose that Hindu-Arabic numbers can be viewed as multisets, and when we take this M-set point of view, then we can exploit things that we already know, and we can create an alternate form of the Hindu-Arabic arithmetic. But it will require us to think a little bit differently, so we have to be prepared to shift our orientation a little bit—but you're used to that anyway on this channel, right? Okay, so the motivation for this is the following: suppose that we take a Hindu-Arabic number like 43, which of course is 3 + 4 * 10^1. So in terms of powers of 10, which is naturally what's going on here, it's 3 * 10^0 plus 4 * 10^1. So here's another way of representing that information as an M-set. So here's an M-set—it's got square brackets and no commas, and it's got repetitions. There are three singletons here—singletons with zeros in them—and there are four singletons with ones in them, right? So these are just like lists with one element, and there are three zeros here to represent the fact that there's 3 * 10^0 in this Hindu-Arabic number, and there are four singletons with ones to represent the fact that we have 4 * 10^1 in this number here. So this is maybe rather long, but it is a legitimate way of representing the information contained in that Hindu-Arabic number. Let's say we have a slightly bigger one, like 512. Well, that's 2 * 10^0 + 1 * 10^1 + 5 * 10^2. And then that information can be represented in an M-set point of view by having two singletons of zeros, one singleton of one, and five singletons of twos. This is then an M-set which essentially contains the information encoded in this Hindu-Arabic number, and it does so in a way that actually corresponds with what we're writing down. So you could look at this and reasonably easily write down this thing here without having to convert to a natural number—so we're not going via "nat" here, okay? Now, if we stare at these things, we say, "Wait a minute, we've seen this kind of thing before." These are just "vexels"—these are the kinds of objects we were playing around with when we were talking about maxel theory, our sort of novel point of view towards matrices and elementary linear algebra, right? Vexels were some kind of M-set replacement for vectors, and they're exactly like this—they're just multisets of singletons, a singleton being a list with a single natural number in it, where a natural number now is allowed to be zero as well. That's a little bit of a difference from previously, okay? So the possibility is now to harness our earlier discussion, our earlier discussion of vexels and that framework, to harness that as a scaffolding to build up a theory of Hindu-Arabic numbers. So we need an arithmetic with vexels, and pleasantly, this then also draws in earlier discussions about pols and what we called poly numbers. So we're going to be unifying a whole bunch of things here—algebra in terms of poly numbers, linear algebra in terms of these vexels, and fundamental arithmetic in terms of Hindu-Arabic numbers are all going to start being part of the same intimately connected story. We'll start on that next time. Hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. [Music] 

---

# 190 - Numbers, polynumbers and arithmetic with vexels I ｜ Data Structures Math Foundations 190 [OFzl37nX58A].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In today's video, we're moving towards understanding the Hindu-Arabic framework for numbers, but I'd like to do that by bringing together earlier work that we did on poly numbers—our versions of polynomials—and connecting that with what we've been discussing about vexels relatively recently. We started talking about poly numbers, which are just another name for polynomials essentially, in the video of Math Foundations 57 and the ones following that. The basic idea is very simple: we have our various number systems, which are a little bit different from what we're calling things now. In particular, the natural numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, we used to call num plus, and now we're just referring to that as nat. So here is the main definition: a positive poly number is a list of positive numbers, in other words, of natural numbers, written vertically with a bracket on top, just like this. All right, so it's a list of natural numbers with a bracket on top, and that is a positive poly number. These entries are all natural numbers. There was a little bit of ambiguity or flexibility in the way we specify a poly number in the sense that we can add additional zeros at the end, and we agree that this does not change the poly number. So, for example, the poly number (3, 1, 4) is the same as the poly number (3, 1, 4, 0). All right, so having defined those things, we can then do some arithmetic with them, and addition is very straightforward. Basically, the addition is just a component-wise addition where, if necessary, you add enough zeros to the shorter one so that we have numbers to add all the way down. A lot more subtle was the notion of multiplication of polynomials, given by this relatively complicated formula. So if P is, say, a poly number consisting of entries Pᵢ, and Q is the poly number consisting of entries Qᵢ, and we're indexing from zero—so P₀ on top, then P₁, then P₂, and so on—then their product P times Q is that poly number R given by R₀, R₁, R₂, and so on, where the Rₙs are given by the following formula: Rₙ is obtained by taking the sum over all k and l, natural numbers that add up to n, and the thing that we're actually summing is the product of Pₖ with Qₗ. So we take all possible products of things in the list so that the two indices sum up to the index that we're interested in over here. So, for example, if we want to multiply (2, 3) by (1, 5, 4), then we get this: (2, 13, 23, 12). The way we get that is 2 times 1 is 2—that's the zero level. Then at the one level, we get that by multiplying a zero with a one or a one with a zero, so 2 plus 11 is 13. At level 2, we get that by maybe choosing a zero and a two, taking that product, or a one and a one, that product, or a two and a one at that product—that last one would be 0—so we'd get 8 plus 15 is 23. And the third one would be taking the one position here and the two position and multiplying them to give us 12. Okay, so this is a non-obvious kind of multiplication, but of course, once we recognize that these are just ordinary polynomials in disguise, then this is just the usual product of polynomials rewritten in this language. And then we saw that this particular poly number (0, 1) plays a kind of special role—we gave it the name alpha—and that allowed us then to write a general poly number, say something like (1, 3, 2), in terms of powers of alpha, so as 1 plus 3 alpha plus alpha squared, where the one represents alpha to the zero, so representing that top thing there. So this is the alpha, and this is the alpha squared, and alpha cubed and so on would be just further on down here. So here's how we then recover the usual polynomial aspect of things: our poly number on this side doesn't have any variables in it. Over here, we recognize that's a familiar kind of polynomial object, but it has this variable alpha in it. And what we want to do is we want to avoid having to say what precisely our variable is—that's a little bit of a tricky thing in mathematics. What is the variable x? What's the difference between the variable x and the variable y or the variable t? So defining precisely what a variable is, is problematic, and we can get around that by just playing this game of really recognizing that it's the coefficients that are the crucial thing in specifying the poly number or the polynomial, and then we can actually recover the variable—or at least one variable—by this particular choice of sort of a standard poly number. Now, the other main ingredient that I want to bring into the story here are these vexels that we were talking about in the context of maxels and linear algebra and matrices. So a vexel was a kind of generalization of a vector—a somewhat larger concept, a somewhat more open-ended concept—and it arose from a prior understanding of a term called a singleton. So the definition of a singleton is that it's a one-list from natural numbers—this was laid out, say, in Math Foundations 171—and I remind you that for us now, nat when we say natural numbers, does include zero. Okay, so we are actually extending what we did last time a little bit in that, well, we're now considering natural numbers as being slightly bigger than it was before. So that's what a singleton is—it's just basically a one-list, that is, with one element, and that element has to be a natural number, zero, one, two, three, something like that. And then a vexel is defined to be an M-set of singletons—so an unordered collection of singletons where repetition is allowed. For example, this thing here—that is a vexel. There's a singleton, there's another one, there's another one, there's another one, there's another one, there's a few repeats there—the order doesn't matter, you can see that by the fact there's no commas. So that's a multiset of singletons—that is a vexel. And then we also talked about rather particular vexels, sort of like basis vexels, if you like, where now we're going to call them e₀, e₁, e₂. Before, we just considered e₁, e₂, and so on, but we're including zero now, so we have to enlarge our story a little bit. So e₀ is the particular vexel with just one singleton in it, namely the singleton which is the list containing zero. e₁ is the one vexel that has the singleton consisting of the list containing one, and e₂ is the vexel whose single singleton is the list containing two, and so on. Okay, so yes, that's singletons and vexels—a very nice kind of data structure orientation towards vectors. So now to connect these vexels, which are multisets, with the more standard idea of a vector as essentially a list, we played the following game: we had a look at a vexel, like say this one here, and we observed that the objects here are in some sense naturally ordered because the objects are essentially one-list from natural numbers, and natural numbers are naturally ordered—there's zero, and then there's one, and then there's two, and so on. So what we can do is we can specify this M-set by creating a multiplicity list for it. We've seen in other situations that it's a useful kind of thing to do—to have an M-set and then to represent it, give an alternate way of thinking about it, as a list that records how many of various things appear. So this thing here we could represent by this, well, kind of like a list or actually a row freeze kind of diagram: (2, 0, 3, 2), the two representing how many singleton zeros we have, the zero here representing how many singleton ones we have, how many singleton twos we have—there are three of them right there—and how many singleton threes we have—there are those two there. Or we could represent the same thing by a column kind of vector, by a column freeze display: (2, 0, 3, 2). So it's a matter of convention whether we would prefer to represent this thing with a row object or a column object. And we also note that the zeros at the end are kind of optional—we could also think about representing this by including a zero here representing that there's zero for singletons, maybe another zero representing zero for singletons, and so on. So you can see now there's an obvious connection between this and the poly number story, right? A poly number was essentially just exactly like this, right? So there must be some connection—there's obviously a connection between poly numbers and vexels, and that's interesting, I think, for us. And we just point out that vexels, because they're multisets, they can be added, because we can add any two multisets, so we just loop them all together. So if V and W are vexels, then we can form V + W. We can multiply V by 2, we can take a linear combination like 3V + 4W—these are all easily defined because V and W are vexels—they're multisets. So we gain a lot of infrastructure for free by having our objects be multisets. This is a kind of theme that's underlying a lot of what I've been doing and will be doing in the next, in the last few dozen videos. So now we want to move towards bringing in the third kind of objects that we're interested in, which are Hindu-Arabic numbers, and we want to kind of bring all of these three things together into the same pie—poly numbers, vexels, and Hindu-Arabic numbers. So it turns out that essentially the same objects are good for describing any three of those things—a natural synergy between these three things. So to move in that direction, we need to talk about how we're going to multiply vexels, and we're going to start doing that by explaining how to multiply singletons, because vexels are M-sets of singletons. If we know how to multiply singletons, hey, we know how to multiply M-sets of them automatically—in other words, we know how to multiply vexels. So the definition of the multiplication of singletons is rather surprising, perhaps, at least if you don't know where we're heading towards. Okay, so we have two singletons here, the singleton n, the singleton m, and n and m are necessarily natural numbers—zero, one, two, three, four, etc.—two of those things. And we're going to define the product of the singleton n times the singleton m to be the singleton n plus m, where this is just the usual addition of natural numbers. This is a surprising definition—we're defining a multiplication in terms of an underlying addition. Actually, it reminds us perhaps a little bit about the tropical—doesn't it? That kind of thing was going on there too. All right, so notice that singleton multiplication is automatically commutative and associative because the addition in natural numbers is. And now we simply extend this multiplication to a multiplication of vexels, because vexels are M-sets of singletons, so if we know how to multiply singletons, then we know how to multiply vexels automatically. So let me remind you about the general philosophy of multiplying M-sets: if here is some diagram for an M-set, and here's another M-set, and we happen to know how to multiply the objects in here by the objects in there, then here's how diagrammatically we multiply the M-sets: we just take all possible products between objects in here and objects in here. So we form a new M-set which consists of, in this case, A times D, A times E, B times D, B times E, C times D, C times E. So in this case, it was going to be three times two, six objects in this M-set. So that's a general principle that we do, no matter what, in terms of multiplying M-sets. So multiplying vexels is now straightforward. So here's a vexel V, here's another vexel W, we need to multiply them. The product is going to be defined in terms of the underlying products of the singletons contained in there, and we know that that product is determined essentially by the addition of the numbers inside the singletons. So when we multiply this singleton by this singleton—zero times one—the singleton zero times the singleton one, we get the singleton one because zero plus one is one. And similarly, this times this one is this, this times this is this, and then this times this is that, this times this is that, this times this is that, and this two times that one is another three—two times three, that'll be the five. And two times three is the five—whenever I want to say two times three is five, I don't really mean the number two times the number three is the number five. I rather mean the singleton two times the singleton three is the singleton five. Okay, and we can represent this by thinking about the corresponding pictures, descriptions of these vexels in terms of row or column freeze type vectors. So this thing here, this vexel, is described by (2, 0, 1)—there's two zeros, 0, 1, and 1, 2. This one here is described by (0, 1, 0, 2)—there are zero zeros, a one, one, zero twos, and two threes. And this one here is given by this thing here. And then if you think about it for a little while, you can convince yourself that this product here is in fact exactly the same as poly number multiplication—these things look like poly numbers. You have a green border here instead of a bracket up on top, but otherwise, they're just the same. And you can check that this is exactly the same as corresponding poly number multiplication. So this is another way of thinking about poly number multiplication—it's really generated by this very simple multiplication of singletons—it's really only that one equation involved. Okay, so we're seeing that there's a very close connection between vexels and poly numbers—they're almost the same thing. And here are some of the laws for the arithmetic with vexels that we can easily deduce from our definitions. And in fact, these laws are almost automatic now—there's not any serious difficulty in seeing why they're true. So if V, W, and U are vexels, and say N and M happen to be natural numbers, then we have commutativity of addition and associativity of addition, because the addition of vexels is basically just M-set addition, and we know that's commutative. We know that we can multiply, say, a sum V + W by some natural number N, and that's the same as N times V plus N times W. With regard to our multiplication, well, the commutativity and the associativity of the multiplication follows immediately from the commutativity and associativity of the multiplication at the singleton level, which is a consequence of the commutativity and associativity for natural number addition. We have some other laws having to do with how numbers multiply vexels, which are pretty straightforward. We have the important distributive laws, and these, again, are just natural consequences of the fact that we've defined our multiplication in an M-set kind of way, in a multiset orientation depending on an interior multiplication of elements. So whenever you have such a situation, these distributive laws are automatic. And it's useful to observe that we have a special vexel, the zero vexel, which is just the empty M-set, because vexels are M-sets, so there's an empty M-set, and that zero vexel satisfies that when you add it to anything, you get the anything, and when you multiply zero times V in either order, you get zero. So it sort of acts like zero does. So it's very pleasant that these laws of arithmetic are almost automatic here—the way we've set things up, they just fall nicely into place, and essentially no work is required to understand them, to prove them. And there's also a distinguished vexel, simple but distinguished vexel—this vexel (0, 1), which officially is the M-set consisting of just the singleton with one in it. And previously we called that α₁, but now we see that it plays exactly the same role as alpha in the context of poly numbers. So in other words, we can now introduce alpha in this vexel context by saying, let's agree that this particular thing is alpha. In other words, we're defining e₁ to be alpha. Well, then if you square it, you square this thing here—you have to add one plus one, you get the vexel which has a single singleton with a two, alpha cubed will be the singleton three, and so on. And so now we can write any vexel V in terms of essentially two equivalent forms—in the form as a linear combination of e₀, e₁, e₂, up to eₖ, as well as e₀, or correspondingly in terms of powers of alpha, alpha to the zero, alpha to the one, alpha to the two, and so on, out to say aₖ times alpha to the k, where we also introduced alpha to the zero to mean this e₀ vexel here. So we have a kind of linear algebra point of view on vexels represented by this, or we have a poly number or polynomial point of view towards vexels represented by this. And this is very nice to have these two different aspects of things sitting inside the same structure—that means we can do polynomial kinds of manipulations or thinking, and we can do linear algebra kinds of thinking in the same context, so that linear algebra and the calculus kind of can interact more freely and pleasantly in this kind of setup. So by introducing this multiplication of vexels, we are getting this convergence of fundamental mathematical objects—vectors and polynomials—they become essentially the same kinds of things. So this is a traditional vector, (3, 1, 2, 0, -5), and now we know that, well, we should really think about that probably in terms of a vexel rather than as a vector. And when we do that, then we naturally can associate it to the vexel written as 3 plus alpha plus 2 alpha squared plus 5 alpha to the four. So vector, polynomial—some kind of connection there. And we realize that both of these objects are just manifestations of the underlying object, which is neither a list nor a polynomial, but rather this underlying M-set. Okay, so the underlying M-set has three zeros, a single one, a couple of twos, no threes, and five fours, and it's really at this multiset level that the operations become simplest to define and the laws are easiest to verify. So mathematically, this is where things want to live. Okay, the interpretations of this object as a vector, or as a polynomial, or as a poly number, or as something else—maybe a column vector rather than a row vector, for example—these are interpretations that we bring to this underlying object, which is just a data structure, a multiset. In our next video, we want to now connect more closely with the Hindu-Arabic story. How do Hindu-Arabic numbers and the usual arithmetic that we're very familiar with slot into this situation? We'll explain that next time. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger, thanks for listening. 

---
