# 211 - Numbers as multipliers + particle⧸antiparticle duality II ｜ Data Structures Math Foundations 211 [gqjiDfiZjps].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. Today, we're gonna do something rather interesting. We're going to take this particle-antiparticle symmetry that comes from physics and extend it into mathematics. This is going to be essential for us to be able to use the multiset framework consistently across the board to set up linear algebra, field theory, and other things as well, including group theory later on. So, in some ways, what we're going to do now will appear like a game, and you can think about it that way. It's a kind of a technical device that we're going to introduce that allows us to make sense of things that we want to write down, but because we're obligated to be very precise about what things actually mean, we actually have to identify the objects that correspond to the things that we want to write down. So the story is motivated by this remarkable discovery of 20th-century physics, going back to essentially Dirac around 1930, that there was some kind of strange antiparticle for an electron, which subsequently was called a positron. And then eventually, physicists realized this was not some kind of isolated occurrence, but was rather a manifestation of some kind of general symmetry—that basically every elementary particle has an associated antiparticle with roughly the same mass but opposite charges. So not only do we have an electron and positron, but we have a proton and an antiproton, a neutron and antineutron, a neutrino and antineutrino, an up quark and an anti-up quark, and so on. Generally, the convention that the physicists like to use is that if the particle is denoted by a b, then the antiparticle is denoted with a b with a bar over it. So there's at least theoretically some kind of symmetry between these particles and antiparticles that was discovered by physicists, and now I think they have a kind of reasonable explanation for why this one goes across the board. That's very interesting, and we're going to take on board a little bit of that understanding from the 20th-century physicists. So to motivate this, let's recall that one of the important things that numbers do for us is that they allow us to scale existing data structures. That means that we can multiply, say, M-sets by numbers. So, for example, here is a rather general M-set. So I'm not being very specific about what objects are in here; these could be any kind of mathematical objects as long as we've already had a prior definition of what they are. We'll just represent them as some kind of objects, say heart, heart, diamond. And we want to take this M-set and we want to multiply it by two. So here is the number two represented by our two little marks, okay? And we know how to do that: we multiply each one of these marks by each one of these things. And the basic rule here is that, well, mark times a heart is a heart. So we have a heart, and then we offer another heart, and then we go to this one heart and another heart, and then a diamond and another diamond. And so that's the way we multiply this M-set by this number. And a special case is what happens when we multiply the same M-set by the number zero or the empty M-set. Well, in this case, there's a lot of products of the form emptiness or nothing times something, and nothing times something is nothing, so we get nothing on the right-hand side. So we get the empty set again. So this empty set is playing a very generic role here. It's really just the empty, I should say, empty multiset. It's the empty multiset, which can be a number, can be a natural number, it can be a negative number, it can be an integer. We can even think about it as being a type of M-set of pixels or Maxwells, and so on. So it's a rather general kind of thing that plays its role in many different situations. So now we know how to define n times A if n is a natural number and A is an M-set, and there's a pleasant algebra. Okay, now we want to ask this question, this natural extension: How do we multiply an M-set by a negative number? How does that work? Well, it all comes down to specifying what happens when we multiply some generic object by each of our three basic ingredients of numbers, namely the mark, emptiness, and the anti-mark. And we've already convinced ourselves that the mark times, say, heart ought to be heart, and that nothing times heart ought to be nothing. So now the question is, what should anti-mark times heart be? Let's temporarily call it heart-bar. So we're going to introduce possibly a new kind of object called heart-bar, which is the result of taking a little anti-mark and multiplying it by heart, okay? These three rules will then tell us how to multiply our basic elements with a general generic object, and that will then allow us to extend our multiplication of numbers times M-sets to a very general situation. We'll now be able to multiply any number, be it a negative number or a natural number, by an arbitrary M-set. Okay, so what about this new object that we're introducing? Well, it has to have a very special property. So we require that this new object, heart-bar, has the property that it cancels with heart—that heart together with heart-bar gives us nothing. And this is an equation that has to happen inside an M-set. So, you know, if we have an M-set and it contains a heart somewhere and it also contains a heart-bar or an anti-heart, then those two things are going to cancel, just the way a neutron and an antineutron cancel, and the result is nothing. So that's the property that this new object has to have, that it has to cancel with this one. And why is that? Well, the reason is the following: that if we take zero times an M-set, we should get zero or the empty M-set. And so if you take the empty M-set and we multiply, say, by the single M-set containing just a heart, whatever that might be, then we know we should get an empty M-set. But we also know that this empty M-set, or the number zero, is in some sense the same as the M-set consisting of a mark and an anti-mark together, because we know that that mark and the anti-mark cancel to give us emptiness or nothing. So what would happen if we consider this product here by the usual rule? So we just multiply the mark times a heart—there it is—and we multiply the anti-mark times a heart. Now we know that the mark times a heart is heart, and so, well, we're going to get this new M-set is heart, and the other one is anti-mark times heart. Now this supposedly is going to be the empty M-set, so for this to work, we require that this new object, anti-mark times heart, which is what we're calling heart-bar or anti-heart, that that object has the property, this cancellation property, that when we have a heart and this anti-heart together, they better cancel to give us the empty M-set. In other words, inside the M-set, we want this crucial cancellation that heart times heart-bar is nothing. So we're really proposing something that's quite dramatically new here, even though it's a little bit formal and technical and maybe so out of the vision most of the time. But nevertheless, theoretically, it really changes the landscape of mathematics. So I remind you that our basic premise is that we are going to build up mathematical objects from the bottom up, right? That's our basic orientation. We're starting at the bottom, not already assuming all kinds of stuff, and we're building things up step by step, one at a time, and going from simpler structures to more complicated structures to more complicated structures. And now what we're proposing is that we're going to have this general orientation that for any bona fide mathematical object, heart—so, some process in this building up, we get some object that we're very comfortable with, let's call it heart—okay, that we're going to propose that there is another object, kind of a mirror object, actually an anti-object that corresponds to that, that object, which is its anti-object. In other words, there's going to be a new object, heart-bar, which we can define as the anti-mark times the heart that we're considering, that has this crucial cancellation property that we've seen several times. So we want this to be true across the board in the same way as the particle-antiparticle duality works across the board for elementary physics. It's not just electron and positron or proton and antiproton. This is a general kind of symmetry that's going to apply to any kind of legitimate mathematical object that we're able to construct. And some definition or terminology: So, let's say if the object that we're considering happens to be the object called heart, then we'll call this thing here anti-heart and we'll say that anti-heart is the anti of heart. So I'm introducing this usage of this word in a slightly different way. So this is supposed to be similar to the use of the word opposite—the opposite of black is white, the opposite of day is night. Now we're going to talk about the anti of a mathematical object, heart, as being this thing here, anti-heart, or we'll say heart-bar when we're sort of talking about how we write it down. So the anti of a mark is the anti-mark. The anti of the anti-mark—how do we get that? Well, the definition is always the same: the anti of something is what you get when you take the anti-mark and multiply it by that something. In this case, we know what we get. We take the anti-mark and we multiply it by the anti-mark. Anti-mark times anti-mark—we've decided already that that's going to be the mark. So this crucial property that the anti of the anti-mark is just the mark. So I do admit that this takes some getting used to, may take a little while for this to sort of sink into our consciousness, but it's pretty interesting, and we'll see that it's really essential to be able to do the kinds of things that we want to do. So what is this anti of something? Is the anti of a number just its negative? No, it's not, okay? The anti of a number is not its negative. The anti of a number is a new kind of object. So, for example, here is the number that we usually call four. It's an M-set with mark, mark, mark, mark in it. It's a natural number. So the anti of this thing, what is it? Well, this is a mathematical object, and its anti is what you get when you multiply it by anti-mark. And by definition, that's just a new object that has a bar over it, okay? So, as if we really do anything, we just take that object and we put a bar over it, and then it's some new object, the anti of what we had previously. So there's the object here, and notice that the bar goes right over all of it. So we're taking the bar over the whole thing, and if we were going to write this as a hint of Arabic numeral four, then we would just write four-bar. And what kind of a new thing is this? Well, it makes sense to say that the type of this thing is anti-net. This is a net, so this is an anti-net. And what property does this have? Well, if we take an M-set consisting of this and four-bar, there's four, and there's four-bar or anti-four, then those two things are going to cancel. That's the basic cancellation, and I'm going to get the empty set or zero. On the other hand, minus four, okay, minus four also involves anti-things, but minus four was the M-set consisting of four anti-marks—anti-mark, anti-mark, anti-mark, anti-mark. That's a negative number. That's actually quite different. So this is different from this. If you like, here the bar extends over the whole thing, and here we're just having the individual elements inside the M-set being barred. So this is not equal to this; they're different. So, for example, if we make an M-set containing the number four and the number minus four, then we're not going to get zero; we're not going to get the empty M-set because these two things don't cancel themselves out as objects. This is now a new M-set which has two elements in it. One of the elements is four, and the other element is minus four, and that's just what it is. It doesn't simplify to anything simpler. But if you have four and anti-four, you are obliged to cancel those object pairs and simplify. So let me give you some motivation for why we want to do this. Anti-objects allow us to introduce negative numbers into vexels and maxels. Here, for example, is a vexel. It is an M-set consisting of singletons: the singleton two, the singleton zero, the singleton minus one, the singleton one, the singleton one. And so these are singletons from integers. That means they are lists whose elements are integers—just a list with one element. And we know that we can represent this kind of thing in a sort of vector form by recording the number of zeros we have, the number of ones we have, and the number of minus ones we have. We can write this down in a freeze kind of fashion like that, with this convention that the zero spot is identified by some bar, so we know where it's sort of centered. Here's another kind of vexel, but now this is a new kind of object because it has not just singletons, but also some anti-singletons. So let's try to get our heads around this, okay? What do we see in this M-set? Well, how about zero singletons? How many zero singletons are there here? Well, there is not really ten singletons; there's minus one singleton because this anti-singleton zero counts as minus one of them. That's what we're going to do; we're going to interpret this anti-object as minus one of the original object. So we see that there's one anti-singleton zero here, so we're going to record that by putting a minus one in the zero spot. In terms of minus one singletons, there's two of them, so we'll put a two in the minus one spot. And also, in the two spot, up here is a singleton two, only one of them, so we'll put a one there. But in the singleton one spot, we have singleton one-bar, singleton one-bar—that corresponds to minus two singleton ones. So we would put a minus two there. So let me try to convince you that this actually makes sense, that this is consistent, this is logical. Because suppose you wanted to add these two vectors, okay? These are M-sets, so we know how to add M-sets. We just put them together. So V + W, so we take all of these elements, just put them in there, and then we add all of these elements, starting from here. There we go. Okay, now we have this big M-set. And now we look at it and say, "Well, wait a minute, there is a singleton zero here and there is its anti-object." Like, there's the anti of this. So this thing and this thing cancel—poof, they're gone, replaced with nothing. And now there's only this singleton zero left. How about minus ones? Well, there's one minus one singleton there, and there's another one there, another one there, so altogether there's three of them. How about the one singletons? So there's one here, one here, and one here, but here there's an anti on one of these, and here there's another anti on one of these. So this anti cancels with one of these; we don't really care which one—this one here—they cancel. This one cancels with another one—say, that one there—and we're left with a single singleton one. And the singleton two is alone, finds over there, it's just there. And then when we count what we have left over, well, we have one singleton zero, we have three singleton minus ones, and we have singleton one and a singleton two. And we see that this is indeed what we expect in terms of component-wise addition of these vectors over here. So there's a zero spot, so two plus minus one is indeed one. One plus two is three, three plus minus two is one, zero plus one is one. So these minus ones here are representing these anti-objects, and they add to the original objects in the right way; namely, they cancel with them in the multiset, so we get the correct additive structure on the vector side of things. The price we pay is that in order to have minus ones, where we want to interpret those minus ones as multiplicities, we're going to interpret them as being multiplicities of anti-objects. I guess it goes back to, you know, the question, what is minus three cows? What does it mean to take four cows and add minus three cows? Well, it's hard to do that physically. You have to imagine a minus cow, some kind of fictitious object representing some debt. It's not really the same as a tangible physical cow, but it's a debt, it's some kind of virtual object. That's sort of what we're doing here; we're being more precise about these virtual objects and giving them names and these bars over them. Basically, we're doing the same kind of thing: four cows plus minus three cows gives us one cow, four objects plus three anti-objects give us one object of that particular kind. So here's another example of how we can use this concept for something that's very important for us, which is to develop maxel theory in a kind of a very good arithmetic way. So here's an example of a new kind of maxel. It's got pixels in it, but some of the pixels are kind of anti-pixels instead of regular pixels. So we have minus one, two, and there's a couple of them. We have a zero, one, but that's an anti-object at zero, two of them. And we have three of these anti-one, one pixels, okay? So this is a maxel with both pixels and what we might call anti-pixels. Obvious what we mean by that, right? That's an anti-pixel, that's a pixel, okay? And we can represent this thing now, and here's our integer screen, a representation where we have our lines like this. There prominently in the middle is our zero, zero spot. That's the basic reference frame, okay? So we would put multiplicities here. So minus one, two—that's minus one, two. So that two there represents these two fellows here. This zero, one—so the zero, one is that position, but we don't have one of them; we have basically minus one of them because there's an anti-zero, one pixel there, not a regular zero, one pixel. So we put a minus one there; that minus one is a representation of the fact that we have an anti-pixel there. We have two of these pixels, the zero, two pixels, so we put two there, and then we have three of these anti-pixels, so we put a minus three in the one, one position. So this is a representation of this M-set which contains both pixels and anti-pixels now. And it's very important for us to be able to do this because we want to do arithmetic with maxels, but we for sure want to have integers in the maxel positions. So we basically want to do standard linear algebra; we don't want to just restrict ourselves to natural number entries in all our matrices and maxels, because we are always thinking in terms of them as being M-sets, and if they were M-sets, then the multiplicities would always have to be positive. So we want to free ourselves from that. That's exactly what we've done. We've enlarged our point of view, admittedly by introducing these strange new anti-objects, but now we're in a position to interpret very precisely what something like this means in a multiset framework. So these numbers are still representing multiplicities of objects. Now, in certain areas of mathematics, somewhat more sophisticated mathematics, negative numbers of objects are introduced by what are sometimes referred to as formal linear combinations. So, for example, in group theory, maybe you have a group, a finite group, and it has elements g1, g2, g3, g4, and so on. In some situations, it's useful to consider linear combinations of these things. For example, we like to do arithmetic with combinations like 3g1 - 2g2 + 2g3, and it's a legitimate question to ask, what exactly are we talking about here? And in traditional algebra, there's a little bit of dodging and weaving when it comes to saying exactly how one sets up such formal combinations. One usually does something like this as well: we're considering some formal combination, and so on. So what we're doing here is trying to be more precise about what's really going on when we consider a formal object like this. What we're proposing here is that this thing here is really probably living in a multiset framework, okay? We're talking about a multiset, really, that's what this represents, where there are three g1's, a single g3, and two anti-g2's. If we have a notion of an anti-object, that makes perfect sense, and then we have a consistent multiset interpretation of this kind of formal combination that's going to be useful in quite a lot of contexts. So this object-anti-object setup is an alternative to some more loose kind of discussion that one often finds in advanced algebra courses. Now, a curious aspect of the 20th-century physicists' understanding of particle-antiparticles is that although there's this theoretical symmetry, practically, if you look out there in the real world, you do not see a symmetry between particles and antiparticles. Pretty well everything in our ordinary world consists of just particles, and the antiparticles of things in our world are very hard to find. That's why they weren't observed until 1930. So there's definitely an asymmetry with regard to the prevalence of particles and antiparticles, at least in this neck of the woods where we are here in our universe. Okay, so similarly, we're going to find that we're going to use anti-objects rather minimally. It's not as if now, suddenly, our mathematics is going to be populated by all kinds of anti-things. Rather, this is a sort of technical device that allows us to justify putting negative numbers in lists that are multiplicity lists for M-sets. So it's a way of still doing our arithmetic but in a more general way, you know, in a kind of formally correct way. Because we're going to be rather general about being able to take an anti of something, it might be that the objects like heart that we're considering might be actually quite long objects. We could be considering an M-set with three thousand things, and we might want to take its anti. So we will use this notation that anti-mark times heart is heart-bar, with the bar right over the heart. But sometimes we will also allow ourselves this alternate usage where we put the little bar to the right, almost like an index, okay? So imagine if this is some big M-set that's very long; we'll just stick the bar at the end, sort of over at the end of the final bracket, just so we don't have to write some big overline. So, for example, here, this is an M-set. It's actually a maxel, it's got three pixels. So it's possible for us to consider the anti of a maxel, and instead of writing this, we could alternately just write the maxel with a prominent bar at the end on the right as an index there. So that means the anti of this maxel. So in our next video, I do want to say a little bit more about this anti operation. It's obviously a kind of a novel and interesting thing, and there's a few more things to say about it. And then after that, we'll get on to some very interesting applications to setting up field theory and higher arithmetic, really from the maxel point of view. But next time, a little bit more about this curious anti operation that we've now introduced into mathematics. Hope you'll join me for that. I'm going Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening.

# 212 - The anti operation in mathematics ｜ Data structures in Mathematics Math Foundations 212 [3oxOHu_QwEc].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In this video, we want to talk about this fundamental symmetry that we've introduced into mathematics. So the usual world of mathematical objects is now augmented by a kind of alternate mirrored world of anti-objects. And formally, this is done very simply by just putting bars over objects to denote the anti of an object. This is going to be crucial for us in allowing us to use the multiset framework to introduce negative multiplicities in a real sense, in a concrete sense, rather than just formally and just sort of waving our hands. So we actually have objects that represent minus three cows, or more typically, minus three pixels or minus three singletons or minus three of some particular mathematical object. It's going to be very important for us when we want to do abstract algebra and look at spaces, linear spaces, groups, rings, fields in our next video. Okay, so the basic idea is: here are our familiar kinds of objects that we might want to use in mathematics, and here are the corresponding anti-objects, which are just created by taking these objects and putting bars over them. We're just following the physicists' usage here. So here's a number three; here's anti-three or three-bar, and we just take the actual object and put a bar over the whole thing. And if we want to save space, or it's a little bit hard for us to put the bar over it, we'll put the bar on the right-hand upper corner. Here's our minus four number; here's the anti of that minus four-bar; just take the object itself, which is actually a bunch of anti-marks, and put a bar over it. Here's a vexel, which is an M-set of singletons; here's the anti V-bar. Here's an ordered set S; it's got some different elements, it's ordered because we see some commas. Here is the corresponding anti-object; we just take the whole thing and put a bar over it. We don't modify it in any way; we just put a bar over the whole thing. Here's a maxel consisting of a bunch of pixels; here's the anti of that maxel, M-bar; just take the whole thing and put a bar over it. So we have this fundamental symmetry, which is really officially introduced by this little equation that anti-mark times some object, heart, whatever that might be, as long as it's an actual specific object that we've already nailed down, then this product is going to be what we call heart-bar or the anti-heart. And the crucial defining property of this anti-object is that if it and the original object reside in an M-set together, then they cancel. That means they both disappear and they're replaced with nothing. So this is not really a multiplication of heart times heart-bar; this is just to denote that if we have a heart and an anti-heart together in the same M-set, then they are going to cancel. And notice that it's crucial for us that we do have a notion of nothing as being represented by a blank part of our page here to express what happens when we combine these two things. So in today's video, we want to talk a little bit more about this symmetry, get a little bit more experience with it, and then move to seeing how it actually helps us in setting up a wider theory of vexels and maxels. Now, to get a full symmetry between objects and anti-objects, we're going to want the anti of an anti-object to be the original object. So we're going to introduce that as a definition. We're going to say that when we take the anti of an anti, we get the original. In other words, if we take anti-mark times heart-bar, which is the anti of the anti-heart—there's the heart, there's the anti of it, and there's the anti of the anti—we want that to be the same as the original object, heart. So we're going to introduce this as a definition, and this is really consistent with our understanding that anti-mark times anti-mark is mark, because this thing here is anti-mark times heart-bar, and this heart-bar is anti-mark times heart. And what we're doing is we're saying let's agree that this thing is the same as this, and we're allowed to use associativity at this level to rewrite this as anti-mark times anti-mark times heart, in which case we would get mark times heart, which we know is heart. So the equality of this and this is really equivalent to this associativity that we're allowing ourselves at the level of multiplication of anti-marks with anti-marks with objects. Okay, and now we can introduce the idea of the negative or negation of an M-set. This is a very powerful thing to be able to do. Okay, so we are now allowed to talk about an M-set and its negative. How do we do that? Well, here's the definition in a kind of abstract example. So if here is an M-set, say heart, heart, diamond, and these are some kind of mathematical objects that we have agreed that we've already clearly defined, then the negative of this M-set, which is what we get when we multiply by minus one—here is the number minus one—then the way we multiply M-sets is what we should do is we should take the entries of this M-set and the entries of this M-set and take all possible products. So anti-mark times heart—well, that's heart-bar; anti-mark times this heart—that's heart-bar; anti-mark times this diamond—that's diamond-bar. So the effect of multiplying by minus one is to take the anti of each of the original elements of the M-set, go from M-set M to the M-set of its anti, and that is the negative of the original M-set. So negating an M-set means taking the anti of all its entries. So we don't have to do this now in a formal sense. Really, we have a rather specific idea and framework for talking about combinations of M-sets, including negative combinations. All right, so we can do things like now not just add the M-set A plus the M-set B; we can also take the M-set A minus the M-set B, and we have a meaning to what that means. So here are some exercises for you to ponder to practice your understanding of how these anti-objects interact with ordinary objects. So, try to simplify these expressions if you can; it might not be possible. So some of these expressions might not actually make sense, so you have to try to decide which ones do. So here, for example, is a what's actually a singleton because it has a singleton one in it, and here is another kind of object, which has a singleton one-bar in it, and we want to add those. Does that make sense, and if so, what do we get? Here we have number two singleton two, and here we have the anti of the singleton two, which is subtly different from this situation here. What happens there? Here is the anti of M-set with inside a three-bar. Here we have this M-set plus this M-set. Here we have the negation of this M-set, where this one here contains a singleton four and the anti of the singleton five. Here we're adding the anti of the singleton six and the anti of the singleton seven. Here we have an M-set consisting of numbers zero, zero, one, and here's an M-set consisting of integers one, minus two, and also the anti. And here's a difference of two M-sets. So maybe we can also introduce some notational convention. If we have to write this anti kind of object in a typing kind of situation, perhaps we could introduce the idea that, say, the pixel thirteen anti or thirteen bar, we could write it perhaps like this: anti of pixel thirteen. That's pretty unambiguous. Or maybe we could write pixel thirteen and then put a bar behind it, meaning this object barred. Okay, so we could use bar or anti, possibly, to convey this in a typed situation. So here are some little things to think about. So one of the main things that we want to do with these anti-objects is allow us to be a little bit more flexible about the use of M-sets. We want to enlarge our notion of M-set now to include, as we've seen, not just objects but also anti-objects. And so we want to introduce a bit of terminology that captures that idea. So let's talk about an integral M-set or an M-set. Okay, so this is some kind of variant or slight generalization of an M-set. And what is an integral M-set? Well, suppose that we have a notion of M-set of some type, so we've decided on M-sets whose elements are of a certain type, and we have a very clear idea of what we're talking about in that context. Then we'll consider IM-sets of that type to be, well, a more general kind of multiset that allows not just the objects of the type that we're considering but also their antis, all right? So that objects of the type we're considering, as well as anti-objects of that type. So when we allow both objects and anti-objects of a given type, we'll talk about that as being an integral M-set, or short, an IM-set of that type. So here's a very important illustration of that; it's going to play a big role for us: the idea of an integral vexel. So we've already talked about vexels in the context of M-sets of singletons from, say, Nat, where, for example, here is a vexel which has some singletons, and the singletons are from natural numbers. There's a singleton zero, the singleton one, singleton one, singleton three—that's a vexel. And we've seen that we can represent that vexel geometrically or visually with this freeze-like object, where we record the number of zero singletons in the special little box at the beginning—there's one of them—and then record the number of one singletons, the number of two singletons (there aren't any), the number of three singletons, and this is an open-ended kind of thing; the box doesn't really end—it's possible for there to be further additions. And then we saw that we could extend that idea once we have integers as well as natural numbers; we can think about using the integers to specify an extended labeling structure. All right, so we had vexel from integers; these are M-sets of singletons from int. That means something like this, where we have singletons whose entries are not just natural numbers but also possibly negative numbers, so over the full range of integers. And representing something like this involves a similar kind of thing, but now we're allowed to introduce multiplicities of the negatively labeled singletons. So here we have a singleton minus two—there's one of them, so we put a one in the minus two spot. There's two minus one singletons, so we put a two in the minus one spot. There's Z—Z singleton, so the special box has a zero. There's zero one singleton, and there's a single two singleton. So we've used integers here crucially in extending our vexel or vector point of view from a one-sided story to a two-sided story; that was important. And now we're going another step up. Okay, so now we want to consider integer vexels or integral vexels, and let's do that in two stages. So first of all, from Nat, where we consider IM-sets of singletons from Nat, means we're back in this situation here, but now we're allowed to use not just singletons but also anti-singletons. Okay, so here we have a singleton zero, a singleton zero, and here's an anti-singleton one and a singleton two. The two singleton zeros are represented by this two in the zero-labeled box. The anti-singleton one is represented by this minus one, right? So this minus one now is telling us that instead of having one singleton one, we have minus one singleton ones, which is the same for us as having a single anti-singleton one. So an anti-singleton one is playing the role of minus one singleton ones, and that singleton two is represented by that one there. So the novelty now is that we're allowed to put integers here, including negative numbers, and the negative numbers that appear here are just a reflection of the existence of anti-singletons in the actual vexel that we're considering. And here's the more general thing, an integral vexel from integers. Here we have a vexel which contains singletons both with natural number entries and also with negative number entries, but we also have anti-singletons. So in this case, what do we have? We have minus one singleton with an anti and minus one singleton with the anti, so there's minus two things altogether in the minus one box. There's a zero box, there's the minus one box, there's minus two minus one singletons, there's a zero zero singleton, there's a single one singleton, and in terms of singleton twos, there's minus one of them because there's a bar over here. So here we're using integers in two distinctly different ways: we're using integers, first of all, to allow ourselves to index an entire two-sided array of positions—position zero, position one, position two, as well as position minus one, position minus two, position minus three, and so on. But we're also using integers to represent negative multiplicities, like negative cows. Negative three cows—what does negative three cows mean? Well, we now know, in the mathematical context at least, what it means; it's like there's three anti-cows in our M-set. Of course, we're not dealing with cows; we're dealing with lists and sets and multisets and ordered sets, these data type structures. And for us, the anti-object is just what you get by formally putting a bar over the object. So to summarize, we've now identified a new framework for vectors, integral vectors from integers. So these are IM-sets of singletons from int. This is going to be our preferred overarching domain for doing vector arithmetic—in other words, for doing linear algebra, in fact, for doing geometry. It's a very nice framework, but now we know exactly what we're talking about. At some point, we may actually go a little bit further and up this a little bit to not just having integer entries in our vexels—in other words, when we write down a vector, not just having integer entries, but also having rational number entries, so once we get to the stage of having an official version of rational numbers. But currently, this is a very powerful framework. So typically, what we're talking about is something that looks like this when we represent it geometrically: there's a freeze, it's two-sided, going to the left and right; there's the special zero position, and the actual entries now are integers representing multiplicities of singletons, where the plus signs would correspond to having honest singletons, and the negative signs would correspond to having anti-singletons. And necessarily, these things are finite, because we are, I remind you, always obliged to write things down fully. When we're talking about things precisely and completely, we have to illustrate our concepts by writing things down completely and precisely, so no going off to infinity at this stage. Okay, so an actual object of this has to be written down entry by entry by entry, all of it there. And it's really a short form for a corresponding M-set or IM-set. So these are two-sided, open-ended vectors with integral entries, and they correspond to IM-sets of singletons from int. We're going to say this is a lovely framework for thinking about geometry and linear algebra in a very big way. And another main example for us is integral maxels. So we started off talking about maxels as M-sets of pixels from natural numbers, and then we enlarged that to consider maxels as M-sets of pixels from integers. For example, here's a maxel with these three pixels: zero, minus one; zero, minus one; one, two. And that corresponds to this geometric grid kind of view, where this is the zero zero position. It's the one zero, two zero, three zero over here; zero zero, zero one, zero two, zero three; but now also zero minus one, zero minus two. So this two here is representing the fact that we have two zero minus one pixels, and there's a single one two pixel. Okay, now we want to extend this by going to integral maxels. First of all, integral maxels—these would be IM-sets of pixels from natural numbers, just the same kind of sort of limited freeze that just works with a quadrant. But more interestingly, integral maxels from int. So these are M-sets of pixels from integers. So these are just like these ones, except now we're also allowed the possibility of having anti-pixels as well as pixels. So here's N, which looks quite a bit like this one, except that instead of having these two pixels, it has their corresponding anti-pixels. So instead of having a two in this position, in this zero minus one position, we have a minus two, because there's these two anti-pixels, and we still have a single honest pixel one two represented by that one there. So now we're in a position to deal with matrices with integer values, and matrices which are very general—that they have entries covering the entire plane. It's a very general, beautiful framework for doing matrix theory and linear algebra and other things as well, as we'll see. Okay, so we are now in a great position because these integral points of view, these integral M-sets that we now have at our disposal, allow us entry to a lot of abstract algebra. So we're able to now, in a more precise fashion than we currently have, start talking about abstract algebra notions, such as groups, rings, fields, vector spaces, modules, and other things, in a more precise, careful, concrete manner. In the next video, I want to start that by giving an overview of abstract algebra, a bird's-eye picture of abstract algebra. It's a relatively modern mathematical development, roughly 100 years old, and which is now very, very extensive. But I want to give you an introduction to this, and then we'll be in a good position to start using this concrete, precise way of thinking about data structures to revitalize many of those topics. Very exciting. So I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for [Music] listening

# 213 - An introduction to abstract algebra ｜ Abstract Algebra Math Foundations 213 ｜ NJ Wildberger [-Z9XL4B0ROk].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger In today's video, we are going to move towards understanding abstract algebra, a large and lovely area of mathematics with lots of applications, especially 20th-century developments. We're going to look at laying the foundations for this subject in, of course, a better, more solid, more tangible, and computable way than we currently have. We're going to be relying very much on our data structure point of view and the lessons that we have learned about just how to set up fundamental arithmetic and a little bit of maxels and vexels. Okay, so what is abstract algebra? Well, it's not that easy to say. It's a kind of an extension of arithmetic or algebra to more general situations, and these general situations include lots of things which have perhaps some fancy names. So we're talking about groups, rings, fields, vector spaces, algebras, lattices, modules, Lie algebras, hypergroups, and one could fill the page with more objects that are studied in modern abstract algebra. Roughly speaking, this list sort of corresponds to the order in which an undergraduate might learn about these various topics, but there's quite a bit of variability possible. Okay, so the common features of most of these situations are that we have some basic algebraic objects, usually one or two algebraic objects, and they're often called addition or multiplication or variants of them, but not always. Another common feature is that we're generally generalizing common or familiar objects or arithmetical situations. So the idea is that we have some particular domain of investigation in which we have some arithmetical operations, and we look at the essential features of that situation and try to extract the skeletal underpinnings of that situation in order to generalize. So we start with a particular object, and then we want to generalize to include a wider family of objects that share a lot of the properties of the main one that we started with. And the idea is then to, well, try to say things in a way that covers this wider range of objects, even though maybe we're still focusing our intuition on the fundamental examples. Okay, so what do we do in abstract algebra? Well, one of the kinds of things that we do is we are interested in structure. We're interested in determining what these objects look like, and we're interested in classifying them to create lists or a taxonomy of various things that satisfy the framework that we have generalized. And once we start doing abstract algebra quite a lot, we start to see that there are certain patterns of thinking, certain standard kinds of constructions or ideas that end up recurring, including, for example, the idea of substructure. We identify some particular kind of structure, and then we look inside those structures for other structures that are basically similar to the original one. We think about product or sum structures. We've got some kind of object, maybe a group, and over here is another group. We think about how we can combine those groups, maybe add them or multiply them to create some bigger group. We look at quotient structures—that's a little bit more subtle. So that's what happens when you have one object of a certain kind, and then inside you have some other kind of object of the same kind, but it's definitely inside. And then the idea of sort of taking a quotient, like dividing the big one by the little one, to get another, a third kind of object of the same kind. So investigating when that's possible, that's an interesting kind of question. A very general kind of consideration is the idea of understanding relationships between two objects, like let's say we're looking at lattices. So here's a lattice, there's a lattice. We're interested in what kind of relations there are between these lattices, and this is often framed in terms of the word homomorphism, which we think of as a mapping or a function or a transformation that takes the elements of one lattice to the other lattice but preserves the lattice properties. So whatever a lattice is, it has certain properties, and we want our relations and homomorphisms to preserve or to respect the various arithmetical or algebraic structures that we have. So these are some of the common features that figure in pretty well the study of any one of these kinds of abstract algebraic structures. So today, we want to give a very brief kind of overview of some of these kinds of objects in a kind of cursory overview but with a traditional point of view. All right, so this is following sort of standard thinking, and then later on, we want to move towards a better, new, improved way of thinking about these same things. But let's start with a somewhat more familiar look at them. Now, it's often considered that group theory is the natural entrance to abstract algebra because it's an algebraic situation where you only have one operation, and the operation is sometimes called addition and sometimes called multiplication. So I'm not going to, in this lecture, concentrate on the official definitions of things. I'm going to rather give you a brief orientation by showing you some examples. Now, we actually have talked about groups a number of times already in this series. You can have a look through the series for some examples, for example, when we were talking about the projective line, projective plane, and isometries. So say the projective line, okay? So another example of a group that we've talked about is these U-groups. So for example, U10, and U10 was the integers less than 10 that are relatively prime to 10. In other words, integers that don't have any factors in common with 10. So that would be 1, 3, 7, and 9. And we saw that we could make an algebraic structure out of just those four objects by doing multiplication mod 10. All right, so here's the multiplication table that we considered. One is the identity, so 1 * anything is that anything, and say 3 * 7. Well, that's 21, but if we're working mod 10, 21 is the same as 1, so 3 * 7 is 1. And this is a commutative group, reflected in the symmetry of the multiplication table, that the 9 * 3 entry is the same as the 3 * 9 entry, so that entry and that entry are the same. And that's a very special and pleasant kind of group. It's much simpler than the non-commutative groups, and so the basic properties of a group are that the multiplication should be associative, that there should be an identity element (in this case, one), and that every element should have an inverse. Basically, it means that along every row or every column, you can find a one entry. So those are commutative groups, and they are quite a pleasant thing to study, and they come up in practice, for example, here, okay? But in other situations too. And then there are non-commutative groups, which have the same underlying rules, except that the multiplication of two elements doesn't need to be commutative. So a * b does not have to be equal to b * a. And we've seen some examples of that, for example, the group S3, which is the group of permutations of three things. We can label those by thinking about the ordered sets which have 1, 2, and 3 as their elements. So these are like ordered sets or permutations. These are the six possible ways of rearranging three objects, and one interpretation of this group S3—it's the symmetric group on three things—is as the transformations of a triangle. So if we here have a triangle with positions 1, 2, and 3, then, for example, we can think about the transformation that takes this triangle and rotates it by a third of a turn in this direction. Okay, so we take this triangle here to this position here. Let's call that transformation sigma, okay? Then that transformation can be recorded. One way is to say, well, the one object goes to the two position, the two object goes to the three position, and the three object goes to the one position. And we could record that by having a little table like this: here are the initial objects, and here are the new positions that they go to. So the object one goes to the position two, the object two goes to the position three, the object three goes to the position one. And once we have a little table like that, we can also say, well, why don't we just record the last row, the bottom row, because the first row is always 1, 2, 3, and so we can associate this thing with the ordered set 2, 3, 1. So that would be one way of identifying these permutations or transformations of a triangle with these ordered sets. And in this context, the multiplication is that of combining or composing transformations. So you do one transformation, and then you do another transformation after that one, and that's called composing the transformations, and that gives an algebraic structure to our permutations. That's a non-commutative group. So that's a very interesting family of algebraic objects—very beautiful, very rich theory. The non-commutative situation is typically a lot more advanced and richer and more complicated than the commutative theory. This division between commutative and non-commutative kinds of abstract algebra is a central one in the subject in terms of organizing the subject. And that also holds with our next example, the example of rings. Okay, so let's start with an example of a commutative ring, and there's a very familiar example: the example of integers or int. So now we have two operations: addition and multiplication, and it's a question of studying not just those operations in themselves but also how they interact. So, for example, the addition operation is commutative and associative, and also the multiplication is commutative and associative. It's the multiplication being commutative that makes the ring be called commutative. The addition always has to be commutative. Now, in addition, the two operations interact via the distributive law that's very familiar to us. And there's also some extra kinds of things. There's a zero element which plays an identity element in terms of addition. There's a one element which plays an identity role in terms of multiplication. And then there's a notion of inverse, closely related to the minus one object, that allows us to start with an element six and obtain the inverse of six, namely such that when we add them, we get the additive identity. So that's an example of a commutative ring. And generally, we like to have more than one example of an algebraic structure. If we don't have more than one example, then it's not really important enough to think about generalizing. So we want to have something where we have a couple of different situations that all have some common features, and we can say, ah, there's some common things going on here in these different situations; let's abstract those common features and study this thing abstractly. Okay, so aside from the integers, another important commutative ring is the ring of polynomials or, for us, poly numbers, which we might denote by poly int. Okay, so we're talking about things that look like this in the poly number language. So we have combinations of numbers, alphas, alpha squares, alpha cubes, and so on. These are familiar ways of writing things down which actually officially are more like column vectors. And we know that we can add two such polynomials or poly numbers basically pointwise, so 2 and 3 give us 5, the alpha minus 4 alpha give us minus 3 alpha, and the alpha squared was there. But we can also multiply them in the kind of distributed way so that 2 minus alpha times 1 plus 4 alpha would be 2 and then plus 8 alpha minus alpha is a total of 7 alpha, and minus 4 alpha squared. And with these two operations of addition and multiplication, we get a situation which is very parallel to that of the integers. It's a little bit bigger and more broad, but it shares a lot of essential features. And so you can think about a commutative ring as being some kind of attempt at generalizing what goes on in these two examples. So after commutative rings, there's some case to be made that a field is a natural next kind of abstract algebraic object to consider. So the main example there is the rational numbers, okay? And that's an algebraic domain where we have addition and multiplication, but prominently, we also have the inverses of those. We have subtraction, which we also have in a commutative ring, but what's especially important is that we have division at least almost all the time. So a field is a commutative ring with, in addition, the possibility of division by nonzero numbers. So we want that every nonzero number has an inverse, a multiplicative inverse, just corresponding to the fact that if you take a rational number like 5/7, then its inverse is 7/5. So the rational numbers are a very interesting and important algebraic domain, but also full of some subtlety. In particular, there is the key ambiguity in the specification of rational numbers because we know that rational numbers are not uniquely defined. 2/3 is the same as 8/12, the same as -14/-21, and the rule, of course, is that there's an unambiguous way of deciding whether two things are equal. So we would check this by checking that 2 * 12 is the same as 3 * 8. Okay, but that does make the analysis and study of rational numbers definitely a little bit trickier because you have to factor in this ambiguity in how you specify one of the objects. And then, for example, we have addition in a kind of a familiar way that we all know. We have multiplication, which is familiar enough. We have division, where you basically multiply by the inverse or the reciprocal. And so these are standard examples, but it's a very rich field, a very important field. Now, students of abstract algebra these days learn immediately that there are then other fields as well. There are fields like the real numbers and the complex numbers, and maybe fields like a + bθ, where a and b are rational numbers. This is highly unfortunate, okay? It's highly unfortunate when students of abstract algebra, after having spent some time thinking about rational numbers, are quickly indoctrinated to believe that there are then these other fields R and C that are pretty well the same as rational numbers. Okay, this is a huge misunderstanding and a blind spot in modern mathematics. Okay, so in fact, going from rational numbers to R and C is a huge step in complexity that really takes us outside of abstract algebra. Okay, the essence of the idea of these two things, which is already faulty, but the essence of this faulty idea is that we can start doing arithmetic with infinite processes. Okay, that's the whole point—that we can take an infinite number of additions, we can take an infinite number of multiplications, and we can output the results of those infinite kinds of procedures and have a viable arithmetic. But modern students are not told the subtleties, and so they're just, you know, taught that, well, there's the rational numbers, and there's the real numbers and the complex numbers, and they're all pretty well the same. But the reality is that they're dramatically different. This is much, much more solid. These things are fraught with difficulties, and in fact, when you look carefully, they don't really work. Okay, so we have a very big difference of opinion when it comes to field theory, and that's going to be a big point here—that our field theory is going to be very different from the field theory that you learn at universities these days. We're going to do field theory solidly, correctly. So then there's non-commutative rings. So we started with commutative rings and then fields, and now we can sort of backtrack to non-commutative rings, where the addition is still commutative, but the multiplication now is no longer commutative. Okay, there's a standard example of this, which is a very rich example and hugely important in mathematics—the example of matrices. And the simple example is already very interesting: the 2x2 matrices with integer entries. This is an arithmetical domain in which we have operations of addition and multiplication. So the addition of 2x2 matrices is just the usual pointwise one, and the product of 2x2 matrices—we know it's this sort of funny multiplication where, to get this 2, you multiply that row by that column, so 1 * 2 + 2 * 0. And this -1 is 1 * -7 + 2 * 3. And that -2 is -1 * 2 + 4 * 0. And that 19 is -1 * -7 + 4 * 3. And in this arena, we also have this distinguished zero element, which is the identity for addition—just the matrix of all zeros. And then we have this curious identity element for multiplication called I, which is this matrix with ones on the diagonal and zeros off the diagonal. Okay, and this whole story generalizes to 3x3 matrices, 4x4 matrices, and so on. And so we have this family of these non-commutative rings. They're very important in mathematics, generating all kinds of interesting arithmetic that goes beyond the arithmetic that we're familiar with. And the last abstract algebraic structure that I want to tell you about today is probably the most common one, the one that undergraduates end up using practically the most—the idea of a vector space over a field. Okay, there's a very common example of this, which is the vectors in dimension three, say, with rational number entries. So this AB object is actually rather technically subtle to set up because you need to understand what a field is first. And we're going to talk about this. Let's say if we use the rational numbers, which is our generic field, then we can think about vectors with three components whose entries are rational numbers. Okay, and the operations here—there are two operations, but they're not exactly what you might think. The first one is vector addition, which is just the usual addition of vectors component-wise, like here. But the multiplication is a more limited multiplication; it's not really the same as in the ring story. It's not as if we multiply two vectors; rather, the multiplication here is what's called scalar multiplication. So in a vector space, we have the idea of multiplying vectors by elements in our field. All right, so for example, here is a vector, and here is a number five, which we think of as being an element in the field. And five times this vector—that makes sense, and it's just the obvious pointwise multiplication of each entry here by that scalar. And this is a very important algebraic object because it's the algebraic embodiment of our three-dimensional space, right? We live in three-dimensional space, and we like to describe that by having coordinates in a familiar kind of way, and then, you know, labeling axes and then using parallel lines and so on to specify directions. So, for example, go two in this direction and three in this direction and four in this direction; then we can get this combined transformation from zero up to here. So this red directed segment is the vector (2, 3, 4). It gives us a very physical interpretation of this algebraic structure. And this is hugely important in engineering and many applications and really is a workhorse for linear algebra, for setting up linear algebra. There's another example of vector spaces over fields. So, for example, we could take the 2x2 matrices—in fact, we could take n by m matrices, they don't have to be square—so just the space of matrices of a fixed kind or a fixed shape with rational number entries. You can add those, and you can multiply them by scalars, and so that naturally forms a vector space. But there are, in fact, lots of different examples of vector spaces, and this is a kind of a key abstract algebraic object that we're, in fact, going to start with. So when we start looking at abstract algebra, this is actually going to be the first one that we look at seriously, and that'll be quite interesting. But before we do that, I want to share with you some of the challenges that modern abstract algebra has. Now, abstract algebra is not as fraught with difficulties as modern analysis. Modern analysis has very, very serious problems. The problems with abstract algebra are also not to be diminished; they are also reasonably serious, but they're not as extensive as for analysis. But nevertheless, we do have to acknowledge what they are and have some understanding of the challenges that we need to overcome to set up abstract algebra properly, solidly, in the 21st century. And so the first thing to understand there is what the problems are, and then we can talk about how we're going to overcome them and move beyond. All right, so that's what we're going to do in our next video. We're going to look at some of the logical challenges with modern abstract algebra. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening [Music].
