# 214 - Logical challenges with abstract algebra I ｜ Abstract Algebra Math Foundations 214 ｜ NJ Wildberger [9-6JaNFsLnA].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In this video, we want to start looking at some of the logical challenges that lie ahead of us when we want to set up abstract algebra correctly. Now, abstract algebra these days is not as fraught with difficulties as modern analysis. Modern analysis has, through and through, logical difficulties—abstract algebra much less so, but still, there are some very significant problems, and today we want to start talking about that. Okay, so modern abstract algebra is a 20th-century development, more or less, and it explores generalizations of rather concrete, explicit arithmetical or algebraic structures. And the logical weaknesses I would like to frame in terms of sort of three different kinds or categories. So the first one, the one that we're going to talk about today, is this interaction between definitions and specifications. It's a subtle thing to try to enunciate because we don't have a good language for dealing with these kinds of problems, but I hope to give you lots of examples of the difficulty with, first of all, being precise about definitions and relating that to what we actually do when we want to specify an object that satisfies a given definition. Then, after that, we are going to also talk about the important challenges in going from the finite kind of situation to a so-called infinite situation without waffling. And then another very key problem with modern abstract algebra that runs through quite a lot of it is this over-reliance on the so-called Fundamental Theorem of Algebra. That wouldn't be a problem if the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra was really a theorem, but in fact, it's not—it's more of a dream. Okay, so we are going to have to face up to the music that the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra has serious problems and how to reframe a lot of the dependence of abstract algebra on the fundamental theorem. Okay, so in today's video, though, we're going to stick with this very interesting question of, you know, how do we define abstract algebraic objects, and what do we really mean by those definitions with the phrases that we use, with the framework that we have in place? So to frame this first challenge that's really centered on the role of definitions in the subject, maybe it's good to introduce it via this question by trying to identify what's actually the difference between a description, a definition, and a specification. These words are all kind of overlapping, and they have some common ground, but what exactly do they mean? And when we define something, are we really defining it, or are we rather describing it or specifying it? And to put that question into maybe a more explicit context, we could ask this other kind of question about how do we actually define or specify an algebraic structure for a computer? So suppose we're interested in getting our computer to understand what we mean by, say, a group G. So we have an idea of what a group G is. Suppose that we're actually interested in programming a computer to understand what a group is in the same kind of way as we do—what do we have to do? How do we have to frame the definition? So this is a very good question for a number of reasons. First of all, the role of computers, of course, is increasing dramatically, quickly, and it's going to play more and more a major role in mathematics—that's something that we're going to have to talk about in the future as well. Another reason is that beginner students—students who are just coming to the subject, who have not any experience with abstract algebra, seeing these definitions for the first time—in some sense, they have similar problems as does a computer. They don't have a prior history of intuitive examples or prior familiarization with things that they can lean on; they really require the definitions to be standalone and self-contained and precise enough for them to be able to distinguish when something is satisfying the definition and when it's not. So there's some kind of parallel between beginner students and computers. They both do not have the kind of intuition that a more experienced mathematician or student might have. So in order to be as specific as possible, let's consider the relatively simple example of a group and how we might define what a group is. Okay, so I'm going to give you a couple of different possible definitions so that we get some kind of idea about the possible scope of possibilities for defining things. Okay, so we want to know what is the definition of a group G, how are we going to introduce that, and our first answer to this, answer number one, is going to be very strongly computer-oriented. So this is a definition that we might give if we're aiming to try to get our computer to understand what a group is. Okay, so here's an example—here's a motivating example. Okay, we see a multiplication table, and the objects that are involved are the numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4, and this multiplication table is a way of telling us that 2 * 3 is 1, for example. Okay, so motivated by this, we'll say that here's our definition: that a kroup G—k now is a natural number greater than one, so k is one or k is 2 or k is three or k is 39, something like this—so a kroup G is a k by k array from k with certain properties. Okay, so before we get to the properties, what are we talking about? We're going back to our earlier discussion about arrays, and I remind you that an array was something like a matrix but ultimately was different. So an array, which we can think of in this case as being this little 4x4 block, this would be the k equal 4 case. So this is a 4x4 array, which we can think about as being a list of lists—so that list followed by that list followed by that list followed by that list. Okay, that's what we think of when we think of a 4x4 array. Okay, and this array is called a G, and in order for this array to be actually a group or a kroup, we want it to have certain properties that correspond to the associativity, the existence of an identity, and the existence of inverses—those are the three main properties that define a group. Okay, so we don't need to pay too much attention here; I just give some kind of official definition—it's actually not so important for us, but let's do it anyway. So the first one, which is a way of expressing associativity, is to say that the group array G, if we look at the indexes here, g l sub m, and here n—so there's two indices that give you the positions of the array. For example, this thing here would be the 3,4 entry. Okay, so the statement is that if you look at the l,m entry and then followed by the n entry, so now we're really taking the product of l * m and then multiplying by n, that that should be the same as the entry that you see when you look under the l and m,n entry—so g sub l comma g m,n. And that should be true for all l, m, and n within our range from 1 to k. All right, so that's some way of expressing associativity in terms of the entries of this array. The existence of the identity is perhaps easier. We specify that g sub 1,a,l is equal to l, and also g sub l comma 1 is equal to l for all l between 1 and k. This is telling us that the one object, the number one, is the multiplicative identity in this group. So the numbers are all one to k, and one is playing the role of the identity. So 1 * 1 is 1, 1 * 2 is 2, and so on. And similarly, 2 * 1 is 2, 3 * 1 is 3, and so on. And this third property is telling us about inverses—that for every index l between 1 and k, g l,m = 1 and g n comma l = 1 for some m and some n in the range. That's expressing the idea that along every row there's some entry with the entry one, and along every column there's some entry with the entry one, and that's telling us that every element has a multiplicative inverse, both a left inverse and a right inverse. Okay, so we don't really need to worry too much about the details—these are properties of a particular k by k array. The main point is that, first of all, the array is a very specific array involving just the numbers from 1 to k, and secondly, that in principle the properties are very cut and dried in that a computer could be programmed relatively easily to check whether a given k by k array from k was actually a group or not. So this is a very computer-friendly kind of definition of what a group is, but it's not the only kind of possible definition that we can think about. Okay, now here is our second definition. It's a little bit more human-friendly and perhaps a little bit less computer-friendly. Let's call it A2. So we're still going to define a kroup—k is a natural number bigger than or equal to one—so a kroup G is now a k ordered set, or oset S, together with a k by k array, say capital A, from S with certain properties. Okay, the properties are going to reflect the usual associativity, identity, and inverse properties that a group needs. But in this setup, the starting point is, first of all, an ordered set. For example, perhaps the set is the numbers 1, 3, 7, and 9. Notice that we have commas here, so our convention is that this is representing an ordered set, and we have braces here—that means that no repetition is allowed. So here's an ordered set which happens to have four elements: 1, 3, 7, and 9. And together with that set, here is an array that's basically telling us the multiplication table that we want to give this set. So it's an array, in this case a 4x4 array, and it's from S, meaning that the entries are all from our set and, okay, okay. So officially, there's a list and another list and another list and another list, and these lists are telling us how we multiply by 1 and by 3, by 7, and by 9, following the order of the points or the elements in our ordered set. So we're using the fact that these things are ordered to give a prescribed order in which we're placing the rows or the columns of this array. So in fact, this is the group that we have seen before. This is U10—this is the set of numbers less than 10 which are relatively prime to 10, things that don't have common factors of 2 and 5. And this is the multiplication table mod 10. Okay, now, in terms of the properties, well, this array should have certain properties. It should have an associativity property, which is the same kind of thing that I defined previously, and one can define it in terms of this array in sort of a similar kind of way. And we want one of the elements of S to be the identity—this is now a little bit different from previously because we're not necessarily having the numbers here be the numbers one through something. In principle, this set S can be rather arbitrary, so we need to specify that one of the elements of S has the identity property. Now, in this case, it happens to be the number one, but we don't need to have the number one in our set, and we don't need to have it be the first one, okay? Somewhere there's an identity, and each element then has a left or right inverse, and that means that once you have identified the identity—in this case one, but maybe it's something else—then you see that every row has a number one in it and every column has a number one in it. Okay, so this is more flexible because we are not insisting that the actual objects be the numbers from 1 to k. And this is in keeping with the human-centric view on groups that really, you know, groups come from a wide range of different situations. Sometimes we have groups coming from number theory like this, in which case the numbers naturally are not the numbers one, four—they're the numbers 1, 3, 7, and 9. In other situations, we are considering maybe matrices—the group elements might be matrices. In other situations, the group elements might be permutations. In other situations, the group elements might be transformations, which are actually given geometrically. So this framework is much more flexible in that the actual objects that are, you know, the basis of the algebraic object, the group, are in fact quite arbitrary, at least in principle. However, there's a corresponding challenge with this approach, which is that we have to have a prior theory of osets, all right? So we're saying a k oset S, but we're not saying that the objects have to come from some specified domain—we're being open-ended about what kinds of elements can be in our set S. And that means that we have a problem in specifying, limiting what kinds of objects are allowed. For example, maybe someone comes along and looks at our definition and says, okay, I've got a new kind of a group. I'm going to let my S be the set of all fish. Set of all fish—that's going to be my set, and the multiplication is going to be, and then I specify some rule, you know, that the product of fish F1 and fish F2 is the fish F3 which is nearest to the midpoint of F1 and F2 or some crazy thing. Okay, so would we allow that kind of situation where the set S is some kind of set which maybe we don't generally consider to be an honest set? In other words, we have to have a prior theory of sets in order to really make this solid, and as we've talked about before, that's in fact quite challenging to first lay out what the boundaries are that separate honest sets from make-believe sets or almost make-believe sets or almost honest sets and so on. So although this is more human-friendly, a computer would find this much more challenging to deal with because, first of all, the ambiguity in the nature of the S, and also because there's less structure. We're not guaranteed that the first element is the identity, so we have to search around for an identity—we don't know where it might be. It's a little bit less structured, tougher for a computer to deal with, but it's an alternative. Okay, now here's our third definition, which is even more flexible, so we're kind of zooming out even more, more friendly in some fashion. So we'll call it A3, and the definition is that now a group G is a set S, no longer an ordered set, just a set, together with a function, say m, from S cross S to S, with certain properties. Okay, S cross S—that means lists of elements of S, two lists, so pairs of elements of S. So for example, here is a set S: 3,1,9,7. This is now a set because it doesn't have commas, it's not ordered, so we can move the elements around and the set hasn't changed. And here is the multiplication. Okay, we don't have an array now—we're thinking more of a function from this set of pairs to S that represents the multiplication. And, okay, typically set theorists like to encode functions like this, at least formally, with some kind of framework that's in this direction. So what we could do is we could list all possible pairs of elements here and the corresponding function value of m. So let's say this little entry here is telling us that 3 * 9 = 7. That's representing one particular element in the array that we wrote down before. So now we've encoded the multiplication, not as an array but as more in sort of a function point of view using more or less standard set theory kind of ideas of how to encode a function. Okay, so then of course there's the question about the properties, the three properties, and we would have to then say what the three properties are that this set and multiplication satisfy. Again, we have to build in associativity and the existence of an identity and the existence of inverses. But we could use this language that's even more flexible because now we have just a set, and we don't necessarily have an order to the set. And also the multiplication is now a little bit more unstructured—we just have this function, and we haven't necessarily organized the function into an array. We haven't listed things; we've just given all the information sort of all at once in terms of this big set of lists. Okay, so this is just another approach to what a group is, another way of thinking about what the definition of a group is. And now I want to consider a variant of that. So it's going to be a very close variant, so we're going to call it A3 Prime instead of A4. And the actual definition is exactly the same as A3, so a group G is a set S together with a function m from S cross S to S with certain properties. What's different is that we're going to interpret this differently, or we're going to interpret it in a more broader, looser fashion. So here's an example: Now I'm going to consider the set S which is rotations of a square. Here's a square, and I'm going to consider the set S of rotations of a square. Now, hopefully, you know what that means. Hopefully, you know that, okay, we're talking about rotations of the square. There's actually four rotations of the square. There's the identity rotation, which doesn't do anything; there's a rotation by a quarter turn, by a half turn, by a 3/4 of a turn, or if you like, a quarter turn in the opposite direction. So there are four rotations of this square. But notice that we haven't really specified what the rotations are explicitly; we've just said the rotations of a square, and sort of the onus is on you to know what that means. And then, what about the multiplication m? Well, here we're saying that the multiplication m is composition. In other words, the product of S1 and S2, or S1 and S2, or two rotations is the composition of S1 and S2. Okay, so you need to know what the composition is—it means that you do one of them first and then you do the other. Typically here, you do S2 first and then you do S1. Now notice that this is different from the previous example. Here we're not saying precisely what all the rotations exactly are and giving them names, and we're not saying precisely what the product of any specific two elements is, as we did in the previous case. Here, we're just giving a general rule that allows you to calculate the product of any two elements. So in some sense, this interpretation of the definition shifts some of the onus of creating this group on the reader. Okay, you are now invited to write down the elements of this set in some notation that you can introduce, and then you are invited to understand what this composition amounts to in terms of those objects. So you can see that a computer would have more of a difficult time with this because a computer would have to know how to interpret this, and then a computer probably would want to restate this descriptive kind of definition into something more concrete, either into an array or one of those multiplication sets that we had in the previous example. Of course, there's then also the question of stating the properties, and so we haven't done that, but you know, in addition to saying these things, we would have to specify what the various properties are—the usual associative, identity, and inverse properties. So it's interesting that this A3 and A3 Prime definition—they both actually used exactly the same wording, but there was a difference in interpretation as to what that actually involved. So the question is, is there actually an effective or computational difference between these various definitions? Does it really make a difference, or are they all really more or less the same? I want to suggest that there is a difference—there's, in fact, a very big difference, both theoretical and practical, between defining a group in these various ways. In fact, I want to suggest that group theory—okay, group theory is very strongly dependent on which of these definitions you choose. Group theory that starts with A1 is going to be a different subject from group theory that starts with A2, which is going to be a different subject that starts with A3 or A3 Prime. Currently, I would say that our modern definition of a group, even though people don't spell it out as precisely and carefully as we're trying to do here, is more in the direction of this third A3 Prime interpretation. We are allowed to specify groups by giving some description of the objects and then some description of the multiplication. We're not really obliged to list all the objects and then to correspondingly list or, you know, specify the multiplication in its entirety. So that's the general understanding—maybe not uniformly, but it's the general understanding in the modern treatment of the subject that more or less this is the right definition or the current definition. But you can see that there's a big difference, or I hope you can see that, from a computer's point of view, this is much more problematic, much more ambiguous. I want to bring the differences between these different points of view into focus for you by looking at a specific example. So let's have a look at U12, which is another group of order four. So let's start by looking at U12 from the second definition point of view, which is maybe reasonably close to our human perception. So U12, by definition, consists of the numbers less than 12 which are relatively prime to 12, and those numbers are 1, 5, 7, and 11. And there's a natural ordering to them because they're increasing, so we may as well list them. So here's an ordered set: 1, 5, 7, 11. And the multiplication is encoded in this array. The array respects the order of the set that we've introduced, and here is the product of these numbers—this is basically multiplication mod 12, but we don't have to say that. All the information is already encoded in this array. And actually, the array is just actually this 4x4 block here—these columns and the rows out here that index things are really just for our benefit, but the computer doesn't need it, it just needs that 4x4 array if it knows the order of the objects. So, for example, 5 * 5 is usually 25, but mod 12 that's 1, and so we get a 1 there. And so, okay, so that's a very familiar thinking of U12—that's sort of what we would write down most naturally and simply. But here, here is a computer version of that, where the computer has come along and said, okay, we're going to encode that not with the numbers 1, 5, 7, but with the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4. Because this is a four-group, it has four elements, and so we're automatically just going to use the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4 to represent the objects. And so basically, the computer comes along and says, I'm going to call out 1, 2, 3, and 4 and just replaces the 5 with a 2 and the 7 with a 3 and the 11 with a 4. Okay, so there's the same table, but having done that, the advantage now is that the computer can encode the entire group just with this 4x4 array. Okay, you send that 4x4 array to another computer somewhere else in the world, and the other computer knows exactly what the group is—not in terms of its original definition as U12, but in terms of the algebraic structure that encodes the group structure. Okay, in terms of our third definition—the third definition involved a set, so we now lose the ordering; we're not concerned with the ordering, it's just now a set. So I represent that by giving you the same four elements, but not necessarily in the usual order. So the objects are 7, 1, 11, and 5, and then we have to record the multiplication in a kind of function point of view. So we have to record that 5 * 5 is 1, so we can do that by somewhere in this list, right there, recording the triple 5,5,1. And we interpret that to mean that 5 * 5 is 1. So there's now 16 elements in this set of multiplications. This is a way of encoding this array in a kind of set-theoretical point of view. So we have the set, and we have the multiplication. And now a quite different variant on that: Now we're going to look at A3 Prime, where we describe the set and the multiplication differently. So we say that S is the set of numbers less than 12, relatively prime to 12. That's a description of the set S—it's the same set, but this is a description of it. And the multiplication M—well, that's multiplication mod 12. So you need to know something about relatively prime numbers; you need to know something about how to multiply mod 12 to understand this definition. But if you do, then I guess you could say that this encodes the same kind of information as does this, but perhaps less specifically, less concretely, more abstractly. Clearly, this is easier to write down, right? We can write that down very, very easily, and if we replace the number 12 with the number, you know, 12,497, then writing any one of these correspondingly would involve a lot more work. But writing this definition would be essentially almost no more work—we would just replace this 12 with 12,497, whatever we said. So there's some efficiency here, but we have to admit that we're kind of putting some of the specific difficulty in enunciating the algebraic structure under the carpet, or we're imposing the task to the reader instead of doing it ourselves. We're not really giving the group in its entirety; we're giving instructions for the reader to be able to conjure up the group. It's like a recipe for a cake rather than a cake itself—it's your birthday, congratulations, here is a recipe for a birthday cake. Okay, thank you. That's sort of what we're doing here. So there's quite a difference between these various things, and you can see that if you wanted to build up group theory as an intellectual discipline, it would matter. It would matter very much which of these definitions, or perhaps some other definition, you adopted—especially if you were a computer. Okay, if you were a computer, you would have to have a lot of AI underneath your bonnet to be able to make sense of this kind of amorphous, rather wide-ranging definition, while if you were a relatively simple-minded computer, you could deal with this quite efficiently. So we're naturally led to some very reasonable questions as to the meanings of phrases that are very commonly found in modern algebra—in fact, in modern mathematics more generally. For example, the statement, "Let G be a group." What does that actually mean? Yes, it means that G is satisfying the definition of a group, but which one of our various definitions or interpretations of the definition are we thinking about when we say that phrase? In fact, there may be other definitions that are different from the ones that I've given or other interpretations. When we say, "Consider a group G," what do we mean actually, effectively, concretely? What are we imagining when we say that phrase? When we say, "Suppose we have a group G," again, that's a phrase, but what is exactly the meaning of that phrase? And to make that more concrete, we can think about it from a computer's point of view or a beginning student's point of view. If you're a computer and someone says, "Consider a group G," okay, well, what does it mean? Hopefully, you've been given one of these definitions of a group to begin with, and then you know precisely what it means, especially if that definition was the A1 definition or possibly the A2 definition, along with some additional information. But if it was the A3 definition, you very well may be at sea in terms of really understanding what that means. And I think those comments also apply to the beginning student, even though many beginning students in algebra don't really appreciate that. Maybe this is part of the difficulties that they find with the subject. So the professor comes in and says, and gives some rather vague or open-ended definition, says, "Okay, now let's consider a group G. Suppose we have a group G. Let G be a group," and the student maybe naturally thinks, well, what does that concretely actually mean? So once a student has spent some years in the academy and is used to using this language repeatedly, they maybe get some kind of intuitive feeling and are no longer so strongly required or confused by it, but I think beginning students find it difficult—what are we actually talking about when we make statements like this? And these statements are very common in mathematics. Okay, so to wrap up, let me give you a more complicated example that really shows how important these distinctions are. If we stick with small and medium-sized examples, maybe the distinction between these various points of view isn't so big; maybe we can feel as if we can overcome the hurdles. But if we move towards bigger examples, then the distinctions between these various points of view become very clear—we see it in very stark relief. So let's ask the question: What is U of Zed? Zed is our favorite big number, 10 triangle, 10 + 23, and we're after U of Z. Okay, so if we take an A1 point of view, the A1 point of view means that we need an array, a k by k array from k. Where k is what? Where k is the size of this thing. U of Zed is going to be the set of numbers less than Zed which are relatively prime to Zed. In order to find the number of elements in that Zed, these days you need to understand the prime factorization of Zed. Without the prime factorization of Zed, you have no hope of finding the number of numbers less than Z which are relatively prime to Z—in other words, the phi is the Euler's totient function. Now, we have seen, right, that the prime factorization of Z is something that's completely beyond us here in our universe that we're living in. There's no way we're going to be able to write down a prime factorization of Zed, even though due to the efforts of Joel Suen, we understand the first eight primes. Okay, but the next quadzillion primes, we have no idea about yet, okay? And finding all the prime factors is completely—it's a completely inaccessible, hopeless problem. Therefore, finding the number of elements in this set is also a completely hopeless thing. And finding actually an explicit array that has all those numbers arranged in a nice way and the multiplication mod Zed—completely impossible, like physically, mathematically, theoretically impossible in our universe. So from an A1 point of view, this is a completely undefined object. The computer just stares blankly at you, says nothing. How about A2? Okay, there we have a little bit more flexibility. We don't actually have to convert everything from whatever we started with to the numbers 1 to k, but we still have to exhibit the objects. We're still not anywhere closer to this thing; we still can't exhibit all the elements in here. There's no way we can exhibit all the elements. First of all, there's far too many—most of them are numbers that are so big they don't even fit into the universe. Completely and hopelessly impossible. Okay, there's no way that this makes sense. So even a human being that has this orientation just stares at you blindly and says, I'm sorry, but it doesn't make any sense. It's not defined. How about A3? In A3, we were a little bit more loose; we were allowing our set to be actually unordered, not like a real set. And we were still, though, specifying that the multiplication had to be laid out in terms of some kind of function, explicit kind of function in a set-theoretical point of view. Again, this is just not accessible to us. But the A3 Prime point of view—ah, that's quite different because there we're allowed to use words and string phrases together. We just say, "Consider S, the set of n in Nat such that n is between 1 and Zed, and n and Z are relatively prime." Voila, there is the set S that the group is supported on. We don't need to concern ourselves with the fact that most of the elements in this set are completely outside our universe; there's no way we can even list them. There's no way we can bring all of these together. There's no way we have any access to this in any reasonable fashion. But nevertheless, we can write down this phrase in a kind of Pollyanna optimistic fashion. It's like poetry: Look, there it is—there is the set. And what about the multiplication? That's easy: m is just multiplication mod Zed. You can see the advantages in having a modern point of view on things. If you have a modern point of view, as represented by this A3 Prime definition, then you have no problem dealing with U of Z because you're allowed to think of U of Z in this amorphous, ambiguous, poetical fashion. No problem, as long as no one asks you about any specifics. So as long as you're willing to ride over the actual reality at some suitably lofty level, where you actually never get your hands dirty with, like, dealing with the objects, just having this abstract point of view—well, then you can imagine that you're doing group theory, that you're studying U of Z, but your computer has no idea what you're talking about. There's a fork in the road here, isn't there, people? All right, there's this poetical, idealistic, modern 20th-century point of view that allows us to just write down stuff like this. And then there's the 21st-century, computational, explicit, concrete point of view that says, no, no, that doesn't make sense. I am advocating that we think about things in a more computational, precise, explicit, concrete form and give up on the 20th-century abstraction, ideology, and wishful thinking encoded in this kind of object specification. So the lesson is that definitions and their interpretations do matter, and this is going to be a very important theme for us as we develop abstract algebra. We are going to have to take very special care about the nature of the definitions that we're invoking, and we want to pin them down to computational reality so that when someone asks for some examples, we can give them real examples and not fake examples like this. This was only our first challenge, okay? So we still have two more challenges to deal with, namely the transition from finite objects to infinite objects, and we'll have to deal with the fundamental dream of algebra. So we'll have a little bit of things to say about those things in our next video. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for [Music] listening.

So currently, when we say infinite objects, we have quotation marks around them. That's not necessary in all cases—perhaps we can create infinite kinds of objects, but we have to do so carefully and ultimately in a finite way. So we may have to have a workaround, okay, and that's going to be part of the story here. So today I want to talk about a particular example that illustrates both some interesting aspects of finite group theory and then moving on to infinite group theory. We're going to employ a little bit about matrices as well, which should be interesting. So this issue of transitioning from finite to infinite. So with current modern algebra, we are often treating infinite algebraic objects with the same framework as finite ones. This is almost invariably a result of sloppy thinking. So the strategy is that if our basic definitions are fuzzy enough and imprecise enough, then yes, we can hope that we cast a big enough net so that both finite and infinite objects are embraced at the same time. But in fact, going from finite groups to infinite groups is a huge step and requires really different, separate thinking. Same thing going from finite rings to infinite rings or from finite fields to infinite fields. In fact, pretty well every abstract algebraic object has the same problem, okay? We need different kinds of thinking, we need often a more general kind of framework that gets at infinite procedures in a finite way. So we have to think more carefully and often we have to be a little bit clever. So I want to illustrate the issues at hand by looking at group theory and a particularly interesting example, that of SL2. So that's the context of 2x2 matrices with determinant one. The SL stands for special linear, so this is the special linear group, okay? So let me remind you of a few basic facts about 2x2 matrices. First of all, the matrix a b c d has determinant a d minus b c. That's a number associated with a 2x2 matrix and here is the familiar product of two matrices and here is the basic rule about determinants that if you take the determinant of a product of two matrices, so A and B might be these two matrices, and then you take the determinant of this thing, it turns out to be the product of the determinant of A times the determinant of B. So this is the fundamental fact about determinants. In particular, if A and B both have determinant one, then their product is also going to have determinant one. That's the basic property that makes this kind of thing into actually a group. Okay, so now let's have a look at a rather specific case of 2x2 matrices with determinant one where we're looking at everything from the point of view of mod 2 arithmetic. In mod 2 arithmetic, it's a very simple kind of modular arithmetic—there are really only two values, zero and one, because two is declared to be like zero. So the group that we're defining here is a set of matrices a, b, c, d where the entries a, b, c, d are all from the set zero and one, so each entry is either 0 or 1 and the overall determinant a d minus b c is congruent to 1 mod 2, so it could be one or it could also be minus one because mod 2 minus one is the same as one and the multiplication, we state that this is the multiplication of matrices mod 2. That means after you do the multiplication you simplify everything mod 2. Okay, so this is definitely an A3 Prime type of definition in that we are using descriptive language to specify the set and also, well, we're basically sort of talking about what the multiplication is instead of laying it out in form of an array a, okay, or a functional set. By the way, the name for this group is traditionally SL2 F2, the F2 referring to the fact that we are basically working in a field with two elements. So for example, here are two matrices—this one has determinant 1 - 0, that's one, this one has determinant 0 - 1, that's minus one, but minus one is the same as 1 mod 2, so this also is a matrix that belongs to this set and the product is 1, 1, 1 + 1 is 2, which is the same as zero, and 1 + 0 is 1, and so that's also an entry of this set. So we're using mod 2 arithmetic when we do the multiplication. So note that this definition, it's one of these A3 Prime definitions, it's not very specific about what the set actually explicitly is and it does leave us to understand what or to compute what the multiplication is. In other words, it's not telling us directly what the product of these two things is, it's giving us a recipe for us to be able to compute what the product is. So it's a different kind of structure than when you are given the multiplication table directly just as a table and you're not required to understand anything more—you just look up stuff. Here, we're asked to calculate stuff. All right, so for our computer, this requires some more sophistication because we have to know something about matrix multiplication in order to understand what this group is. So I want you to appreciate that different definitions really do lead to different kinds of group theory. So let's unpack the previous A3 Prime kind of definition to a more explicit A2 kind of specification. This is using the language of our previous video. So then what we need to do is we need to determine explicitly first of all what actually the elements of this group are, and here I've actually listed the elements of the group. It turns out that there's exactly six matrices made up of zeros and ones that have determinant one, at least when you're working mod 2. Here they are—there's the identity matrix and then there's this one which is a little bit like the identity off-diagonal, and then these other ones have a single zero in each of the four places and otherwise ones. And then here is a multiplication table, an explicit array that lists these elements in exactly that order both horizontally and vertically and then tells you what the various products are. So the product of this matrix times this one is this one, you can check that, hopefully I've done all the multiplications correctly. So if we were thinking about groups in terms of the A2 definition, then we would say "Aha, here is the definition of this group G, here is the set and here is the multiplication table as given by this 6x6 array." It's different, it's a different way of thinking about what a group is. And now here is that same group but viewed from the A1 definition. This was the most computer-oriented definition where we require that the elements be converted to just numbers 1, 2, 3, up to K where K is the number of elements in the group, and that we record just the array, the K by K array of multiplications in the language of 1 to K. So what I've done is I've just replaced the six matrices in the order that I gave you them in the previous slide with the numbers 1 through six and I've just converted the table into a corresponding multiplication table now for the numbers 1 through six. So notice that we've lost information because the original definition of the objects as 2x2 matrices with a certain determinant etc. and the original matrix multiplication mod 2 formula for the multiplication, these are now lost. We no longer see them, we just see the Bare Bones algebraic structure of what multiplies to what to get what. So this is a very efficient way of thinking but it does really look at things from just a pure algebraic point of view. Okay, okay, um but this is a a pleasant group with six elements, it's one that we should be familiar with. So as an exercise, show that up to relabeling this is the same group as S3, it's the same as the symmetric group on three things which we've seen before, but it's now not in terms of permutations, not in terms of matrices but just in terms of multiplications of the numbers 1 through six. And when I say "up to labeling" we should be aware that this particular multiplication table is what we've obtained by choosing a particular order of the elements, right? If we had chosen a different order of the six elements then we would have gotten a different multiplication table, so there's a little bit of subtlety here about how the ordering and choice of ordering determines the multiplication table. But from an A1 point of view, a group is just a multiplication table like this. Convince yourself this is really S3 in disguise. And so now let's make a jump from this finite group that we've been looking at to a sort of infinite version. Okay, so we'll get an infinite version by replacing the finite condition that the entries are just zeros and ones with a more general condition that will allow a c and d to be arbitrary integers and we won't do mod 2 arithmetic, we'll do ordinary integer arithmetic so the determinant has to be exactly one. So this is now a new group H, let's say, and its name might be SL2 Int because it's special linear group determinant one with integer entries and we'll specify that the multiplication is just matrix multiplication. So this is very much an A3 Prime type of definition. We are just giving a description of a set, a set in quotes, very big quotes, and a description of matrix multiplication as being the multiplication that's involved. We are definitely not exhibiting the set as a set with all the elements there for you. We are definitely not exhibiting the multiplication in terms of an array where you can see the entire array in your database. That's going to be impossible because there's going to be an unbounded number of such matrices. This is not a finite group, it's unbounded, and in particular these integers can get very big. So for example, here is a matrix with determinant 1, 6 - 5, here's another one with determinant 1, that's - 7 + 8, that's + one. So the product of these two matrices is that one there, you can check, and you can also check that the inverse of one of these matrices is also a matrix of the same type. That's an important observation, and so it's nice to note that the inverse of such a 2x2 determinant one matrix is obtained by transposing the diagonal elements, so they swap places, and the off diagonal elements get multiplied by minus one. You can check that when you make the multiplication then you do get the identity matrix. Okay, so this is a very interesting group and it's actually a group that's studied very much in number theory and in geometry as well. So it's a very important group mathematically, SL2 over the integers, but it's an infinite group via this A3 Prime definition. So in modern mathematics, we do not, we do not have any problems with making a definition like this. So this would be absolutely routine kind of definition that you would find in mathematical papers throughout the subject. But we are going to look more skeptically at this because we recognize that this definition here, this A3 Prime kind of definition, really finesses some key points, namely it doesn't tell us explicitly what all the objects are and it doesn't tell us explicitly what the multiplication actually is. It's up for us to generate elements, and we're only going to be able to do that obviously sort of up to a point because there's an unbounded number of these determinant one 2x2 integral matrices, and it's up to us to sort of work out what the multiplication table is from this sort of basic direction here. So this kind of definition is definitely not in the spirit of A1 and A2, and in fact from the spirit of A1 and A2, if you had that as your orientation, you would not be able to take this and to transform it into something that was meaningful to you, right? If you're a computer that has been trained to understand that a group is a an array with you know index by 1 to K, 1 to K, it's a K by K array with certain properties, you're not going to be able to express this in that language. This is going to be outside your understanding. Similarly with the A2 definition, even if you're allowed to not use the numbers 1 through K, but rather some arbitrary kind of set, you're still not able to list all of the elements. And when we start thinking about things, okay, when we start thinking especially with the point of view of bigger numbers, we start to appreciate that a lot of these matrices inevitably are going to end up involving very big numbers, and then we run into the same kinds of problems that we do when we're considering the set of numbers from one to Z. We talked before about this, right? What does it mean to talk about the set of numbers from one to Z because we know that most of those numbers are too big to fit into the known universe. They are completely invisible to us. Well, very similarly, that's going to happen with the entries of this group as well. Most of these matrices that we're going to end up considering are going to have entries which are so big that they don't fit into the universe. So exactly the same kind of problems are going to occur here that it's no longer clear that we really have control over all such matrices. In reality, we only have control up to a certain point, up to the point that we're able to do arithmetic, but here we're positing that we are going to consider this whole thing even beyond the limits of our arithmetic. Okay, so from a computational point of view, this is fraught with difficulties. But modern mathematics doesn't recognize that, doesn't see that, because it's quite happy to allow such sort of sloppy definitions, because for one thing it makes it easier to make this transition from finite to infinite things, because if you're not going to be concrete about specifying stuff, then it doesn't really matter if you become a little bit more sloppy by introducing an infinite number of things into the story, infinite number in quotes. So let me phrase what we've been discussing in the following lesson, that if we use the sloppy A3 Prime definition or description of a group, and I'm inclined to think of it more as a description rather than a definition, then we can substitute infinite set for finite set with impunity because part of the whole story is that we are not being careful about what the word set means in the first place. But on the other hand, if we adopt a more careful computational approach along the lines of the A1 or A2 definitions which are geared more towards what a computer can understand, then we must do more work if we want to go from the finite group definition to an infinite group definition. It's not enough just to replace the word group or finite group with the word infinite group. We must actually understand things from a different point of view. So we're going to need separate definitions of group and infinite group. These are not going to be things which are found in the same basket. How to do this is a very good question and we are going to talk about that. But at this point, I just want to flag that there is a big jump if you're going to do things properly between understanding finite concrete explicit objects and infinite versions of them. All right, currently we only have quotes around all the infinite versions, and to make theories where there's no quotes and things are actually precise, a lot more work is required. In our next video, I want to talk about our third issue that abstract algebra really needs to wrestle with, and that's the fundamental theorem of algebra. All right, so we're going to have to say some uncomfortable truths about the current PR to this important but currently dubious result. I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for [Music] listening.

# 216 - The fundamental dream of algebra ｜ Abstract Algebra Math Foundations 216 ｜ NJ Wildberger [QSZsTeO-C1o].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. We're moving to set up abstract algebra, and we've outlined some challenges that modern treatment faces. This is our third challenge: the "fundamental dream of algebra," which is usually known as the "fundamental theorem of algebra." Unfortunately, this is not really a theorem currently. This is a very big topic; it has huge implications throughout mathematics, so it's a very foundational kind of issue and completely appropriate for this playlist, this channel, where we're dealing with foundational issues. So the fundamental theorem of algebra—we have to address some unpleasant, uncomfortable truths about this very important and interesting result, but it's logically problematic. Okay, so it's a fundamental result or claim from undergraduate mathematics, and in fact, there are a couple of different forms of the fundamental theorem. I’d like to distinguish between them by calling the first one the fundamental theorem of algebra number one. It is that every polynomial P(x) equals a₀ + a₁x + ... + aₙxⁿ (so this is a polynomial in x of degree n greater than or equal to one, so there's at least a single x appearing somewhere) has a complex zero, R = a + bi. What does that mean? It means that P(R) = 0, so it means that if you have a non-constant polynomial, then you can find a complex number so that when you substitute that complex number in for the x, then the total result is zero. Okay, now we have to understand that these coefficients that are appearing here are usually taken to be real numbers, and the a and the b appearing here in this description of complex numbers are also taken to be real numbers. Now, that, of course, is hugely problematic because there are all kinds of difficulties, as we've seen, with the theory of real numbers. So that's a big part of the problem, but there's more as well. So this is the first version of the fundamental theorem: that every non-constant polynomial has a complex number zero or root. And now here is the second version, or alternate version, of the fundamental theorem of algebra, which asserts that a polynomial of degree at least one can be factored as a product of linear factors if we're allowing ourselves complex coefficients. So it says that we can find complex numbers R₁, R₂, up to Rₙ (where n is the degree of the polynomial), and a lambda real number so that the polynomial is actually equal to lambda times the factor (x - R₁) times the factor (x - R₂) all the way up to the factor (x - Rₙ), and these numbers are the zeros or the roots of the polynomial. Clearly, if we replace x with, say, one of these R's, say R₁, then one of these terms will be zero, and so the whole product will be zero. And as before here, all these coefficients are generally real numbers, although it turns out that there's a somewhat more general version of this theorem which extends the same statements to the case where the coefficients are not just so-called real numbers but are in fact more general complex numbers themselves, and the theorem still holds true. So some of the ways that the fundamental theorem of algebra ends up being used in undergraduate mathematics are through the consequence that if you have a real polynomial, then you can deduce that it factors into a product of linear and quadratic factors, all of whose coefficients are real numbers. So even if you're a little bit unhappy about or unfamiliar with complex number arithmetic, a consequence of this is that if you have an arbitrary polynomial with so-called real coefficients, or maybe rational coefficients, then you could write it as a product of linear and quadratic factors all with real or possibly rational coefficients, typically real. That has a lot of applications; for example, it's used a lot to justify certain integration formulas using the idea of partial fractions. So it's a key tool in the theory of integration, and it also allows all kinds of solutions to various problems like recurrence relations, differential equations, eigenvalue problems, problems coming from combinatorics involving generating functions, and other things too. So there are quite a lot of situations where we need to find zeros of polynomials or we need to factor polynomials, and it's always the fundamental theorem that is the workhorse that lets us assert such zeros or factorizations. But it's not just pure mathematics that uses this kind of thinking. A lot of applied mathematics leans very heavily on the fundamental theorem of algebra. So there's a wide variety of problems coming from physics, engineering, chemistry, and also economics and industry that one way or the other need to extract zeros of polynomials and factorization. So here's a picture of a bunch of springs that separate masses M₁, M₂, M₃, and M₄, and they're allowed to oscillate this way because they're these springs, and they have different masses, and you might be interested in understanding how these coupled oscillators behave, okay? And that's a kind of problem that once you set it up in physics involves these kinds of polynomials that have to be solved. There's a wide variety of such problems; for example, Google's PageRank algorithm has some connection with this—neural nets, stress matrices, rotations—you can give a very, very long list of real-life applications, ultimately, of the fundamental theorem of algebra. So it's not just a theoretical tool; it's also very important for practical applications. Now, for us, we're interested in setting up abstract algebra, and so we have made the point that in abstract algebra also, these theorems play a very important role. So, for example, the fundamental theorem really underpins character theory for groups. So, if you want to study group theory, non-commutative finite group theory, there's a theory of characters, and the fundamental theorem is intimately connected with that theory. It's also the basis for much of field theory. When we set up fields and look at concrete examples of fields, and especially the associated area called Galois theory, the fundamental theorem and its role are so intimately woven into this theory. It's very important for the structure theory of matrices—matrices and the fundamental theorem of algebra are intimately connected. For example, Jordan canonical form and eigenvalue problems are all associated with this. But it has a role in abstract algebra; algebraic number theory is very much relying on this kind of thinking of the fundamental theorem because it tries to embed a lot of the fields and rings that occur in number theory into complex numbers, and that's essentially playing this game. It also underpins the importance of complex algebraic geometry. In algebraic geometry, we study curves and higher-dimensional extensions called varieties, and for various technical reasons, one likes to work over the complex numbers rather than more natural intuitive sort of rational or real numbers. And a lot of the reason for that is because invoking the fundamental theorem makes a lot of things easier. This is a very common orientation in algebraic geometry, again, ultimately coming down to the fundamental theorem. So this is a very important result for undergraduate and higher mathematics. It figures prominently in a wide range of mathematics and also applications to applied mathematics, but for us, it also has a major input into abstract algebra. So how sad is it that, in fact, these theorems are actually seriously logically flawed? It's very unfortunate, and it's something that people, I know, would rather not hear and would rather not learn about. They would like to pretend that things are okay, but they are not, in fact, okay. Today, there are serious problems, and in fact, these theorems have to be recast, they have to be rethought, and there's a lot of very, very important work and opportunities for young people to come and to help in reconfiguring the mathematics around the fundamental theorem of algebra. So we're going to be talking about that. So unfortunately, the reality is that the fundamental theorem of algebra parts 1 &amp; 2 that we've discussed are actually dreams. Currently, they are not real theorems—they are dreams. They are the result of wishful thinking and insufficient attention paid to rigor and careful analysis. There are many proofs of the fundamental theorem of algebra, and this was already one indicator of the logical difficulties that the subject has. So many people have come along and tried to pin down, first of all, what this theorem is really about and then provide some kind of argument for it. Many people have thought that they have an argument, and then later generations come along and say, "Well, wait a minute, he was actually assuming something rather there, and that's not entirely justified," and then they give a new proof, and then some decades later, someone comes along and states the same thing about their arguments. This has been going on for a long time, going back probably first to D'Alembert, and Euler prominently was also very interested in the fundamental theorem, and then Lagrange, Galois, Cauchy, and Weierstrass. It's like a who's who of famous mathematicians who tried to understand what was really going on with this fundamental theorem to try to provide proofs. And it didn't stop with Weierstrass; many 20th-century authors have also contributed new proofs. In fact, Gauss contributed to probably half a dozen proofs. So he proved it, and then he would wait a few years and then come up with another proof. And this probably gets him recognized that there was something not quite solid; there was a need for further understanding because this topic touches base with what I think is really the fundamental problem in mathematics, which is the nature of the continuum. And I'll talk about that more in my next video, but the nature of the continuum is actually a very subtle issue, and it's intimately connected with the fundamental theorem of algebra. And so mathematicians have wrestled with this for a long time, and I'm claiming that in fact, unfortunately, all of these proofs or attempted proofs are incomplete or wrong. Unfortunately, they don't really work. So even the current statements of the theorems are faulty, and you can see that in a simple way because if you have a theorem that rests crucially on real numbers and you don't have a proper theory of real numbers, well, then obviously you're in strife, and the fact that we don't have a proper theory of real numbers almost immediately implies that there's going to be problems with things like the fundamental theorem of algebra that crucially rely on properties of real numbers. But in fact, we can see the problems in a much more direct way. So mathematics is not just about theorizing and making abstract theories; it's also about examples, explicit examples, and concrete computations, and we don't want to divorce ourselves from contact with these explicit examples and concrete computations. So, for example, most undergraduates don't really see a proper proof of this fundamental theorem of algebra, even though they use it in many, many, many places until perhaps in a third-year complex analysis course. So there's usually a place somewhere after you've done the real analysis where the proof is presented, but it's almost always not very convincing to your typical undergraduate. They go, "Okay, now I have some abstract argument which I probably don't really believe," but if there were really a convincing argument, then that convincing argument could be supported by examples, right? So we give the argument, and then we can say, "And here, by the way, guys, here are some examples of how it actually works." But unfortunately, the fundamental theorem of algebra falls down as soon as you start looking at examples. You don't have to go very far, and you see that, wait a minute, there is some divergence between the claims that are made and what actually happens in concrete situations. Let me illustrate. Here is an example—there's nothing particularly special about this example. P(x) = x⁵ - 2x + 3. That's a polynomial of degree 5; it has, in this case, integer entries, so it's particularly simple. We can graph it, or at least we can graph part of it. So it has some kind of shape like this for values, say, between -3 and 3. We can see that it crosses the x-axis seemingly somewhere around here between -1 and -2. It goes up here, has a little maximum there, then down to a minimum, and then it goes on together, okay? So what's the claim here? The claim is, first of all, that this thing has a zero. We can see that without using the fundamental theorem of algebra; we can see it from the picture, can't we? And we're going back now to Newton's method—Newton can see that there's a zero there, and to evaluate that zero, he can create this algorithm that will, well, not actually give you the zero but will give you a sequence of approximations that will give you points on the x-axis that are closer and closer to having the property that P(x) is zero. So we've talked about Newton's method, which is very important, at some length earlier in this series. So the Math Foundations 83 up to 86, my lectures, are all about that, and they give you a way of successively creating a sequence of values that are closer and closer to where this function ostensibly crosses the x-axis. So it certainly suggests—the picture suggests—that yes, there's some point, but in fact, the actual logical structure of that statement is not at all obvious. It's not at all obviously clear that we understand continuity well enough to really be able to assert that there actually is an exact value of x for which this polynomial takes on the value 0. Okay, but I can get my computer, I can set this equal to 0, and I can ask my computer to solve it, and it will do so at least up to a certain resolution that I can input beforehand. So if I ask it, say, for eight decimal places, something like that, then it will tell me that a zero or a solution to P(x) = 0 is, say, given by -1.4236058. That, according to my computer, is a solution to the equation P(x) = 0. In other words, that's a root or a zero of the polynomial. Great. But if I want to check and I actually take this exact value and plug it in here and get my computer to evaluate that, it does not give me zero. It evaluates it again to eight decimal places as 8.9999626 x 10⁻⁷. That's a relatively small quantity, but it's definitely not zero, all right? Then I can up the resolution, so I can go into my program and ask it to evaluate things not to eight decimal places but to maybe 16 decimal places or something like that. Okay, so I've done that, and I've asked it again to solve P(x) = 0, and it's come up with a better value. So here's the same kind of thing as we had before, but now we have some more digits. So is this the right answer? Well, it's a better answer; at least it's more accurate. If we plug this, however, into the polynomial and actually evaluate it, our computer will tell us that we get something that is about -4.8376541 x 10⁻²⁰. But it's not exactly what we get—there are more digits here, but this is to eight decimal places. That's what we get. In particular, we're not getting something that's zero. We're getting something that's getting small because that's 10⁻²⁰, so it's a very small quantity, but it's definitely not zero. And this is the reality of it, again—this is the reality that yes, you can get more and more digits, and so you're getting closer and closer to having a number that you can plug in here to get zero, but you cannot actually get such a number. You cannot get it. So this is why discussions of this in undergraduate texts are always kind of limited. People don't want to explore this very clearly and carefully because then undergraduates would start to go, "Well, wait a minute, you're claiming that there's a number that is a zero on this thing, but you can't actually exhibit such a number, so why should we believe the arguments? Maybe there's some problem here." Well, yes, there is a problem, okay? There's a very serious problem logically. Alright, so here's my claim—Wildberger's claim—but in direct contradiction to the fundamental theorem of algebra part one: there is, in fact, no number R in C satisfying P(R), which is R⁵ - 2R + 3 = 0. I claim there is no such number. This is in direct contradiction to the fundamental theorem of algebra. I do not dispute that there are complex numbers that you could plug in here, and you would get something that's close to zero. I do not dispute that at all; I'm happy to believe that. I'm happy to believe that you can get values here for which this thing is some very, very small complex number that's very, very close to zero. But problem: you cannot find a number R, a complex number R, such that P(R) = 0. So this is a very direct claim, isn't it? I mean, we're not talking about philosophy here—it's not a question of opinion. It's not something that someone can say, "Well, I have an axiomatic position that says such and such." I'm making a very direct claim about the existence of a solution to a polynomial equation, and I challenge any mathematician to prove me wrong. And you can prove me wrong by showing us a number R, a complex number R, that I can then plug in and check. So you can do whatever you like and use all kinds of sophisticated cohomological theories if you like, but at the end of it, you have to give me an R, and I'm going to take that R and plug it into P(R), and I'm going to see whether or not your R actually works. And I claim that it's not going to work. It's just not going to work. And so the fundamental theorem of algebra is incorrect. It is just not true. Well, there will be people who will say, "Well, wait a minute, Norman, I can exhibit such an R for you. What you do is you start doing this procedure, okay? You do this, and then you do this, and then you do this, and you keep on going to infinity, and at the end of infinity, you will have such an R." So they will give us a prescription for finding such an R that involves an infinite number of steps. Now, I'm not able to do an infinite number of steps, so I'm not happy to be given such a prescription. But if someone has such a philosophy towards finding such an R, then I suggest that they themselves carry out the infinite number of steps, and after they have done so, then they can exhibit the end result to us, alright? So I'm not interested in someone telling me a procedure that will find such an R; I'm interested in someone actually exhibiting such an R directly. That's not going to happen. That's why the fundamental theorem of algebra is wrong. And for a very similar reason, the second version of the fundamental theorem of algebra doesn't work either. So if we take our same polynomial and ask our computer for all of the complex zeros, it will give us some answers. So it will give us the number that we've already seen, and it will give us also four other ones, which are not visible on the graph that we drew, okay? They're not actually corresponding to places where the graph crosses the x-axis because we're talking about complex values. But nevertheless, here are these complex numbers that have a property that when you plug them in, you get something that's very close to being the complex number zero, like zero plus zero i. But notice that these things here have the same aspect as this one does. So I'm putting three dots here now to represent the fact that my computer actually has a range of possible things that it can give me depending on the resolution level that I've asked it for. It doesn't give me a unique answer; it depends on how many digits I have asked it to give me an answer for, so it's a little bit open-ended. But nevertheless, there's something in this direction—your computer will find something in this direction as well. So there's this one plus i, then the same values, really, minus i. Now, these are called complex conjugate pairs, and then there's another pair like this involving these two numbers, plus and minus, and here's a number, which are the coefficients of i. Okay, so in the same way, this is very explicit, really. If the fundamental theorem of algebra part two really did work, then we could write our polynomial as a product of linear factors, and the linear factors would correspond exactly to these five numbers. So we could form the expression (x - R₁)(x - R₂)(x - R₃)(x - R₄)(x - R₅), and you can do this. You can plug the values that you had in—that will necessarily mean making a choice. You know, your computer will give you a certain number of digits, and you will have to use that certain number of digits in order to create these linear factors. And then you can get your computer to evaluate that product, and you will see that you have a polynomial that is pretty close to this one, in the sense that the difference between these two polynomials is another polynomial with very small coefficients—you know, the coefficients are like 10⁻⁸ or 10⁻²⁰ or something like that. So these things are very close to being the same polynomial, but they're not actually the same polynomial. The fundamental theorem of algebra part two does not work either. Now, does this render all those applications of the fundamental theorem to physics and chemistry and industry and so on wrong? No, not at all, because the applied mathematician doesn't need the pure form of the fundamental theorem. They only need the approximate form. They only need to know that we can find complex numbers such that this thing is very close to this. They only need to find a zero in the sense that we have some number so that when we plug in, we get a number that is very close to zero. To an engineer, if something is zero to eight or twenty decimal places, then it's effectively zero for them. They don't care if theoretically it's not zero, right? So the fundamental theorem of algebra is always going to be an important and useful tool for applied mathematicians. The question is, how shall we pure mathematicians understand it? How shall we think about what's really going on? This is a huge challenge for modern mathematics and, in fact, a challenge that has very, very big ramifications for abstract algebra. So we'll talk more next time about how we can move forward on this challenge. What is the fundamental theorem of algebra really? What can we actually aspire to in terms of setting it up correctly? I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. [Music
