# 217 - What is the Fundamental theorem of Algebra, really？ ｜ Abstract Algebra Math Foundations 217 [qoSGtoy7DlI].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In this video, we would like to set out an alternative framework for the fundamental theorem of algebra—or actually the fundamental theorems of algebra that we talked about last time because there are actually a couple of them that are closely related. What I want to do is reposition these actual theorems to be much more algebraic, to not actually involve any analysis, at least in their statements, so that at least the statements of the theorems are completely algebraic and sit inside of algebraic reality. In particular, they should be logically provable. So let's go ahead and lay out precise algebraic formulations of the fundamental theorems of algebra. This was actually first suggested in my algebraic topology series quite some years ago, so it's really an enormous opportunity that we open up here, and I'm hoping that some viewers will take this up because it's an incredible opportunity for some young people—or maybe not-so-young people—to make a really serious contribution to mathematics. This particular theorem especially is of fundamental importance in mathematics; it really is a pillar of algebra, as the name suggests. It's crucially important, and so it's very, very, very important that we have a solid formulation and proof of it. Currently, we don't have that, so this is an enormous opportunity for some young people to become rich and famous on these grounds. Okay, so this is really among the foremost challenges in pure mathematics in the current era, and I think it's probably not much of an exaggeration to say that proving this result—really getting it on a firm basis—would be as important as any other single mathematical accomplishment in the 20th century. It really is at that level; it's a foundational-level kind of challenge—very, very important. So, maybe you can do it. Alright, so our challenge is to set up this framework in a purely algebraic way—so really relying on the basic operations of addition, multiplication, subtraction, and division. No transcendental processes, no infinite processes being completed, so none of that implicitly under the rug, surreptitiously. Everything squared away logically with a finite number of arithmetical steps. Okay, so the setup is that of rational complex numbers. These are not the usual complex numbers; these are actually the true complex numbers. So a rational complex number is one—it's an expression of the form a + bi where a and b are rational numbers. This is a rather quick way of introducing rational complex numbers. We will also, in a little while, show you some other ways of thinking about these things that are perhaps more intuitive or in the line of data structures, but for now, this is familiar enough, I think, to most people: a + bi, where a and b are rational numbers. That's the critical point. So these things can be added in the usual kind of way, and they can be multiplied in this particular way, which really amounts to the rule that i times i is -1. This bi and this di become -bd. This creates a field—we won't say precisely what that means—but a field with the name rat(C), or rational complex numbers, which contains the rational numbers in the special case when the coefficient of i is zero. In that case, we get something which is a rational number, so we can think about the rational complexes as being an extension of the rational numbers. While historically complex numbers were problematic, when it became apparent that there was a two-dimensional representation of them, then it was a little bit easier for people to visualize them and understand them. So this is sometimes called the Argand plane, where we take the usual xy-plane and identify the y-axis with the i-axis. So we have usual rational numbers here and multiples of i in that direction. That allows us to visualize a complex number like 3 + 2i as 3 in this direction and 2i in this direction. So it really corresponds to the point (3, 2) in sort of an affine sense. So we can visualize rational complex numbers as points in the familiar affine plane. And now here is the main measurement notion that we'll need. The quadrance of a rational complex number z, which is a + bi, is the rational number q(z) which is a² + b². So a² + b²—that's the quadrance between these two points in the usual two-dimensional Euclidean plane. Remarkably, this quantity doesn't really get officially defined in a complex analysis course, which is really quite astounding because it's an absolutely central and fundamental notion. Unfortunately, most treatments of complex numbers start by saying, "Let's immediately take the square root of this and call this the absolute value of the complex number." We do not want to do that, okay? We do not want to use absolute value in this context, which is the square root involved—really an infinite process. You have the square root of, in this case, for example, 9 + 4, 13—the square root of 13—that's not something that you can access in a standard way with only a finite number of arithmetical steps. The quadrance, which is just 13, is of course very easy and pleasant to work with. So that's a big adjustment that one ought to make when studying complex numbers. So think about quadrance rather than absolute value. And two very important and useful facts about quadrance: first of all, that the quadrance of a complex number z is 0 precisely when z = 0. So it goes both ways—if the quadrance is 0, then the complex number is 0, and it’s easily seen that if the complex number is 0, then its quadrance is 0. And this is probably the most important fact about complex numbers from an elementary point of view: the fact that the quadrance is multiplicative—that the quadrance of a product z times w with two rational complex numbers is the product of q(z) times q(w). Okay, so we're not going to go too far in this direction for now. The main thing is to understand that we can expand polynomial algebra or arithmetic from polynomials with only rational coefficients to polynomials involving complex coefficients, and we can replace the idea of plugging in rational values with the idea of plugging in rational complex values. So, for example, here in a polynomial P(z) = z⁵ - 2z + 3, if, for example, we substitute the complex number 1 - ½i, we can sub that in here—that would involve taking the fifth power of this thing, subtracting 2 times this, and adding 3. And one can check that the result of that is the complex number -3/16 - 9/32i. So, for example, if we were looking for a zero of this polynomial regarded in this complex setting, then this would not be a zero because the polynomial evaluated at it is not zero. But if we manage to find a such a complex number such that we do get zeros, then that would be a zero—a root—of the complex polynomial. Okay, so now these true fundamental theorems of algebra statements that we're interested in—they really are going to be purely algebraic. So we want statements that are purely algebraic, like they can be understood in principle by a primary school student. No calculus, no analysis, no complicated functions—we just know how to add, multiply, subtract, and divide, and they have learned a little bit about these complex numbers. Okay, so there are going to be several such theorems. In the same way as in the previous video, we had two different theorems—here, there will be two different theorems as well, and they have some increasing sophistication. And I want to reiterate this very important point: we have no proper proof of these statements. None of the many, many proofs that have been given are logically correct because they all implicitly, one way or the other, assume somewhere or another infinite processes. But one way or the other, if you look at all the proofs, they're all doing this, and we want to avoid that. We want to understand the situation so deeply that we can see the essential algebraic aspects of them. So this is an incredible opportunity for smart, careful, and precise thinkers. Now, 50 years from now, people will be jealous of you for having this opportunity to be able to prove such an important, crucially important theorem—an absolute foundational aspect for people—all of algebra. That’s waiting for us in the future, that’s to come. Maybe some of you can be involved. Very exciting! Alright, so here's the first true fundamental theorem of algebra, and it's necessarily a little bit more complicated to state than the usual one because it's more precise and pins things down more carefully. And so we pay a little bit of a price for being more accurate and staying in the algebraic world. So, what is it? For any non-constant polynomial P(z) = a₀ + a₁z + ... + aₙzⁿ, we'll assume that this aₙ is not equal to 0 with n greater than or equal to 1. So this non-constant means that the degree of this polynomial is at least 1, and these coefficients a₀, a₁ up to aₙ are all rational complex numbers. Given such a polynomial, and given a natural number K which is bigger than or equal to 1, the statement is that there is a w = a + bi, a rational complex number, with the property that the quadrance of P(w) is less than or equal to 1/K. So you're given a polynomial, and then someone says, "Let's choose K = 10" or whatever—there's some particular K chosen—and then your job is to find an approximate zero, not really a zero but an approximate zero. What does that mean? It means a complex number—a rational complex number, crucially—so that when you plug it into the polynomial, you get this other complex number P(w), and when you take the quadrance of that, it's small. The quadrance being small means that the complex number is close to zero. Well, close in the sense that its quadrance is at most 1/K. Okay, so let's have a look at an example because we like explicit, concrete examples. Here is a polynomial P(z) = z⁶ + 3z² + z + 1. In the ordinary, say, rational number domain, this is what the graph looks like. It has some kind of shape like this, and it's quite clear that it's not going to cross the x-axis, so it doesn't have a traditional zero. At times gone by, people would have said, "This function doesn't have a zero." But over the complex plane, we have a wider story, and we can get some approximate zeros at least. So let's see how that works. So the ingredients for the fundamental theorem of algebra part one as we've set out are a polynomial like this together with some resolution level K, say K = 30. So if we're given these two quantities, then we want to find a complex number w = a + bi, crucially a rational complex number, such that the quadrance of P(w) is less than or equal to, say, 1/30. So we want that when we plug this thing into here, we're getting a complex number which is in quite a close proximity to zero—within the quadrance of 1/30. Okay, so I have found such a zero for you, and here it is: it's -1/6 + 1/2i. If we take this complex number and plug it in here, we calculate the sixth power and so on, and make this calculation, then we get this complex number 127/729 - 13/648i. And if we call that thing v, then this complex number v has quadrance—well, it's that thing squared plus that thing squared, and that turns out to be this: 1045945/34012224. Let me kind of line them up here so you can kind of see it's basically like 1/34, and that's just a little bit less than 1/25. So if our K value was 25, then this would be a linear factorization of the polynomial which is approximate, but which is within the resolution of K = 25. I think it's quite interesting. I think the numbers are interesting. I think it's pleasant and also curious that the zeros that we're getting here are relatively simple fractions, right? They're not very big. This is a reasonably decent-sized polynomial, and it’s curious that we can get to this pretty good approximation because actually, the sum of all these five or six quadrances is for a total of less than 1/25. We're doing pretty well with relatively simple-minded factors, you might say. Which brings us to the interesting question: okay, so just how—how can we generalize this kind of thing? You know, given this polynomial, and we understand its coefficients, given a K value, how complicated do these zeros have to be? In other words, how big are the numerators and denominators typically going to be so that we get within the resolution required? This is an example of a different kind of question that arises when we give up on our abstract, theoretical, pie-in-the-sky kind of explanation of what's going on and return to a more concrete, computational, "let's see what actually happens" point of view. So we give up on the dreaming, we face the reality, but more interesting questions arrive. We see more; we get in more contact with real mathematics. So I'm hopeful that's what we'll do—finding proofs for these theorems, that those proofs will have a strong computational bias. I think it will involve having more insight than we currently have. So if you're aspiring to prove these things, the first thing you should do is have a good look at the classical proofs. There are a lot of great ideas in those classical proofs, okay? You will want to understand the basic ideas there—there are all these topological ideas—and then you want to wrestle with how you’re going to restate those topological arguments without making all these real-number assumptions about continuity, and how you can get it down to just algebra. So it’s a very major challenge. I think it really is probably useful to keep in mind this computational question. It's not just a question of proving these things, but really what we'd like to do is to have some understanding of how we find those zeros. Given a polynomial, given a K, how do we actually find an approximate zero of that degree of accuracy? What do we have to do? Can we actually give a computational proof of these theorems? That really matters. And I also want to mention that there’s another challenge lurking in the background. After you start thinking about this for a little while, you'll realize that the first two theorems that I've given you are not the end of the story. There are naturally some further extensions that you want to enunciate and then pin down. But nevertheless, it's an opportunity for a lot of invigorated discussion and development in the subject, I think. And I want to have a look at a very specific example coming from roots of unity and so-called cyclotomic fields. I'll be able to do that without a lot of background, and that will give you a good understanding of why we really need to think about algebra in a majorly different way now that we sort of understand these difficulties with the fundamental theorem of algebra. So I hope you'll join me for that. I'm Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. [Music 

---

# 218 - Why roots of unity need to be rethought ｜ Abstract Algebra Math Foundations 218 ｜ NJ Wildberger [Snq7UJT8EWg].en-en

Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Good day, I'm Norman Wildberger. In today's video, we want to look at roots of unity, which are ostensibly complex numbers that are equally spaced around the unit circle and obey a seemingly remarkable algebraic property. These roots of unity are mainstays in field theory and are related to cyclotomic fields, which play an important role in abstract algebra. This topic serves as a good example of a very explicit situation where we can see that we need to separate two concepts that are currently fused together. We must approach this in a more organized way. The context is the unit circle viewed inside the complex numbers. Here’s the number 1, and here is the number i. The unit circle is given by the equation x² + y² = 1 or, in complex analysis terms, the points z, or complex numbers z, whose quadrance Q(z) = 1. I remind you that the quadrance of a complex number a + bi is defined as a² + b². The absolute value of a complex number doesn’t really play any role in algebra. Now, if we want to approach the unit circle from an algebraic point of view, we probably know what to do. What we need is a rational parameterization, which is a way of describing points on the circle purely algebraically. Here it is in terms of complex numbers: 𝑒 ( 𝑡 ) = 1 − 𝑡 2 1 + 𝑡 2 + 2 𝑡 1 + 𝑡 2 × 𝑖 e(t)= 1+t 2 1−t 2 ​ + 1+t 2 2t ​ ×i. We are rewriting something that I hope is quite familiar to you because I’ve talked about it quite a lot—the importance of being able to describe points on the unit circle algebraically, with this as the x-coordinate and this as the y-coordinate. So, we’re rewriting that now in terms of a complex number. This is an algebraic approach, and it’s far superior to the cosine(theta)-sine(theta) picture for most applications—not all, but for most, especially geometrical and algebraic ones. From the fundamental relation that Q(z * w) = Q(z) * Q(w), which is a basic aspect of quadrance for complex numbers, we can deduce that the product of two unit complex numbers is going to be another unit complex number. So, if we have two points on the unit circle and we think of them as complex numbers and we take their product, z * w, then the new number that we get also has a quadrance of 1, so it will also lie on the circle. This brings us to a very natural and interesting question: if our first number is 𝑒 ( 𝑡 ) e(t) (that's the parameter) and the second number is 𝑒 ( 𝑟 ) e(r), then, first of all, geometrically, where should the product be? And another kind of question is, how do we describe this position algebraically as well as geometrically? Where is 𝑒 ( 𝑡 ) × 𝑒 ( 𝑟 ) e(t)×e(r), both geometrically and algebraically? The beauty of working algebraically is that we can be precise and specific. We don’t have to deal at the level of abstractions; we can get right down to concrete examples. So let’s do that here. Let’s have a look at two particular complex numbers on the unit circle. Let’s say 𝑒 ( 1 / 3 ) e(1/3). You can calculate that 𝑒 ( 1 / 3 ) e(1/3) is 4 / 5 + 3 / 5 × 𝑖 4/5+3/5×i, which we’ll call z. Geometrically, that means if we go up here in the y-direction to the point with the coefficient 1/3 (what we’re calling 𝑖 / 3 i/3) and we join these two points here, then where that line meets the unit circle is the point 4 / 5 + 3 / 5 × 𝑖 4/5+3/5×i. The other one we’ll take is 𝑒 ( 2 / 3 ) e(2/3). So we take this point 2 / 3 × 𝑖 2/3×i on the y-axis, draw the line, and see where it intersects the unit circle. We get the point 5 / 13 + 12 / 13 × 𝑖 5/13+12/13×i, which we’ll call w. Alright, so we have z and w, and we can take these two complex numbers and multiply them algebraically because we know how to multiply complex numbers. I’ll leave it to you to check that what you get, the product of these two things, is − 16 / 65 + 63 / 65 × 𝑖 −16/65+63/65×i. And you can further check that it’s actually 𝑒 ( 9 / 7 ) e(9/7). That means if we take the point 9 / 7 × 𝑖 9/7×i up here and draw a line from here to that point, it will meet the circle at exactly the point z * w. A natural question is, how do we get 9/7 from 1/3 and 2/3 if we wanted to do it directly? The answer lies in this beautiful formula right here: 9 / 7 9/7 happens to be the sum of these two parameter values divided by 1 − 1− the product of those two parameter values. So if this was r and s, then the formula here would be 𝑟 + 𝑠 r+s on top, divided by 1 − 𝑟 × 𝑠 1−r×s. I’ll leave this as an exercise for you to verify. You might want to look at some previous videos in this series for some hints. Geometrically, it turns out that z * w, the line joining z and w, is parallel to the line from 1 to z * w. This is a remarkable additional feature of this situation. So, if we took, say, this line right here (the line joining z and w), and then we also took the line joining 1 and z * w, those two lines are parallel. That’s a purely geometrical way of describing the product of points on the unit circle. Now, if you’re interested in this, I talked about it in my algebraic topology series quite some time ago—so I think it’s in Algebraic Topology, maybe number 3 or something like that. You can have a look. I talked about how this is a special case of a much more general phenomenon that works for more general conics. It’s quite interesting. So, this is a purely geometrical way, and here’s the algebraic solution: we know what the parameters for two complex numbers are, and here’s the parameter for their product. And purely geometrically, if we know two points on the unit circle, we can draw the line between them and then draw the line that’s through the point 1, parallel to that given line, and we get the product. So, two ways of describing the product of points on the unit circle in the complex plane—purely algebraically and purely geometrically—and it’s a great exercise for you to state these precisely and prove them. You might be surprised to find out that your typical undergraduate does not know the contents of what I just said on that previous slide. That’s quite surprising to most of them. They think, “Wow, why hasn’t somebody told us this before?” They’ve all been told a different story, and it’s true around the world—doesn’t matter where you go, they’re all told an alternate story, which is actually much weaker. And at UNSW, we do this as well, unfortunately. But of course, as the 21st century rolls on, things are going to be changing. But currently, we do the following old-fashioned thing: we say that it all has to do with angles. When you’re multiplying complex numbers on the unit circle, it’s a question about adding angles. This is what students actually learn. It’s very unfortunate. They learn that if you take the angle formed by the x-axis and z, and the angle formed by the x-axis and w, those are called the argument of z and the argument of w (those angles). Then the product z * w has an argument or an angle that is the sum of those two angles. So, the argument of z * w equals the argument of z plus the argument of w. And that’s how you describe the multiplication of complex numbers geometrically, using angles. This is applied mathematics, not really pure mathematics. The reason is that the angles are, in fact, impossible to define properly and to compute approximately. We can define them and deal with them, but defining them precisely and computing them precisely requires an infinite amount of work. So, we cross this border—this Rubicon—between the world in which we live, where you can actually make calculations, and this imaginary, fictitious world where you’re allowed to do an infinite number of things and obtain outputs. And it’s this land across this imaginary river out there that we’re constantly appealing to in modern mathematics, but which is actually illusory—it’s a dream. But that’s ultimately the dream that’s responsible for this idea of angles. Because angles are so difficult to get your hands on precisely, what happens in undergraduate mathematics around the world is that we all have this tacit agreement that we’re going to stick to two baby triangles: the 1-1-√2 triangle and the 1-2-√3 triangle—the triangle with angles 45 degrees, 45 degrees, and 60 degrees, and 30 degrees. Those are basically the only two triangles that we’re going to use. So by positioning them in different ways, we can create a bunch of different questions for undergraduates. And invariably—it’s remarkable—around the world, whenever students are asked about specific questions involving complex number calculations and involving angles, invariably 60 degrees, 45 degrees, or 30 degrees figure—or some rotation of them in some of the other quadrants. It’s actually a very limiting situation. Let’s look at an example that’s a little bit outside that familiar trodden territory. Let’s have a look at the same numbers that we were looking at: say, 𝑤 = 5 13 + 12 13 × 𝑖 w= 13 5 ​ + 13 12 ​ ×i. And ask ourselves, okay, that’s a very concrete number on the unit circle associated with the lovely Pythagorean triple 5-12-13, right? It’s really just a rescaling of that Pythagorean triple. Let’s ask, what is the angle associated with that? What is the theta, the argument of 5 / 13 + 12 / 13 × 𝑖 5/13+12/13×i? Well, that’s a standard computation that most undergraduates know how to do at some level. They know that you have to do a certain number of things. So, the first thing that probably an undergraduate would think is, “Well, we need an arctan function here.” So, the usual formula is that theta equals tan inverse of y/x. So you take your y-coordinate and your x-coordinate, which in this case are 12/13 and 5/13, and you take the ratio and you take tan inverse of 12/5. But what is tan inverse, or arctan? It’s the same thing. Well, that’s given by an infinite formula, which most undergraduates, after they’ve done a course in calculus, should hopefully know. So, it’s z - z³/3 + z⁵/5 - z⁷/7, and so on. The pattern is pretty clear: there are just odd numbers in the denominators there, and the powers in z are going up by 2 through all the odd possibilities. Okay, so that’s some kind of infinite expression. That means that if we want to calculate tan inverse of 12/5, we should just replace z with 12/5 to get 12/5 - 1/3 of (12/5)³ + 1/5 of (12/5)⁵ - 1/7 of (12/5)⁷, and so on. But if the undergraduate stares at this for a second, they will realize, “Wait a minute, something’s not quite right,” because 12/5 is bigger than 2, and so these powers—these are powers of 2—they’re getting big. We are dividing by something that’s not getting as big as fast, so this thing is going to blow up. It’s not going to converge and get smaller and smaller as we would like. And in fact, if the undergraduate thinks back, they’ll think, “Okay, actually, this formula comes with a certain requirement that really only works when z has a modulus less than 1, or quadrance less than 1—same thing.” So that means that we actually need to try something else. We need to twist this a little bit to get at this angle theta. So, how do we find theta? Well, a clever student might look at it this way. They might say, “Okay, this theta here was a bit problematic because the y was bigger than the x, but if we take this sort of angle here, which is π/2 - theta, then that’s a little bit different because then the x and the y are interchanged.” So, if we look at π/2 - theta, that’s going to be tan inverse of now x/y. And the x/y is 5/12. Now we’re in a happier position with respect to this infinite series defining tan inverse of x/y. So we now could take 5/12 - 1/3 of (5/12)³ + 1/5 of (5/12)⁵ - 1/7 of (5/12)⁷, and so on. Now, I want you to consider this right here. I know the average undergraduate learns that you’re not supposed to flinch when you see something like this, okay? But logically speaking, there is something seriously problematic around here, because we’re being asked to do an infinite number of arithmetic operations here, and this is not even defined. We have not defined what we mean by doing an infinite number of arithmetic operations, okay? So, it’s not a question of whether the thing exists or not; it’s just that the actual definition of arithmetic that we have always involves adding a finite number of things. We have not defined what it means to add an infinite number of things. That’s why you have to speak of all the problems: what happens to these numbers actually? What happens when we get to the z power? Okay, we’re taking (5/12)^z. Well, what does that mean? That’s way beyond the universe in terms of writing it down in any reasonable fashion. And in what sense does it make any sense to add that particular term to what we’ve already got? It’s clear that this is blowing out on us computationally long before we get to infinity. Long before we get to infinity, this has just exploded in our faces, and we just have egg all over us. It’s only because we actually do not make the computations that we’re allowed to not address the nonsense of it, okay? In fact, that’s what happens. Our machines, of course, know enough not to try to add up all of these infinite number of things. The machine knows that, well, we’re just going to truncate at some point and then give the human whatever we have. So that’s what your machine will do if you ask it, well, to evaluate tan inverse of 5/12. It will give you something like this: 0.39479119699976, and at that point, it will stop. It will not actually exhibit three dots, usually. Okay, so the undergraduate takes this, which is the answer to this infinite computation. In fact, it’s not, okay, but let’s pretend it is. And then we solve for theta. So, theta is going to be π/2 - tan inverse of 5/12. So now we have to procure π/2. Well, π is known to about five or ten trillion places these days; that’s the extent of it. But ostensibly, we’re taking this π, dividing it by 2, and taking this infinite decimal that we’ve already computed and taking the difference. So there’s another infinite amount of work that we’re ostensibly doing. Okay, you can see this is all just pretending, right? We’re just pretending here. This is how we do modern mathematics when we come to examples. We are thinking about these infinite things; that’s theoretically what we’re doing. But practically, we’re actually doing something quite different. We’re just always working at the level of floating-point and truncating things and pretending that the floating-point truncations are true reflections of these infinite fantasies that we like to dream about. That’s what it is, ladies and gentlemen—that’s what’s actually happening. So the undergraduate comes up with something like this: so the answer is the angle is 1.176005207095, and so on. So you can see why my claim that this is actually the wrong way of thinking about things, right? The previous way of thinking about things, in terms of describing points on the unit circle, involved the rational parameterization. No infinite processes were required, okay? Very concrete. The seeming advantage of being able to add angles is far overcompensated by the difficulty in procuring the angles in the first place. It’s a false economy to think that we’ve saved ourselves some time or effort because we only need to add the angles. In fact, if we actually did that correctly, there’d be an infinite amount of work. And adding angles is also completely beyond us, really. So, it’s very important to realize that the angle story is an analytic story, which is just an approximation to things, and logically speaking, it’s not in algebra and doesn’t really make sense, except in an applied, truncated kind of fashion. That’s the reality of where things are. Alright, let’s now turn to this roots of unity question, which deals with a very simple, seemingly simple, kind of equation—something like z⁷ = 1. This is a particular example of roots of unity, where the n is 7, so we end up getting the seventh roots of unity, supposedly. Almost all modern mathematicians these days believe that this equation has seven solutions in the complex plane and that they’re equally spaced around the unit circle—a seventh of a turn between any two of them. The usual names or symbols for these so-called roots of unity are the following: this one, which is the obvious solution, and then there’s 𝑒 𝑖 2 𝜋 / 7 e i2π/7 , the 2π/7 referring to the fact that we’re taking the full turn all the way around, which is an angle of 2π, and dividing by 7. And then 𝑒 𝑖 4 𝜋 / 7 e i4π/7 , 𝑒 𝑖 6 𝜋 / 7 e i6π/7 , and then these other ones here are the negatives of these: 𝑒 − 𝑖 2 𝜋 / 7 e −i2π/7 , 𝑒 − 𝑖 4 𝜋 / 7 e −i4π/7 , 𝑒 − 𝑖 6 𝜋 / 7 e −i6π/7 . So your typical modern mathematician is absolutely convinced—no question whatsoever about it—that there are exactly seven complex numbers, and here they are, that are exactly on the unit circle, equally separated, that are the solutions to this simple equation, z⁷ = 1. However, if you actually try to draw a heptagon, then maybe some doubt starts to creep into your mind. And if you appreciate that the ancients, like the Greeks, were quite flabbergasted by this problem—how do you actually construct a beautiful, regular heptagon? If you do it by hand like I’ve done, you can probably even see just by looking at it that this is probably not exactly a heptagon. It’s actually quite surprisingly hard to draw a heptagon accurately. In fact, it’s impossible to draw it completely accurately, even in principle. There’s no technology known to mankind that will in principle draw a perfect heptagon, a regular heptagon. This is an interesting thing, okay? And we can see this by thinking about how a computer would draw it. I mean, if you ask your computer, your computer will draw a very nice-looking one, but it’s not actually exactly accurate. So your computer actually has to calculate where these points are. It has to convert the sort of symbolic 𝑒 𝑖 2 𝜋 / 7 e i2π/7 to something that’s concrete in terms of x-coordinate and y-coordinate. And how does it do that? So 𝑒 𝑖 2 𝜋 / 7 e i2π/7 is a kind of short form for cos(2π/7) + i sin(2π/7). Okay, so we haven’t talked very much about these transcendental functions, and they’re really fraught with difficulties—all of this should actually be in quotes, okay? But just to give you an overview, the idea is that what you have to do in order to find out what this complex number really is, is you have to evaluate these two things. So, cos(2π/7)—well, that’s again the same kind of difficult story as the tan inverse of 5/12. There’s a certain series, a certain infinite series, that was well-known, goes back a long time, probably even to Hindu mathematicians, and it’s 1 - 1/2! (2π/7)² + 1/4! (2π/7)⁴ - 1/6! (2π/7)⁶, and so on. So here we have factorials now, so 4! (for example) means 1 * 2 * 3 * 4. So what’s involved here is again an infinite number of arithmetic operations with increasingly more and more complicated things, and it’s quite clear that you can’t ever really do this. In fact, even the definition of this infinite sum is actually problematic. And then there’s a corresponding thing for sin(2π/7) or a similar kind of series for the imaginary part. But our computers don’t fuss around about this because our computers just truncate at some convenient point and spew out some decimals, which we take. We take the decimals that our computer gives, and then we add three dots, okay? And these three dots represent, in our minds, all of that extra infinite amount of work that we could do, or perhaps should do, but we’re not going to do it. And we can kind of console ourselves that an engineer is not concerned about these three dots. So the engineer comes along and says, “Okay, that’s eight decimal places. That’s far more than I need to be able to put this on the page very, very cleanly and clearly. That’s all the precision I need. I don’t care anything about these three dots here.” But the reality is that an exact understanding of this z is beyond us, okay? The point is that we cannot actually get a hold of this thing. In fact, this thing does not even exist, properly speaking. The right orientation is that there is no such thing, okay? We’re talking about something that doesn’t exist as if it did exist. And similarly, the fundamental theorem, which tells us that z⁷ - 1 ought to be the product of all these linear factors corresponding to all these different seventh roots of unity (by the way, which can be expressed as this is z, and this would be z², z³, z⁴, z⁵, z⁶), so there’s a pleasant kind of algebraic way of writing them down like this—this fundamental theorem also doesn’t really work. So if you plug something like this in here and you try to equate the right-hand side to the left-hand side, you will see that it doesn’t work, okay? So it’s a bit of a dream. It’s an approximation to something, okay? But it’s actually algebraically not hanging together. So, the existence of roots of unity is really something that we need to rethink, and that has a lot of implications for algebra. What makes this topic a little bit difficult to get your head around is that there are, in fact, algebraic ways of solving something like z⁷ = 1. It’s not that this equation is beyond us, and I’m going to be showing you ways of very clearly setting up a framework in which we have seven solutions of that. So there’s a bona fide algebraic way of dealing with this equation. But the point is that it’s separate, okay? This algebraic approach to, say, solving something like z⁷ = 1 should be kept separate from the analytic approach or an analytic approximate approach of solving z⁷ = 1 involving seven numbers on the unit circle, which are equally spaced. So, currently, in many algebraic minds—probably not all, but in many of them—these two things are so intimately connected, and they’re just part of the same story. What I want you to start thinking about is that we have to separate these things. We’re going to have to push them apart; we have to make some space. Over here is the algebraic approach, purely finite, purely algebraic, completely solid. Over here is the analytic approach, where we’re working within the complex numbers, but almost everything is approximate. We have some nice geometry, but the geometry is only approximate. And these two pictures are really different kinds of pictures. There is some kind of connection between them, and it’s interesting to explore what the connection is, but they’re logically separate, okay? So, we are going to be trying to turn our gaze away from this complex number picture that rests on the fundamental theorem of algebra towards this more algebraic approach to solving polynomial equations. In our next video, we are actually going to start with some abstract algebra. We’re going to look at probably the simplest kinds of things that we can do, which are not groups and not fields—they are something in the direction of linear spaces. So, we’re going to concentrate on linear spaces to begin with. That’s a great starting point for building up abstract algebra. I hope you’ll join me for that. I’m Norman Wildberger. Thanks for listening. [Music 

---
